QUESTION  PRESENTED

Petitioner  Gail  Atwater  was  placed  under  custodial
arrest  for not  wearing  a seat  belt,  a misdemeanor  that  carries
a  fifty-dollar  fine  as  its maximum  penalty.  Does  the  Fourth
Amendment  limit  the  use  of custodial  arrests  for  fine-only
traffic  offenses?

OPINIONS  BELOW

The  decision  of the  Fifth  Circuit  Court  of Appeals,
sitting  en  banc,  is reported  at Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,
195  F.3d  242  (Sth Cir.  1999)  (en banc),  and  is reproduced  in
Appendix A to  this  Petition.

The  decision  of  the  three-judge  panel  of  the  Fifth
Circuit  Court  of  Appeals  is  reported  at  Atwater  v.  City  of
Lago  Vista,  165  F.3d  380  (Sth Cir.  1999),  and  is reproduced
in Appendix B to  this  Petition.

The  Order  of the  United  States  Court  for  the  Western
District  of Texas,  Austin  Division,  was  not  published,  but  is
reproduced  in Appendix C to  this  Petition.

JURISDICTION

The  judgment  of the  Fifth  Circuit  Court  of Appeals
was  entered  on  November  24,  1999.  The jurisdiction  of this
Court  is invoked  under  28  U.S.C.  § 1254(1).

CONSTITUTIONAL  AND  STATUTORY  PROVISIONS

The  Fourth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution  provides:

The  right  of the  people  to  be  secure  in  their
persons,  houses,  papers,  and  effects,  against
unreasonable  searches  and  seizures,  shall  not
be  violated,  and  no  Warrants  shall  issue,  but
upon  probable  cause,  supported  by  Oath  or
affirmation,  and  particularly  describing  the
place to be searched,  and  the persons  or  things
to  be seized.

Texas  Transportation  Code  Ann.  § 545.413,  provides,  in
relevant  part:

(a)

A person  commits  an  offense  if the  person:

(1)

is at  least  15 years  of age;

(2)

(3)

is riding  in the front  seat  of a passenger
car  while  the vehicle  is being operated;

is  occupying  a  seat  that  is  equipped
with  a  safety  belt;  and

(4)

is not  secured  by a safety  belt.

(b)

A person  commits  an  offense  if the  person:

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE

This  case  presents  the question  of whether  the  Fourth
Amendment  limits  the  use  of a full  custodial  arrest  for a fine-
only  misdemeanor.  The  Court  appeared  ready  to  resolve  the
issue  in  1998  when  it granted  certiorari  on  essentially  the
same  question.  See Ricci  v.  Village of Arlington  Heights,  522
U.S.  1038  (1998)  (No.  97-501)  (granting  cert.  to  116  F.3d
288  (7th Cir.  1997)).  Unfortunately,  the Court  subsequently
dismissed  certiorari  after  it  became  apparent  during  oral
argument  that  the  facts  of the  case  did  not  actually  raise  the
question  presented.  See Ricci  v.  Village of Arlington  Heights,
523  U.S.  613  (1998)  (dismissing  cert.  as  improvidently
granted).  This case  presents  the Fourth  Amendment  issue  that
Ricci  did  not.

Operates
equipped  with  safety  belts;  and

a  passenger

car

that

is

I.  The  Facts.

(1)

(2)

allows  a child  who  is at least  four  years
of age but younger  than  15 years  of age
to  ride  in the  front  seat  of the  vehicle
without  requiring  the  child  to  be
secured  by a safety  belt.

(c)

(d)

A passenger  car  or  a seat  in a passenger  car  is
considered  to  be equipped  with  a safety  belt  if
the  vehicle  is required  under  Section  547.601
to  be equipped  with  safety  belts.

An offense  under  this section  is  a misdemeanor
punishable  by  a  fine  of not  less  than  $25  or
more  than  $50.

x  %

The  facts  of this case  are  straight-forward.  Following
a  traffic  stop,  a  Lago  Vista  police  officer  placed  Petitioner
Gail  Atwater  under  full  custodial  arrest  for  violating  a Texas
statute  that  requires  use  of seat  belts.

On March  26,  1997,  Gail  Atwater  was  driving  her two
children  home  from  soccer  practice.
She  was  driving  the
family’s  pickup  truck  at approximately  fifteen  miles  per hour
on  a residential  street  near  her  home.  Her  four-year-old  son
was  seated  in the middle  of the  front  seat  and  her six-year-old
None  were
daughter  was  seated  on  the  passenger  side.
wearing  seat  belts.

Bart  Turek,  a Lago  Vista  police officer,  observed  the
violation  and  stopped  Ms.  Atwater.  While  Ms.  Atwater  and
her children  remained  in the truck,  Officer  Turek  approached
and  screamed  that  they  had  met  before  or  that  they  had  had
this  conversation  before.  This  frightened  the  children,  who
began  to  cry,  so  Ms.  Atwater  calmly  and  quietly  requested
that  Officer  Turek  lower  his  voice.  He  responded  by telling
her  that  she  was  going  to jail.

Without  provocation,  Officer Turek continued  to speak
to  Ms.  Atwater  in a verbally  abusive  manner,  accusing  her of
not  caring  for  her  children.
Officer  Turek  claimed  he  had
recently  stopped  Ms.  Atwater  for  not  having  her  children  in
seat  belts,  but  this  was  false.  He  had  stopped  her  several
months  before  while  with  her  son,  but  the boy’s  seat  belt  had
been  securely  fastened.
No  citation  had  been  issued,  and
Officer  Turek  had  not  told  Ms.  Atwater  his basis  for stopping
her.

Police  officers  in Texas,  as  in other  states,  may  issue
tickets  after  stopping  motorists  for  traffic  violations.
Tex.
Transp.  Code  Ann.  §§  543.003-.005  (West  1999).  Rather
than  doing  this,  Officer  Turek  placed  Ms.  Atwater  under  full
custodial  arrest.'  He  handcuffed  her  with  her  hands  behind

Officer

Turek’s

'  Officer  Turek  later  claimed  that  he had  also  arrested  Ms.
Atwater  for  not  carrying  a driver’s  license  and  proof of insurance.
(She  had  neither  with  her  because  her  purse  recently  had  been
stolen.)
claim
his
pronouncement,  before  requesting  her license  or  insurance,  that he
was  taking  her to jail.  It is also  disingenuous  because  Ms.  Atwater
provided  him  with  her driver’s  license  number,  and  Officer  Turek
had  seen  both  her  license  and  proof  of  insurance  during  the
previous  stop.
Be  that  as  it  may,  even  if  Officer  Turek’s
after-the-fact  justification  is  accepted,  the  penalties  for  these

is  inconsistent

with

her  back,  loaded  her  into  his  squad  car,  and  took  her  to  the
police station.  At the police station,  she was  forced  to remove
her  shoes,  jewelry  and  glasses,  and  empty  her  pockets.  Ms.
Atwater’s  “mug shot”  was  taken,  and  she was  placed  in a jail
cell  for  approximately  one  hour  until  being  taken  before  a
magistrate.

Gail  Atwater  pled  no  contest  to  the  seat  belt  offense
and  paid  the  maximum  penalty  for  this  violation  —  a fifty-
dollar  fine.  The  charges  of driving  without  a  license  and
proof of insurance  were  dismissed.

wren.
pipe
ee een

II.  The  Proceedings  Below.

Petitioners  Gail  Atwater  and  her  husband  Michael
Haas,  as  next  friend  for their  children,  filed  suit  in state  court
against the City of Lago Vista,  Police  Officer  Bart  Turek,  and
Chief  of  Police  Frank  Miller  (collectively  “the  City”  or
“respondents”)  for  damages  suffered  by  petitioners  arising
from  Ms.  Atwater’s  incarceration.

The City removed  the suit to the United  States  District
Court  for  the  Western  District  of  Texas,  which  granted
summary  judgment  for the City.  The district  court  ruled  that
petitioners  had  not  identified  a  constitutional  right  that  had
been  violated  by the custodial  arrest.

offenses  are  also  minor  fines.  See  Tex.  Transp.  Code  Ann.  §§
521.025(c),  601.191(b)  (West  1999).  Furthermore,  it is  a defense
to  prosecution  if the  person  charged  thereafter  produces a valid
license  and proof of insurance  (as Officer  Turek  well  knew  that  Ms.
Atwater  would  be  able  to  do).  See  Tex.  Transp.  Code  Ann.  §§
521.025(d),  601.193  (West  1999).  Officer  Turek’s  contention  has
no  bearing  on  the  Fourth  Amendment  issue.

A  three-judge  panel  of  the  United  States  Court  of
Appeals  for the  Fifth  Circuit  reversed  the summary  judgment
with  respect  to  the  Fourth  Amendment  claim,  holding
petitioners  had  established  that  the  custodial  arrest  for  not
wearing  a  seat  belt  violated  a  clearly  established  Fourth
The  remainder  of  the  district  court’s
Amendment  right.
judgment  was  affirmed.

The  Fifth  Circuit  granted  rehearing  en  banc  and
vacated  the panel’s  decision.  After  oral  argument,  a majority
affirmed  the district  court’s  judgment  in all respects,  holding
that  the  custodial  arrest  did  not  violate  Ms.  Atwater’s  Fourth
Amendment  rights.  Six judges  dissented.

REASONS  FOR  GRANTING  CERTIORARI

Twenty-six  years  ago,  Justice  Potter  Stewart  observed
in  Gustafson  v.  Florida  that  “[iJt  seems  to  me  that  a
persuasive  claim  might  have  been  made  in this  case  that  the
custodial  arrest  of the  petitioner  for  a  minor  traffic  offense
violated
his  rights  under  the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth
Amendments.  But  no  such  claim  has  been  made.”  Gustafson
v.  Florida,  414  U.S.  260,  266-67  (1973)  (Stewart,  J.,
concurring).  The  motorist  had conceded  the constitutionality
of  his  arrest,  and  instead  challenged  only  the  search  that
followed.

Just  over  two  years  ago,  this  Court  granted  certiorari
to consider  whether  an  individual  could  be arrested  without  a
warrant  for a non-breach-of-the-peace  misdemeanor.  Ricci  v.
Village  of Arlington  Heights,  522  U.S.  1038  (1998)  (No.  97-
501)  (granting  cert.  to  116  F.3d  288  (7th  Cir.  1997)).
However,  after  full  briefing  on  the merits  and  oral  argument,
the  Court  learned  the  arrest  was  for  the  violation  of a  civil
ordinance,  not  a  misdemeanor,  and  certiorari  was  dismissed

as  improvidently  granted.
Heights,
improvidently  granted).

Today,  the  instant  case  squarely  presents  the  Fourth
Amendment  issue  in  the  factual  setting  in  which  it  most
Like  Ms.
frequently  arises:  the  ordinary  traffic  stop.
Atwater,  approximately  185  million  Americans  as  of  1998
were  licensed  drivers.
At  some  point  in  their  driving
careers,  virtually  every  one  of them  will  violate  some  traffic
law,  making  the  impact  of the  question  presented  extremely
wide-ranging.  The  facts  of this case  perfectly  frame  the  issue
for  the  Court’s  consideration.

Recognition  of  the  importance  of  the  question
presented  here  is widespread,  and  was  most  notably  shown  by
the  Court's  decision  to  grant  certiorari  in Ricci  v.  Village  of
Arlington  Heights.  The  importance  has  been  heightened  by
divergent  results  reached  by lower  courts  and  the  incorrect
result  reached  by the  court  below.

A.  The  Importance  of this  Issue  Has  Been  Widely

by this Court.
Recognized—Including

There  is  widespread  recognition  that  the  question
presented  here  is  an  important  issue  of  national  law.
Disparate  entities  and  groups  na ve made  this clear:  this Court
in Ricci  and  individual  members  of the  Court  in other  cases;
the  Fifth  Circuit;  respondents  in this  case;  numerous  public
interest  groups;  and  respected  legal  commentators.  Finally,
the  issue  is particularly  important  to  the  average  American
citizen.

The  issue’s  importance  is  most  apparent  from  the
Court's  granting of certiorari  on essentially  the same  issue just
two  years  ago.  The Court  granted  certiorari  to consider  what
limits  the  Fourth  Amendment  places  on  custodial  arrests  for
non-breach-of-the-peace  misdemeanors.  Ricci  v.  Village  of
Arlington  Heights,  522  U.S.  1038  (1998)  (No.  97-501)
The
(granting  cert.  to  116  F.3d  288  (7th  Cir.  1997)).
question  remains  unanswered,  however,  because  after  full
briefing  on  the merits  and oral  argument,  the Court dismissed
certiorari  as  improvidently  granted.  523  U.S.  613  (1998).°

>  In Ricci,  the  petitioner,  who  had  been  arrested  at  his
place  of business  for operating  without  a business  license,  argued
unsuccessfully  to the Seventh  Circuit  that “the common  law allowed
arrest  for  a  misdemeanor  committed  in an  officer’s  presence  only
if that  crime  constituted  a breach  of the peace.”  Ricci  v.  Village of
Arlington  Heights,  116  F.3d  288,  289,  291  (7th  Cir.  1997),  cert.
granted,  522  U.S.  1038,  cert.  dism’d,  523  U.S.  613  (1998).  The
Court  granted certiorari  to consider  the issue,  but unfortunately , the
question  presented  had  no  relation  to  Ricci’s  case:  the  offense  for
which  he  had  been  arrested  was  not  a  misdemeanor,  but  a  non-
criminal  civil  infraction.  See  Transcript  of Oral  Argument,  Ricci
v.  Village  of Arlington  Heights,  No.  97-501,  at  14,  33,  35-37,  59-

arrest

Individual  members  of this Court also have recognized
the importance  of the question  presented  in other cases.  See,
e.g.,  Gustafson  v.  Florida,  414  U.S.  260,  266-67  (1973)
(Stewart,  J.,  concurring);  Robbins  v.  California,  453  U.S.
420,  450  n.11  (1981)  (Stevens,  J., dissenting);  cf. Maryland
v.  Macon,  472  U.S.  463,  471  (1985)  (“We  leave  to  another
day the question  whether  the  Fourth  Amendment  prohibits  a
warrantless
for  the  state  law  misdemeanor  of
distribution  of obscene  materials.”).  Furthermore,  just a few
months  after  the dismissal  of certiorari  in Ricci,  the  issue  re-
emerged  during  the oral  argument  for Knowles  v.  lowa,  525
U.S.  113  (1998),  when  one  Justice  asked:  “I hadn’t  realized
that  you  had  raised  the question  that  Justice  Stewart  left over
in  the  Gustafson  case,  which  was  whether  you  could  have

Recognition  of the  issue’s  importance  extends  beyond
this Court.  The  Fifth  Circuit  recognized  its importance  when
it granted  rehearing  en  banc  in this  case,  which  it does  only
for  issues  “of  exceptional  public  importance.”

represented  that  the case  presented  “questions  of exceptional
importance”  when  they  sought  the  en  banc  rehearing.
Appellees’  Suggestion  for  Rehearing  En Banc  at  iii, Atwater

-9-

Moreover,  numerous  public  interest  groups  have  been
moved  to weigh  in on  the  issue.  When  the subject  was  before
the  Court  in Ricci,  eleven  non-parties  (including  the  federal
government)  filed  amicus  briefs.  In addition  to  the  Solicitor
General's  brief,  amicus  briefs  were  filed  on  behalf  of  the
American  Civil  Liberties  Union,  the  ACLU  of Illinois,  the
National  Association  of  Criminal  Defense  Lawyers,  the
Institute  for  Justice,  the  National  League  of Cities,  the  U.S.
Conference  of Mayors,  the  National  Governors’  Association,
the  National  Association  of  Counties,  the  International
Municipal  Lawyers’  Association,  and  the  International
City/County  Management  Association.

The  importance  of  the  issue  also  has  moved  legal
commentators  to  write  on  the  topic,  the  most  prominent  of
whom  urged  the  Court  to  address  the  issue  and  explained  its
importance:

It may  be that  on  a future  occasion  the
Court  will  conclude  that  there  are  some
constitutional  limits  upon  the use  of “custodial
arrests”  as  the means  for invoking  the criminal
process  when  relatively  minor  offenses  are
involved.
Such  a  holding  would  be  most
desirable,  as  it  would  address  specifically  a
current  problem  of considerable  seriousness:
the  arbitrariness  and  inequality  which  attends
unprincipled  utilization  of  the  “custodial
arrest”  and citation  alternatives.  Moreover,  it
would  substantially  diminish  the opportunities
for  pretext  arrests,  and  thus  would  make  the
Robinson-Gustafson  “standardized  procedures”
approach  to searches  of the  person  incident  to
arrest  much  more  palatable.

3 Wayne  R.  LaFave,  Search  and Seizure  § 5.2(g),  at 94 (3d
ed.  1996) (footnote omitted).  See also William  A. Schroeder,
Warrantless  Misdemeanor Arrests and the Fourth Amendment,
58 Mo.  L. Rev.  771  (1993);  Barbara  C. Salken,  The General
Warrant  of the  Twentieth  Century?  A  Fourth  Amendment
Solution  to  Unchecked  Discretion  to  Arrest  for  Traffic
Offenses,  62 Temple  L.  Rev.  221  (1993).

Finally,  the question  presented  is extremely  important
to  the  average  American.  Virtually  all  of the  approximately
185 million  licensed  drivers  in this country  will  violate  at least
one  traffic  law  over  the  course  of their  driving  careers.  Yet
a  custodial  arrest  is one  of the  most  extreme  governmental
intrusions  imaginable  from  the  perspective  of  the  average
citizen.
Whether  the  Fourth  Amendment  places  any
limitations  on  the  use  of  custodial  arrests  for  a  fine-only
traffic  offense  potentially  impacts every driver  in this country.

B.  The Issue  is Particularly  Important  Because  of the
Need  to  Resolve  Confusion  Among  Lower  Courts
and to Correct  the Error of the Fifth  Circuit  Court
of Appeals.

As  long  as  this  important  Fourth  Amendmeni  issue
remains  unanswered  by this Court,  lower  courts  will  struggle
and  outcomes  will  differ.
Without  guidance,  lower  courts
have  taken  varied  approaches,  with  that  of the  Fifth  Circuit
being  the  most  wrought  with  error.

1.  When  confronted  with  the  issue,  lower  courts

have  reached  varied  results.

As Justice  Stevens  noted  in Robbins  v.  California,  “the
Court  has  not directly  considered  the question  whether  ‘there
are  some  constitutional  limits  upon  the  use  of  “custodial

-10-

-11-

arrests”  as  the means  for  invoking  the criminal  process  when
relatively  minor  offenses  are  involved.’”
Robbins  vy.
California,  453  U.S.  420,  450  n.11  (1981)  (Stevens,  J.,
dissenting)  (quoting  2 Wayne  R.  LaFave,  Search  and Seizure
§ 5.2,  at 290 (1st ed.  1978)).  The  result  has been  a myriad  of
approaches  on  the  part  of lower  courts.

One approach  has been  simply  to assume,  without  any
analysis,  that  the  rule  announced  in United  States  v.  Watson,
423  U.S.  411,  414-24  (1976),  which  permits  warrantless
arrests  for felonies  as  long as  there  is probable  cause,  likewise
applies  to  warrantless  arrests  for  even  the  least  serious
misdemeanor.  Higbee  v.  City of San  Diego,  911  F.2d  377,
379  (9th  Cir.  1990)  (“A  police  officer  is permitted  to  arrest
without  a warrant  if a misdemeanor  or  a felony  is committed
in the officer's  presence.”)  (citing  Watson)  (emphasis  added);
Fisher  v.  Washington  Metro.  Area  Transit  Auth.,  690  F.2d
1133,  1139  n.6  (4th  Cir.  1982)  (choosing  to  “assume”  that
probable  cause  is  the  only  issue  until  hearing  from  the
Supreme  Court  that  reasonableness  is  part  of  the  inquiry).
The  en  banc  majority  of  the  Fifth  Circuit  made  this
assumption.  See  App.  A at  4a,  195  F.3d  at  244.  Likewise,
respondents’  arguments  have  been  based  on  this  assumption.
See,  e.g.,  Brief  of Appellees  at  12, Atwater  v.  City of Lago
Vista,  165  F.3d  380  (Sth  Cir.  1999).

Other  lower  courts  have  avoided  the  constitutional
issue  and  found  custodial  arrests  improper  for minor  offenses
based  on  state-law  grounds.  See,  e.g.,  United  States  v.  Mota,
982  F.2d  1384,  1387  (9th Cir.  1993)  (refusing  to  “sanction  an
otherwise  unconstitutional  search  on  the  basis  of  an  arrest
which  is illegal  as  a  matter  of state  law”);  Barnett  v.  United
States,  525  A.2d  197,  199-200  &  n.6  (D.C.  App.  1987)
(arrests  not  permitted  for  civil  infractions  with  only  a
monetary  sanction).  At  times,  there  is an  indication  that  the

court  regarded  the custodial  arrest  as unconstitutional  as well,
but these decisions  seem  tied  to the facts  of the case  and to the
determination  that the arrest  was  invalid  under  state  law.  See,
e.g.,  Barnett,  525  A.2d  at  199  (“[I]}t  was  not  reasonable,
within  the strictures  of the Fourth  Amendment,  for [the police
officer]  to effect  a full custodial  arrest  accompanied  by a body
search.”).

At  least  one  state  high  court  reached  the  opposite
result  from  the  decision  below  purely  as  a  matter  of public
policy,  where  even  state  law did  not  compel  the  result.  State
v.  Hehman,  578  P.2d  527,  528-29  (Wash.  1978).  In doing
so,  the Supreme  Court  of Washington  noted  Justice  Stewart's
concurring  opinion  in Gustafson.  Hehman,  578  P.2d  at  529.
See also  Thomas  v.  State,  614  So.2d  468  (Fla.  1993)  (Fourth
Amendment  violated  by a full  custodial  arrest  rather  than  the
issuance  of a citation  for  the  violation  of a  noncriminal  civil
ordinance).  Without  guidance  from  this  Court,  outcomes  and
analyses  will  continue  to  vary  widely  depending  on  the
jurisdiction  in which  the  traffic  stop  occurs.

2.  The  Fifth  Circuit  en  banc  reached  the  wrong

decision.

The  three-judge  panel  decision  of  the  Fifth  Circuit
recognized  that this Court’s  Fourth  Amendment jurisprudence
compels  the  conclusion  that  placing  Ms.  Atwater  under
custodial  arrest  under  the circumstances  of this  case  violated
her  Fourth  Amendment  rights.  The  Fifth  Circuit  en  banc
majority  reached  the  opposite  conclusion  because  it erred  in
First,  the  majority  did  not  balance  the  City’s
three  ways.
interest  in enforcing  the seat  belt  law  against  the extent  of the
governmental  intrusion  to  test  the  reasonableness  of using  a
full  custodial  arrest,  a  misapplication  of  Whren  v.  United
States,  517  U.S.  806 (1996).  Second,  the majority  refused  to

-]2-

-13-

include  the  common  law  as  part  of  its  Fourth  Amendment
analysis,  ignoring  the  mandate  that  it do  so  in  Wyoming  v.
Houghton,  526  U.S.295,  _,  119 S. Ct.  1297,  1300 (1999).
Third,  the  majority  effectively  eviscerated  the holding  of this
Court  in Knowles  v.  lowa,  525  U.S.  113  (1998).

a.  Whren  v.  United  States  did  not  exempt
custodial
Fourth
Amendment’s  reasonableness  requirement.

arrests

from

the

It is beyond  dispute  “that  the  warrantless  arrest  of a
person  is  a  species  of  seizure  required  by  the  [Fourth]
Payton  v.  New  York,  445
Amendment  to  be  reasonable.”
U.S.  573,  585  (1980).  Further,  all seizures  must  be made  in
a reasonable  manner,  despite  the existence  of probable  cause.
Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1, 7-8  (1985).  As  the  Court
has  explained,  “[t]o  determine  the  constitutionality  of  a
seizure  ‘[w]e  must  balance  the  nature  and  quality  of  the
intrusion  on  the  individual’s  Fourth  Amendment  interests
against  the  importance  of the  governmental  interests  alleged
to  justify  the  intrusion.’”  Garner,  471  U.S.  at  8 (quoting
United  States  v.  Place,  462  U.S.  696,  703  (1983)).

and

autonomy

individual

Any  balancing  of  the  competing  interests  of  law
the
establishes
enforcement
unreasonableness  of  Ms.  Atwater’s  custodial  arrest.
The
government’s  side  of the  scale  is basically  empty.  The  small
fine  indicates  a  relatively  slight  governmental  interest  in the
seat  belt  law’s  enforcement.
Welsh  v.  Wisconsin,  466  U.S.
740,  754  n.14  (1984).  Further,  absolutely  none  of the  many
factors  that  can  make  a  custodial  arrest  appropriate  to
accomplish  a  legitimate  law  enforcement  objective  were
present  in this  case.  See  App.  A at  17a-18a,  195  F.3d  at 250
(Weiner,  J., dissenting).  In contrast  to the lack of interests  on
the  government’s  side  of  the  scale,  Ms.  Atwater  faced  a

significant  deprivation  of her  freedom  in the  form  of a  full
custodial  arrest.

The  Fifth  Circuit  avoided  applying  this  test  -  which
necessarily  results  in a conclusion  that  Ms.  Atwater’s  Fourth
Amendment  rights  were  violated  -  by assuming  that  Whren
v.  United  States,  517  U.S.  806  (1996),  somehow  made  it
unnecessary  to  apply  any  balancing  test.  But  the  validity  of
a  custodial  arrest  was  not  considered  in  Whren:  the  Court
considered  only  “whether  the  temporary  detention  of  a
motorist”  violated  the Fourth  Amendment.  Whren,  517  U.S.
at  808.  The  court  below  therefore  erred  in citing  it as  a basis
for  not  balancing  the competing  interests  implicated  by these
facts,  balancing  this  Court  has  done  in  numerous  decisions.
See,  e.g.,  Garner,  471  U.S.  at 9-11,  20-21  (1985)  (balancing
competing  governmental  and  individual  interests  to conclude
that  use  of deadly  force  to apprehend  a burglary  suspect  was
unreasonable  under  the Fourth  Amendment);  United  States  v.
Place,  462  U.S.  696,  703-06  (1983)  (balancing  competing
governmental  and  individual  interests  to conclude  that briefly
detaining  luggage  to  be  sniffed  by a narcotics-detecting  dog
was  reasonable  under  the  Fourth  Amendment);  Delaware  v.
Prouse,  440  U.S.  648,  654-55,  663  (1979)  (balancing
competing  governmental  and  individual  interests  to conclude
that  stopping  an  automobile  and  detaining  the driver  without
an  articulable  suspicion  was  unreasonable  under  the  Fourth
Amendment).

b.  The  Fifth  Circuit  majority  ignored  the

common  law.

The  court  below  also  went  to  great  lengths  to  avoid
considering  whether  the common  law at the time of the Fourth
Amendment’s  adoption  would  have  prohibited  a  custodial
arrest  for  a  minor  criminal  offense.  The  majority  opinion

-14-

-15-

thereby  ignored  this  Court’s  recent  mandate  that  when
determining  whether  a particular governmental  action  violates
the  Fourth  Amendment,  “we  inquire  first  whether  the action
was  regarded  as  an  unlawful  search  or  seizure  under  the
common  law  when  the  Amendment  was  framed.”  Wyoming
v.  Houghton,  526  U.S.  295,  __,  119  S.  Ct.  1297,  1300
(1999).

There  is significant authority  indicating that a custodial
arrest  would  not  have  been  sanctioned  at common  law  for the
minor  criminal  offense  at issue  in this case.  See, e.g.,  Carroll
v.  United  States,  267  U.S.
132,  157  (1925)  (quoting
Halsbury’s  Laws  of England,  Vol.  9, part III, 612) (“A peace
officer  like  a private  person  has  at common  law  no  power  of
arresting  without  a warrant  except  when  a breach  of the peace
has  been  committed  in  his  presence  or  there  is  reasonable
ground  for supposing  that  a breach  of the peace  is about  to be
committed  or  renewed  in his  presence.”);  Regina  v.  Tooley,
2 Ld.  Raym.  1296,  1301,  92 Eng.  Rep.  349,  352  (K.B.  1710)
(Lord  Holt,  J.) (observing  that  “a constable  cannot  arrest,  but
when  he sees  an  actual  breach  of the  peace;  and  if the  affray
be over,  he cannot  arrest”).

The en banc majority’s  excuse  for not  considering  this
authority  was  that  petitioners  had  not  cited  it to  the  district
court  or  three-judge  panel,*  even  though  the  panel  decision
itself relied  upon  common-law  authority  to reach  its decision.
There  are  at  least  two  problems  with  this  position.
First,
nearly  every  decision  of this  Court  considering  the  scope  of
the  Fourth  Amendment,  including  those  cited  by petitioners

*

Actually,  petitioners  did  cite  the  panel  to  authority
addressing  the  common-law  issue.  Reply  Brief  for  Appellants  at
10, Atwater  v.  City  of Lago  Vista,  165  F.3d  380  (Sth  Cir.  1999).

at  every  stage  of  this  case  as  well  as  those  cited  by  the
majority  opinion,  rely upon  the common  law  to some  extent.
Citing  any  of  this  Court’s  Fourth  Amendment  decisions
necessarily  means  raising principles  inextricably  linked  to the
common  law.  Second,  even  if there  were  any  merit  to  the
Fifth  Circuit’s  position  that  not citing specific  authorities  can
and  is  necessarily
waive  an  argument  that  supports
included  in  -  an  issue  that  was  raised,  this  Court  has  made
clear  that  in  section  1983  cases,  appellate  courts  are  not
limited  to  considering  the  specific  authority  cited  to  the
district  court.
Elder  v.  Holloway,  510  U.S.  510,  511-12
(1994)  (qualified  immunity  dispositions  reviewed  in light  of
all  relevant  precedents,  not  simply  those  cited  to,  or
discovered  by, the district  court).

-

Whether  analyzing  the  common  law  is  a  threshold
inquiry  in Fourth  Amendment  analysis,  Houghton,  526  U.S.
at__,  119  S. Ct.  at  1300,  or  the common  law  “forms  a part
of the reasonableness  inquiry,”  Wilson  v.  Arkansas,  514  U.S.
927,  929  (1995),
it  is  inseparable  from  the  Fourth
Amendment.  A court  considering  a Fourth  Amendment  issue
Cannot  just pretend  the common  law  does  not  exist.

c.  The  en  banc  majority  eviscerates

the

holding  of Knowles  v.  Iowa.

This  Court  held  in  Knowles  v.  lowa  that  a  police
officer  could  not  search  a  car  following a traffic  stop  where
the  officer  issued  the driver  a citation  and  had  not  placed  the
driver  under  arrest.  Knowles  v.  lowa,  525  U.S.  113,  114
(1998).  The  court  below,  however,  held  that  an  officer  may
place a person  under  custodial  arrest  whenever  an  officer  has
probable  cause  to  believe  that  any  traffic  violation  has
occurred,  however  minor.

-16-

-17-

This  eviscerates  any  protection  provided  by Knowles
against  full  searches  of vehicles  following  traffic  stops  when
there  is  no  justification  for  a  search  other  than  the  traffic
violation  itself.  Any  officer  wishing  to avoid  the  holding  of
Knowles  need  only  place  the driver  under  custodial  arrest  for
the  traffic  violation,  and  thereafter  search  the  vehicle  as  a
search  incident  to arrest.  United States  v.  Robinson,  414  U.S.
218  (1973).°  If the  majority's  decision  is allowed  to  stand,
the holding of Knowles  will  cease  to provide  any  protection  to
the  average  citizen.  And  even  worse,  it will  encourage  law
enforcement  officers  who  wish  to  search  automobiles  after
traffic  stops  to  place  drivers  under  custodial  arrest  for  even
the  most  insignificant  traffic  violations.

II.  This  Case  Offers  a  Particularly  Suitable  vous for

Resolving  this  Important  Issue.

This  case  presents  the  ideal  factual  framework  to
consider  the  question  presented.  While  Ricci  v.  Village  of
Arlington  Heights  appeared  at  first  blush  to  frame  the  Fourth
Amendment  issue  well,  this case  is better,  surpassing  Ricci  on
Further,  the  issue  comes  to  this  Court
every  comparison.
after  having  been  addressed  in depth  by five  written  opinions
in  the  Fifth  Circuit.
Finally,  the  straight-forward  and
commonplace  facts  of this  case  highlight  the  nature  of a full
custodial  arrest  and  will  not  cloud  resolution  of  the  legal
issue.

>  Indeed,  an  inventory  search  of Ms.  Atwater’s  vehicle  was

performed  in this  case.

A.  This  Case  Better  Frames  the  Fourth  Amendment
Issue  than  Ricci  v.  Village of Arlington  Heights,  in
Which  the  Court  Granted  Certiorari.

Ricci  v.  Village of Arlington  Heights  initially  appeared
to squarely  present  the Fourth  Amendment  issue regarding  use
Unfortunately,  its
of custodial  arrests  for  minor  offenses.
clearly-worded  question  presented  was  not  raised  by the  facts
of the case.  The  present  case,  in contrast,  has  none  of Ricci’s
faults.

First,  this  case  does  not  have  the  shortcoming  that
apparently  resulted  in the  dismissal  of the  petition  in Ricci.
The  seat  belt  offense  for  which  Gail  Atwater  was  arrested  is
It is  a misdemeanor
a  criminal  offense,  albeit  a minor  one.
Tex.  Transp.
punishable  by a  maximum  fifty-dollar  fine.
Code  Ann.  § 545.413(d)  (West  1999).
In  contrast,  the
business  ordinance  at  issue  in  Ricci  was  not  a criminal
See  Transcript  of  Oral
offense,  but  a  civil  infraction.
Argument,  Ricci  v.  Village of Arlington  Heights »No- 977501,
at  14,  33,  36-37,  1998  WL  204590  (April  21,° 1998).

There  is no  dispute  that  Gail  Atwater  was  subjected  to
a  full  custodial  arrest.  The  petitioner  in Ricci  was  arguably
subjected  to  something  less  than  a full  custodial  arrest.  Brief
of Respondent  at 9-10,  Ricci  v.  Village  of Arlington  Heights,
(No.  97-501).  While  Ricci  was  not  handcuffed,  Gail  Atwater
was  handcuffed  behind  her  back  in full  view of her  neighbors
and  her children.  Compare  Brief of Respondent  at 3, Ricci  v.
Village  of Arlington  Heights,  (No.  97-501),  with  App.  A  at
2a,  13a;  195  F.3d  at  244;  195  F.3d  at  248  (Weiner,  J.,
dissenting);  App.  B at  30a-31a;  165  F.3d  at  382.  Ricci  was
*  simply  brought  to  the  police  station  for  the  processing  of a
bond,  and  made  to  wait  in a locked  interrogation  room  while
this  was  done.  Ricci,  116  F.3d  at  289.  Gail  Atwater  was

—   —_—_—  et  =

—  6

-18-

-19-

taken  to the police station  for booking,  where  her “mug shot”
was  taken,  glasses, jewelry,  shoes,  and other personal effects
were  taken  away  from  her,  and  she  was  locked  in a jail cell.
See  App.  A at  13a;  195  F.3d  at 248  (Weiner,  J., dissenting);
App.  B at  31a;  165  F.3d  at  382-83.

B.  The  Issue  Has  Been  Thoroughly  Considered  in
Multiple  Opinions  Produced  by the Fifth  Circuit’s
En  Banc  Consideration  of this  Case.

Because  the  Fifth  Circuit  considered  this  case  on
rehearing  en  banc,  many  of the arguments  have  already  been
explored  in detail  by the  court  below.

The  Fifth  Circuit  was  sharply  divided  on  rehearing.
The  eleven-judge  majority  opinion  concluded  that  a  person
may  be placed  under  custodial  arrest  for any  criminal  offense
so  long  as  there  is probable  cause  to  believe  that  the  offense
occurred.  See  App.  A at  3a-5a,  195  F.3d  at 244-45.

In contrast,  four different  written opinions prepared by
the  six  dissenting  Judges  disagreed  with  the majority  opinion
and  concluded  that  the arrest  of Ms.  Atwater  for  not  wearing
a  seat  belt  violated  her  rights  under  the  Fourth  Amendment.
See  App.  A  at  8a-lla,  195  F.3d  at  246-47  (R.  Garza,  J.,
dissenting);  App.  A at  1 la-20a,  195  F.3d  at 247-51  (Weiner,
J.,  dissenting);  App.  A  at  20a-27a,  195  F.3d  at  251-54
(Dennis,  J.,  dissenting);  App.  B,  165  F.3d  380  (Parker,  J.)
(adopted  as dissenting  opinion  of Stewart,  J., on  rehearing  en
banc,  App.  A at  la-2a  n.*,  195  F.3d  at  243  n.*).

In  short,  the  question  presented  has  been  considered

more  thoroughly  here  than  in any  other  case.

C.  This  Set  of  Facts  Presents  the  Issue  in  a
Commonplace  Setting  Ideal  for  Consideration  by
this Court.

Finally,  because  this case  arises  from a traffic  stop,  it
is particularly  appropriate  for  consideration  of whether  the
Fourth  Amendment  limits  the  use  of  custodial  arrests  for
minor  criminal  offenses.
This  Court  is extremely  familiar
with  balancing  the  competing  interests  of  effective  law
enforcement  and  the  individual’s  right  to  be  free  from
unreasonable  governmental  intrusions  against  the  factual
backdrop  of  a  police  officer’s  stop  of  a  motor  vehicle.
Wyoming  v.  Houghton,  526  U.S.  295  (1999);  Knowles  v.
lowa,  525  U.S.  113 (1998);  Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.
806  (1996);  Berkemer  v.  McCarty,  468  U.S.  420  (1984);
Robbins  v.  California,  453  U.S.  420  (1981);  New  York  v.
Belton,  453  U.S.  454  (1981);  Gustafson  v.  Florida,  414  U.S.
260 (1973);  United  States  v.  Robinson,  414  U.S.  218  (1973);
Carroll  v.  United  States,  267  U.S.  132  (1925).  Perhaps  this
is true  because  so  many  Americans  are  licensed  drivers  and
their  most  common  interaction  with  police  occurs  during  a
traffic  stop.  The  straight-forward  facts  of this  case  vary  little
from  the  numerous  traffics  stops  that  occur  every  day  -
except  that  Gail  Atwater  was  placed  under  full  custodial
arrest.

The  fact  that  this  was  a  full  custodial  arrest  of  an
average  citizen  after  a normal  traffic  stop  makes  this  case  all
the  more  appropriate  for  consideration  by this  Court.  The
Fourth  Amendment  issue  is  particularly  well-presented
because  this is not a criminal  defendant  challenging  the arrest.

The interests  of the majority of law-abiding  Americans
usually  are  not  well  represented  by those  who  raise  Fourth
Amendment  issues  before  this  Court.  Most  of the  Court’s

-20-

-21-

decisions  construing  the  Fourth  Amendment  involve  a
criminal  defendant  contending  the exclusionary  rule  applies,
thereby  prohibiting  the admission  of probative  evidence.°

The  interests  of criminal  defendants  and  the  average
citizen  are  not  the  same.  The  natural  concern  of a criminal
defendant  seeking  to  avoid  conviction  is having  the  search
that  uncovered  incriminating  evidence  held  unconstitutional.
The  invalidity  of an  arrest  is relevant  only to the extent  that  it
can  invalidate  a search,  not  because  of the  intrusive  nature  of
the  arrest  itself.
In  contrast,  for  the  average,  law-abiding
citizen  with  no  contraband  or  incriminating  evidence  to hide,
most  searches  are  significantly  less  of  a  governmental
invasion  than  the  traumatic  experience  of being  placed  under
full  custodial  arrest.

Members  of this  Court  have  recognized  this.  “[T)he
invasion  and  disruption  of  a  man’s  life  and  privacy  which
stem  from  his  arrest  are  ordinarily  far  greater  than  the

°  As  Justice  Douglas  astutely  observed  over  forty  years

ago:

Decisions  under  the Fourth  Amendment,  taken  in the long
view,  have  not  given  the  protection  to  the  citizen  which
the  letter  and  spirit  of  the  Amendment  would  seem  to
require.  One  reason,  I think,  is that  wherever  a culprit  is
caught  red-handed,  as  in  leading  Fourth  Amendment
cases,  it is difficult  to adopt and enforce a rule  that would
turn  him  loose.  A rule  protective  of law-abiding  citizens
is  not  apt  to  flourish  where  its  advocates  are  usually
criminals.

Draper  v.  United  States,  358  U.S.  307,  333-34  (1959)  (Douglas,
J., dissenting).

relatively  minor  intrusions  attending  a  search  of  his
premises.”  Chimel  v.  California,  395  U.S.  752,  776 (1969)
(White,  J., dissenting);  see  also  United  States  v.  Robinson,
414  U.S.  218,  237  (1973)  (Powell,  J.,  concurring)  (the
custodial  arrest,  not the search,  “is the significant  intrusion  of
State  power  into  the privacy  of one’s  person”).

A custodial  arrest  includes  a search  of the  arrestee’s
entire  body, and of the contents  of pockets  and any containers
found  within  the  person’s  reach.  New  York  v.  Belton,  453
U.S.  454,  460-61  n.4  (1981).  Likewise,  an  inventory  search
of the  vehicle  may  be performed  and  the  vehicle  impounded,
with  money  charged  for  storage  and  the  vehicle’s  release.
South  Dakota  v.  Opperman,  428  U.S.  364,  367-76  (1976).
Further,  the arrestee  can  experience  the public  humiliation  of
being  led  away  in  handcuffs,  and,  once  transported  to  the
police  station,  fingerprinted  and  booked,  and  personal  items
taken  away.  The  person  then  potentially  faces  incarceration
a  judicial
for  as  long  as
determination  of  probable  cause.
County  of Riverside  v.
McLaughlin,  500  U.S.  44,  56-57  (1991).

forty-eight  hours  without

The  repercussions  of a custodial  arrest  continue  after
detention  ends.  The  arrest  will  be a matter  of public  record,
maintained  and  disseminated,  potentially  damaging  the
Further,  an  arrest  record  can  make  it
person's  reputation.
difficult  -  and at times  impossible  -  to obtain  employment.

Although  both  traffic  stops  and  custodial  arrests  are
Fourth  Amendment  “seizures,”  Berkemer  v.  McCarty,  468
U.S.  420,  436-37  (1984),  it is  well-settled  that  a  custodial
arrest  is a  far  greater  governmental  intrusion  than  the  brief
roadside  detention  typically  associated  with  a traffic  stop that
ends  with  issuance  of a citation.  In recognition  of this,  there
are  fewer  constitutional  protections  implicated  by the  lesser

-22-

25.

Debra  Irwin
Law  Offices  of DeCarli  & Irwin
12034  Research  Blvd.,  Suite  6
Austin,  Texas  78759
(512)  258-9299
Fax:  (512)  258-1395

727  East  Dean  Keeton  St.

Fax:  (512)  471-6988

*Counsel  of Record

governmental  intrusion  of the  traffic  stop.  Berkemer,  468
U.S.  at 437-38,  440 (because  traffic  stops are  “presumptively
temporary  and  brief,”  Miranda  warnings  need  not  be given).

For  the  average  motorist,  as  it was  for  Ms.  Atwater
here,  a  custodial  arrest  is a  greater  governmental  intrusion
than  a  traffic  stop  or  a  search,  and  far  worse  than  the
punishment  imposed  for  the  violation  itself.  See  App.  A at
18a,  195  F.3d  at 250 (Weiner,  J., dissenting)  (noting  that  the
arrest  amounted  to Officer  Turek’s  punishing of Ms.  Atwater,
which  “is  also,  at  least  potentially,  an  independent  violation
of the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments,  which  permit  the
infliction  of punishment  only  after  a  formal  adjudication”).
This  case  presents  an  ideal  opportunity  to consider  a  Fourth
Amendment  challenge  to  the  use  of a  custodial  arrest  for  a
minor  criminal  offense,  a  challenge  brought  by  a  typical
motorist,  not  motivated  by a desire  to  invalidate  a subsequent
search  and  exclude  probative  evidence.

CONCLUSION

Even  the  most  law-abiding  Americans  violate  minor
traffic  laws  from  time  to time.  They expect  that,  if observed
by a police  officer,  they  will  get a ticket.  These  millions  of
licensed  drivers,  however,  do  not  expect  to  be placed  under
custodial  arrest  for  traffic  offenses  punished  only  by  fines.
Petitioners  respectfully  request  that  this  Court  grant  their
petition  for  writ  of  certiorari,  and  address  the  Fourth
Amendment  limits  on  custodial  arrests  for  fine-only  traffic
offenses  —  offenses  that can  be enforced just as effectively  by
issuing  traffic  citations.

Michael  Haas  ("Haas"),  as  next  friend  of Anya  Savannah
Haas and Mackinley  Xavier  Haas, appealed  the district court's
grant of summary  judgment  in favor  of Defendants-Appellees

of this  court  reversed  in part and  remanded.  See Atwater  v.

I

—

Officer Turek  arrested  Gail  Atwater  for failing to wear
her  seat  belt,  failing  to  fasten  her  children  in  seat  belts,
driving  without  a  license,  and  failing  to  provide  proof  of
insurance.  Officer  Turek  handcuffed  Atwater  and  took  her to
jail,  where  she  spent  approximately  one hour.
Atwater
appeared  before  a  magistrate  and  was  released  after  posting
bond.

>

The  panel  affirmed  the  district  court's  summary
judgment  on  all other  claims.  See Atwater,  165  F.3d  at  389.  We
reinstate  this  part  of the  panel  opinion.

>  Atwater  advances  an  alternative  argument  for  the  first
time  in her en  banc  brief.  She  argues  that  in determining  whether
her  arrest  violated  the  Fourth  Amendment,  we  should  follow  the
common  law  rule  that  existed  when  the  Fourth  Amendment  was
promulgated,  which  she  claims  limited  the  circumstances  under
which  a  misdemeanant  could  be arrested  without  a  warrant.  She
did not  raise  this argument  before  the district  court  or the panel  that
Instead,  the  panel  considered  this
initially  considered  this  case.
argument  sua  sponte,  and  even  though  it ruled  in Atwater's  favor,
it declined  to do so  based  on  the common  law  rule.  See Atwater  v.
City of Lago  Vista,  165  F.3d  380,  386  (Sth  Cir.  1999)  (noting  the
common  law  rule  and  stating  that  “[a}lthough  the  Fourth

3a

4a

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

Amendment  and common  law do not  always coincide,  the Supreme
Court  has recognized  the logic of distinguishing  between  minor  and
serious  offenses  in evaluating  the  reasonableness  of a seizure  under
the  Fourth  Amendment").  Because  Atwater  did  not  properly  raise
this  argument  previously,  she  has  waived  her  right  to  pursue  this
issue  here.  See  Craddock  Int'l  Inc.  v.  W.K.P.  Wilson  & Son,  Inc.,
116  F.3d  1095,  1105  (Sth Cir. 1997) ("To prevail  on an  issue  raised
for  the  first  time  on  appeal,  an  appellant  must  show a plain  (clear
or  obvious)  error  that  affects  substantial  rights.");  cf  Arenson  v.
Southern  Univ.  Law  Ctr.,  53  F.3d  80,  81  (Sth  Cir.1995)  (per
curiam)  ("Appellant's  request  [in  his  petition  for  rehearing]  for
Title  VII  relief  is  denied  because  Arenson  waived  his  Title  VII
claim  by failing  to  seek  a  ruling  on  that  issue  from  the  Arenson  I
panel.").

Moreover,  Atwater  is unable  to cite any cases  where  courts
have  invoked  the  common  law  rule  to  invalidate  warrantless
misdemeanor  arrests  otherwise  supported  by  probable  cause.
Indeed,  the  cases  uniformly  uphold  warrantless  misdemeanor
arrests  where  probable  cause  exists,  even  where  variants  of  the
common  law  standards  to  which  Atwater  refers  are  incorporated
into  state  law  and  raised  before  the  court.  See  Vargas-Badillo  v.
Diaz-Torres,  114  F.3d  3, 6 (ist  Cir.1997)  ("To  date,  neither  the
Supreme  Court  nor  this  circuit  ever  has  held  that  the  Fourth
Amendment  prohibits  warrantless  arrests  for  misdemeanors  not
committed  in the presence  of arresting  officers.");  Pyles  v.  Raisor,
60 F.3d  1211,  1215  (6th Cir. 1995)  (rejecting  a Fourth  Amendment
challenge  to  a  misdemeanor  arrest  when  probable  cause  existed,
even  though  the  arrest  may  have  violated  the  plaintiff's  state  right
"as  an  alleged  misdemeanant  to  be  arrested  only  when  the
misdemeanor  is  committed  in  the  presence  of  the  arresting
officer");  Fields  v.  City  of South  Houston,  922  F.2d  1183,  1189
(Sth  Cir.1991)  (upholding  a  misdemeanor  arrest  supported  by

116  S.Ct.  at  1776.  For example,  it is "necessary  actually  to
perform"  a balancing  analysis  notwithstanding  the  existence
of probable  cause  when a search  or  seizure  involves  deadly
force,  an  unannounced  entry  into  a home,  entry  into  a home
without  a warrant,  or  physical  penetration  of the  body.  See
id.  (reviewing  cases).

After  reviewing  the  record,  we  conclude  that  Officer
Turek  had  probable  cause  to  arrest  Atwater  and  that  he did
not  conduct  the  arrest  in  such  an  “extraordinary  manner."
Neither  party  disputes  that  Officer  Turek  had  probable  cause
to  arrest  Atwater.  Atwater  admits  that  she  was  not  wearing
her  seat  belt  and  that  she  had  not  belted  in  her  children.
Operating  a motor  vehicle  without  wearing a seat  belt violates
Texas  law,’  and  Officer  Turek  had  discretion  to  arrest

probable  cause  and  stating  that  "[t}he  United  States  Constitution
does  not  require  a warrant  for  misdemeanors  not  occurring  in the
presence  of the arresting  officer");  Barry v.  Fowler,  902  F.2d  770,
772  (9th  Cir.1990)  ("The  requirement  that  a  misdemeanor  must
have  occurred  in  the  officer's  presence  to  justify  a  warrantless
arrest  is  not  grounded  in  the  Fourth  Amendment.");
Street  v.
Surdyka,  492  F.2d  368,  371-72  (4th Cir. 1974)  ( "We  do not  think
the  fourth  amendment  should  now  be  interpreted  to  prohibit
warrantless  arrests
for  misdemeanors  committed  outside  an
officer's  presence.");  cf.  Whren,  517  U.S.  at  819,  116  S.Ct.  at
1777  ("Here  the  District  Court  found  that the officers  had probable
Cause  to believe  that petitioners  had  violated  the traffic  code.  That
rendered  the stop reasonable  under  the  Fourth  Amendment.  .

was  based  on probable cause  and because  it was  not conducted
in  the  above-described  "extraordinary  manner,"  Officer
Turek's  arrest  of Atwater  was  reasonable  under  the  Fourth

REYNALDO  G.  GARZA,  Circuit  Judge,  dissenting:

I believe  that  the  original  opinion  of the  panel  that  |

was on,  which  can  be found  at  165  F.3d,  380 (Sth Cir. 1999)
is  sufficient  to show  that  the seizure  and  handcuffing  of Mrs.
Atwater  in this  case  was  unreasonable  and  uncalled  for.

I write separately  in dissent  because  I believe  that  our
colleagues  in  the majority  are  wrong  in not  dividing  an  arrest
or  a stop  and a seizure  of the person  arrested  or  stopped.

We  have  of necessity  to  keep  in mind  that  this  was  a
traffic  stop  or  arrest  of Mrs.  Atwater  for  failure  to  have  her
seatbelt  or  her  children's  seatbelts  on  when  stopped.
As
pointed out  by my colleague,  Judge  Parker,  and  author  of the
original  opinion,  Mrs.  Atwater's  seatbelt  offense  was  a
misdemeanor  for  which  she  could  be  fined  up to  $50 and  no
more.  Her  offense  would  in no  way  have  been  a  danger  to
any  one  else,  but  herself  and  her  children.

eee

violated,  we  do not  reach  the  issue  of qualified  immunity.").  Nor
do  we  discuss  the  liability  of the  City of Lago  Vista.  See  Doe  on
Behalf of Doe  v.  Dallas  Indep.  Sch.  Dist.,  153  F.3d  211,  216  (Sth
Cir. 1998) ("Thus,  § 1983  municipal  liability  may  be imposed  when
(1) the enforcement  of a  municipal  policy  or  custom  was  (2)  ‘the
moving  force’  of  the  violation  of  federally  protected  rights.")

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

I have  been  a Texas  lawyer  for over  sixty years  and an
Article  III Federal  Judge  in Texas  for over  thirty-eight  years.
I think  that  I can  take  judicial  notice  of  the  fact  that  in  a
regular  traffic  stop;  when  a person  runs a red  light,  makes  a
wrong  turn,  is  speeding,  or  in  this  case  is  not  wearing  a
seatbelt,  the  usual  procedure  for  the  officer  making  the  stop
or  the arrest  is to give the accused  a citation,  which  shows  the
charge  against  the  person  driving  the  car,  a notice  to  appear
before  a municipal judge on  a certain  day at a certain  time and
signed  by the  accusing  officer;  which  also  adds  a paragraph
that  is signed  by the  accused  that  they  agree  to  appear  on  the
date  and  time  stated  and  that  is the  end  of the  story.

There  are  times  when  during a traffic  stop  an  officer
finds  that  the  driver  for  instance,  is driving  while  under  the
influence  of either  alcohol  or  drugs;  the officer  sees  a gun  on
the  seat  of the  car;  the  car  smells  of marihuana;  the  officer
sees  packages  of cocaine  or  some  other  reason,  like  a check
of the  license  number  of the  person  stopped  shows  that  the
person  is a  fugitive  or  has  another  charge  pending;  in which
case  the officer  that  made  the stop  seizes  the person  and  takes
him  or  her  to  the  police  station  to  await  being  taken  before  a
Magistrate,  where  the officer  makes  the complaint  against the
person  and  the  Magistrate  then  sets  a bond  or  refuses  to allow
one,  whichever  he chooses.

There  is no  evidence  in our  case  that  there  was  any
reason  for  Mrs.  Atwater  to  be  seized  and  taken  to  the  police
station  where  she  waited  for  an  hour  for  a  Magistrate  to
release  her.

The  majority  setting  aside  the panel opinion  makes  no
mention  of  an  affidavit  that  is  in  the  Record  Excerpts  of

appellants,  Gail Atwater  and her husband,  on behalf of two of
their  children.  The  affidavit  is that  of Keith  A.  Campbell,
who  was  a  member  of the  Recruitment  Unit  of  the  Austin
Police  Department  from  August  1994  to  March  1997.  In the
affidavit  he  makes  mention  of  all  the  things  he  did  when
screening  applicants  for  positions  in  the  Austin  Police
Department.
Mr.  Campbell  states  he  has  reviewed  the
personnel  file  of  Michael  Barton  Turek  and  he  can  state
without  reservation  that he would  not have  recommended  this
individual  to be hired  by the Austin  Police  Department  for the
following  reasons:

Mr.  Campbell's  affidavit  is an  eye-opener  of the  kind
of  person  Officer  Turek,  who  saw  fit  to  handcuff  Mrs.
Atwater  behind  her  back  for  not  wearing  her  seatbelt,  is.
Mrs.  Atwater  and  her  husband  have  sued  the  City  of  Lago
Vista  for  its  unreasonable  hiring  and  lack  of  training  of
Turek.

Under  Texas  law,  the  City  of  Lago  Vista  is  not
responsible  for the actions  of their  police officers  unless  they
violate  somebody's  Constitutional  Rights.  Our  colleagues  in
the majority  seem  to think that if an officer  has prubable cause
to make  a stop and an arrest  it immunizes  them  to where  they
can do whatever  they please.  This approach  is wrong  because

9a

Court of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

in  my  view,  probable  cause
constitutional  violation.

will  never  immunize  a

Officer  Turek  had  probable  cause  to  stop  the  car  that
was  being  driven  by  Mrs.  Atwater  for  failure  to  have  her
seatbelt  on,  but  he should  have  given  her a citation  to appear
instead  of seizing  her,  putting  handcuffs  behind  her back,  and
taking  her  to  the  police  station.  He  would  have  taken  her
children  with  her  except  that  a  neighbor  that  came  on  the
scene  took  the  children  to  her  home.

I strongly  believe  that  my  duty  under  the  oath  that  I
have  taken,  once  as  a  United  States  District  Judge,  then  as  an
Appellate  Judge,  is to uphold the Constitution  and Laws of the
United  States.  Under  Article  IV  of the  Amendments  to  the
Constitution,  the  seizure  is different  then  the  stop  and  the
arrest  of  Mrs.  Atwater  was  unreasonable  and  therefore  a
violation  of the Constitution  of the United  States.  I cannot  see
why  some  of  my  colleagues  are  unwilling  to  say  that  the
seizure  by Officer  Turek  was  unreasonable.

WIENER,  Circuit  Judge,  dissenting:

Today  a majority  of this court  announces  that  any  full
custodial  arrest,  replete  with  transportation  to  jail  and
booking,  is per se  a reasonable  seizure  within  the meaning  of
the  Fourth  Amendment  as  long  as  the  arresting  officer  has
probable  cause  to  believe  that  the  individual  being  arrested
has  violated  the  law  -  any  law,  even  an  innocuous  traffic
ordinance.  Not  only  does  this  holding  ignore  the  Supreme
Court's  longstanding  pronouncements  that  every  Fourth
Amendment  analysis  must  turn  on a tripartite  balancing  of
individual  interests,  government  interests,  and  the degree  of

certainty that the government  interest  will be furthered  by the
search or seizure
at issue, but  it also turns a blind eye on the
extreme
facts of this case; facts that so clearly demonstrate an
unreasonable seizure  that  those  of my colleagues  who  concur
in  the  majority  opinion  should  have  been  tipped  off  that
something  must  be critically  awry  with  its  reasoning.  The
result  reached  is  so  counterintuitive  that  it cries  out  for  a
deeper  look.  As the  Fourth  Amendment  requires  that  every
seizure must be effected  pursuant  to a legitimate governmental
interest,  and  as  the  only  conceivable  reason  for  the  full
custodial  arrest  at issue  here  was  Officer  Turek's  illegitimate
desire  to punish  Atwater,  I respectfully  dissent.

the  facts

When,  as  here,

virtually  speak  for
themselves,  it is disappointing  -  even  if not  surprising  -  that
the majority  opinion goes out of its way to sanitize  them.  The
instant  facts  reveal  that  this  case  is not  truly  about a traffic
stop followed  by an arrest;  it is about  a police officer  going to.
extreme  lengths  to satisfy a personal  crusade  or possibly  even
4 vendetta.  The  evidence  would  allow a jury  reasonably  to
infer  that  Officer  Turek  had  been  eagerly  awaiting  the
Opportunity  to threaten,  frighten,  and humiliate  Gail Atwater:
Approximately  two  months  prior to the  incident  in question,
Officer  Turek  had pulled Atwater  over  for a putative  seatbelt
violation;  however,  much  to his dismay,  he had  been  forced
to  let her drive  off without  his  issuing  her a citation  when  he
discovered  that she and the other occupants  of  her car had
their  seatbelts  securely  fastened.

Officer Turek's  frustration  over this prior incident  was
made  readily  apparent  from  the  very  beginning  of the traffic

stop  and  arrest  that  are  now  under  review.  When  Atwater
was  pulled  over  this  time,  she  was  driving  her  two  children,
ages  six  and  four,  home  from  soccer  practice.
She  was
traveling  in a residential  neighborhood,  on  bone-dry  streets,
in broad  daylight,  and  at  a  reasonable,  lawful  rate  of speed.
When  Officer  Turek  observed  that  neither  Atwater  nor  her
children  were  wearing  seatbelts,  he proceeded  to pull her  car
over.  According  to  the testimony,  Officer  Turek  approached
Atwater's  car  and yelled at her in a belligerent  and threatening
manner,  pointing  his finger menacingly  in Atwater's  face  and
Officer  Turek
terrifying  her  and  her  young  children.
screamed  that they had  “had this conversation  before"  and that
this  time  she  (Atwater)  was  going  to jail.

Officer  Turek  then  ordered  Atwater  to  produce  her
Atwater  informed
driver's  license  and  proof  of  insurance.
him  that  both  documents  had  been  in her  purse  when  it was
stolen  two  days earlier.  She  did,  though,  provided  him  with
her license  number  and address  from  her checkbook.  Despite
the fact that Officer  Turek  had seen  Atwater's  driver's  license
and  proof  of  insurance  when  he  had  pulled  her  over  only
weeks  earlier,  he proceeded  to  make  good  on  his  promise  to
take  her to jail.  First,  he had  her  step out  of her car;  next,  he
handcuffed  her  behind  her  back;  then  he  loaded  her  into  the
back  of his  squad  car  and  took  her  to  the  police  station;  and
there  she  was  forced  to  remove  her  shoes  and  glasses,  empty
her  pockets,  and  allow  her  “mug  shot"  to  be taken.  Finally,
Atwater  was  placed  in  a  jail  cell  and  made  to  wait  for
approximately  one  hour  before  being  produced  before  a
magistrate.

Il

The  law  is long and  well  established  that,  under  the
Fourth  Amendment,  the scope of a search  or seizure  "must  be
strictly  tied  to  and  justified  by  the  circumstances  which
rendered  its initiation  permissible."  Terry v.  Ohio,  392  U.S.
1,  19,  88  S.Ct.  1868,  20  L.Ed.2d  889  (1968)  (citation
omitted).  Implicit  in this  simple  but  forceful  statement  of the
law  -  and  explicit  in its application  by the Supreme  Court  to
a  broad  panoply  of  cases  -
is  the  precept  that  the
permissibility  of any search  or seizure depends on a balancing
of (1)  the  government's  purported  interest  in  effecting  the
search  or  seizure,  (2) discounted  by the  degree  of certainty
that the search  or seizure  will  in fact further  the government's
interest,  against  (3)  the  extent  of  any  infringement  on  the
targeted  individual's  constitutionally  protected  privacy  and
liberty  interests.

'  Thus,  the Supreme Court  has held that the government's
interest  in protecting  police officers  is sufficient  to justify  a limited
"stop  and  frisk"  of an  individual  when  supported  by reasonable
suspicion  that the individual  is carrying  a weapon.  Terry,  392  U.S.
at  27,  88  S.Ct.  1868.  But  the  same  governmental  interest  is
insufficient  to  justify  the  “wholly  different  kind  of  intrusion”
involved  in an  arrest  absent  a greater  degree of certainty  -  namely,
probable  cause  -  that  the  seizure  will  vindicate  the  governmental
interest  in question.  /d.  at  26,  88 S.Ct.  1868.

Similarly,  although  reasonable  suspicion  is  sufficient  to
justify  a  “stop  and  frisk"  for  the  purpose  of protecting  the  police,
the  same  quantum  of certainty  is insufficient  to justify  a  “stop and
frisk"  for  the  less  important  and  more  generalized  governmental
interest  in investigating  and preventing  crime.  /d.  at 22-23,  26-27,

l3a

l4a

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

The  problem  I perceive  with  the  majority  opinion  is
that  its  analysis  focuses  solely  on  the  quantum  of certainty
involved  in the case,  to the exclusion  of the other  two  relevant
variables:  the importance  of the government's  interest  and the
extent  of the  intrusion  on  the  individual's  liberty  and  privacy
interests.  This  is all  the  more  regrettable  in light of the  fact
that  quantum  of certainty  is not  even  at  issue  here:  Atwater
concedes  that Officer  Turek  had probable cause  to believe  that
she  had  broken  the  law  by failing  to wear  a seatbelt.  Indeed,
Atwater  further  acknowledges  that  the  government's  interest
in enforcing  the  traffic  laws  was  sufficient  to justify  Officer
Turek's  decision,  based  on  probable  cause,  to effect  a traffic
stop,  and  that  the  same  interest  would  have  justified  his
What  Atwater  vehemently  denies,
issuing  her  a  citation.
though,  is  that  the  government  had  any  legitimate  interest
whatsoever  -  whether  on  the  basis  of reasonable  suspicion,
probable  cause,  or  even  absolute  certainty  -  in effecting  her

88  S.Ct.  1868  (holding  that  a  generalized  interest  in  crime
prevention  justifies  “approach[ing]  a  person  for  purposes  of
investigating  possibly  criminal  behavior,"  but  that  it is “the  more
immediate  interest  of the  police  officer  in  taking  steps  to  assure
himself  that  the  person  with  whom  he  is dealing  is not  armed  with
a weapon"  that  supplies  the justification  for the  intrusion  involved
The  Supreme  Court  applied  the  same
in  a  stop  and  frisk).
framework  in Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1,  105  S.Ct.  1694,
85  L.Ed.2d  1 (1985),  when  it held  that  the  government's  interest
in enforcing  the  criminal  laws  is sufficient  to justify  an  arrest  that
is  supported  by  probable  cause  to  believe  that  a  suspect  has
committed  a burglary,  but  that  the  government  cannot  employ  the
more  extreme  form  of seizure  involved  in the  use  of deadly  force
absent  the  more  important  governmental  interest  of protecting  the
public  from  the  threat  of serious  bodily  harm.

full Custodial  arrest  and  transporting  her  to  jail  when  the
issuing  of a citation  would  have fully protected  and vindicated
all of the government's  interests.

ee  "The  FourthY  Amendment  proceeds  as  much  by
limitations  upon the  scope  of  governmental  action  as  by
imposing  preconditions  upon  its initiation."  /d.  at 28-29,  88
S.Ct.  1868;  see  also  United  States  v.  Place,  462  U.S.  696,
707-08,  103  S.Ct.  2637,  77  L.Ed.2d  110  (1983).  The  mere
fact  that  Officer  Turek  was  justified  in pulling  Atwater  over,
and  would  have  been  justified  in issuing  her  a citation,  does
not  necessarily  mean  that  he  was  justified  in taking  the  far
more  intrusive  step  of  effecting  her  full  custodial  arrest,
complete  with  behind-the-back  handcuffing,  transporting  to
jail, and  booking.  "In justifying  [a] particular  intrusion  [a]
police officer  must  be able  to point to specific  and articulable
facts which,  taken together with rational  inferences  from those
facts,  reasonably  warrant  that  intrusion."  Terry,  392  U.S.  at
21, 88 S.Ct.  1868.  In other  words,  to justify each  successive,
increasingly  stringent  intrusion  on  an  individual's  liberty and
privacy  interests,  a police  officer  must  at  a minimum  be able
to  articulate  some  reason  why  it was  necessary  to  effect  the
further intrusion.  The  articulated  reason  does  not  need  to be
independent  of the  reasons  that justify  the  initial  intrusions:
"The test  is whether  those  interests  are sufficiently  substantial,
not  whether  they  are  independent  of  the  interest
in
investigating  crimes  effectively  and  apprehending  suspects.  "
Place,  462  U.S.  at 704,  103 S.Ct.  2637.  But  if the identified
reasons  for  both  the  initial  and  the  successive  intrusions  are
the same,  a police officer  must  be able  to advance  a plausible
claim  that the initial  intrusions  were  inadequate  fully to serve

15a

l6a

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

the  proffered  governmental  interests.’  If the  officer  cannot
plausibly  make  that  claim  -  in other  words,  if there  were  no
legitimate  reasons  for  the  further  intrusions  -  then  the
heightened  intrusions  are  by definition  "unreasonable."

I agree  with  the  majority  that  the courts  should  avoid
getting  into the business  of micro-managing  arrests.  I do not
agree,  however,  that  a jurisprudence  that  is  faithful  to  the
fundamental  principles  of the  Fourth  Amendment  -  and  that
thus  would  find  a Fourth  Amendment  violation  in the  instant
case  -  requires  the courts  to engage  in such  a practice.  In the
overwhelming  majority  of cases,  when  a  police  officer  has
probable  cause  to believe  that  an  individual  has  violated  or  is
violating  the law,  there  are  good and obvious  reasons  for that
Examples  of  reasons  that
officer  to  arrest  the  individual.
justify  a  police  officer's  effecting  an  arrest  include  risk  of
flight,  see  United  States  v.  Salerno,  481  U.S.  739,  745,  107
S.Ct.  2095,  95  L.Ed.2d  697  (1987);  need  to  interrogate  an
individual  or  search  him  for  evidence,  see  New  York  v.
Harris,  495  U.S.  14,  110 S.Ct.  1640,  109 L.Ed.2d  13 (1990);
and  need  to  protect  the  community  from  any  threat  that  an
individual  poses  to its safety,  see  generally  Bass  v.  Robinson,
167  F.3d  1041,  1045  (6th Cir.1999);  Dunn  v.  Denk,  79 F.3d

2

This  is an  objective  standard  rather  than  a  subjective
one.  See  Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.  806,  814,  116  S.Ct.
1769,  135  L.Ed.2d  89  (1996)  ("[T]he  Fourth  Amendment's
concern  with  ‘reasonableness’  allows  certain  actions  to be taken  in
certain  circumstances,  whatever  the subjective  intent.").  Thus,  the
question  is not  whether  the  police  officer  had  a plausible  reason  in
mind  at the time  of the arrest,  but  rather  whether  the  facts,  viewed
objectively  and  from  a  distance,  admit  of  a  plausible  reason
justifying  the  arrest.

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

401,  408  (Sth  Cir.1996),  cert.  denied,  519  U.S
.
S.Ct.  61,  136  L.Ed.2d  23 (1996).

,

'

S.  813,

-

Clearly,  none  of these  or  other  similar

applicable to Officer  Turek's  arrest  of Atwater.  She is a local
resident,  well-known  to Officer  Turek.  There  is no  indication
that  she  posed  even  a  minimal  flight  risk.  The  evidence
amply  demonstrates  that  she  did  not  pose  any  threat  to  the
officer  or  the community.  And  there  was  no  need  to conduct
any further  investigation,  as  the  full  extent  of  Atwater's
violation  of  the  seatbelt  law  had  already  been  Clearly
ascertained.  By this process  of elimination,  then,  the one  and
only conclusion  that can  be reached  on the evidence  is that the
sole  reason  Officer  Turek  arrested  Atwater  was  his
determination  to inflict  punishment  on  her, above  and beyond
the $50.00  fine  prescribed  by Texas  law.  Not  only  is  the
arresting Officer's  personal  desire  to punish a constitutionally
illegitimate reason  under  the Fourth  Amendment  for effecting
a seizure;  it  is  also,  at  least  potentially,  an  independent
Violation  of  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments,  which
permit  the  infliction  of  punishment  only  after  a  formal
adjudication.  See  Bell  v.  Wolfish,  441  U.S.  520.  99  S.Ct
1861,  60  L.Ed.2d  447  (1979);
Kennedy  v.  Mendeas-
Martinez, 372  U.S.  144, 83 S.Ct.  554, 9 L.Ed.2d  644 (1963):
Valencia  v.  Wiggins,  981  F.2d  1440  (Sth  Cir.1993).
Allowing  Officer  Turek  to skate  here  gives the officer  on  the
street carte  blanche  to  be  a one-person  cop  cum  judge  cum
jury  cum executioner:  In effect,  he can  arrest,  charge  try
convict,  and  both  assess  and  inflict  punishment.
aig

.  The desire of the majority  of my esteemed  colleagues
to arrive  at a simple,  bright-line  rule that can  be easily applied

17a

18a

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

Court  of Appeals  En Banc  Opinion  -  11/24/99

by officers  in the  field  is both  understandable  and  laudable.’
But  such  "a  rigid  all-or-nothing  model  of justification  and
regulation  under  the  Amendment"  ignores  the complexity  of
real-world  events  and  thus  fails  to  remain  faithful  to  the
fundamental  principles  underlying  the  Fourth  Amendment.
It also  has  the
See  Terry,  392  U.S.  at  17,  88  S.Ct.  1868.
unfortunate  effect  of licensing  the admittedly  rare  rogue  patrol
officer  to  inflict  vigilante  punishment  on a citizen  under  the
guise  of an  arrest  -  a  state  of  affairs  that  the  Constitution
clearly  does  not  tolerate.

>

The  majority  claims  that  its  holding  ‘s  mandated  by
language  found  in  Whren,  517  U.S.  at  818,  116  S.Ct.  1769
("Where  probable  cause  has  existed,  the  only  cases  in  which  we
have  found  it necessary  actually  to perform the  ‘balancing’  analysis
involved  searches  or  seizures  conducted  in  an  extraordinary
manner,  unusually  harmful  to  an  individual's  privacy  or  even
physical  interests").  Not only is this  language  dicta,  however  -  the
Whren  court  was  assessing  the  validity  of a traffic  stop,  not  a  full
custodial  arrest--but  it arguably  supports  my  position  rather  than
the majority's.  "Extraordinary"  is defined  in Webster's  Dictionary
regular,  or  customary.”
as  “going  beyond  what  is  usual,
WEBSTER'S  SEVENTH  NEW  COLLEGIATE  DICTIONARY
Of  course,  what  qualifies  as  “usual,  regular,  or
296  (1965).
customary”  is entirely  dependent  on  the  circumstances;  an  action
that  would  be deemed  an  extraordinary  response  to one  set  of facts
might be thought  quite ordinary  and commonplace  if the  facts  were
It  need  hardly  be  said  that  a  full  custodial  arrest,
different.
complete  with  behind-the-back-handcuffing,  transporting  to  jail,
and  booking,  is  an  extraordinary  response  to  a  local  mother's
daytime  seatbelt  violation.

Simply  stated:

The  rule  that  I would  apply  to this  case  has  the  virtue
of being just as  simple as  the majority's,  and  thus just as  easy
for  the  police  to  apply  in the  myriad  complex  and  confusing
Situations  that  they  regularly  encounter,  without,  however,
jettisoning  the fundamental  principles  embodied  in the Fourth
Amendment.
Before  a  police  officer  can
constitutionally  place an  individual  under  full custodial  arrest,
even  with  probable  cause,  the  officer  must  have  a plausible,
articulable  reason  for  effecting  such  an  intrusion  -  a  reason
other  than  a  desire  on  the  part  of the  officer  to  punish  the
individual  for  his  or  her  conduct.‘
This  is  hardly  a
burdensome  imposition  on  the police;  I cannot  see  any  reason
for  the  police  to  complain  about  having  to  articulate  some
manner  of justification  for the significant  intrusion  on  Fourth
Amendment  privacy  and  liberty  interests  inherent  in effecting
any  full  custodial  arrest.

Try as  I may,  I can  discern  no  legitimate  justification
whatsoever  for Officer  Turek's  lamentable  decision  to arrest,
handcuff,  transport,  book,  and jail Atwater;  conversely,  I see
every  indication  that Officer  Turek's  sole purpose  in doing so
was  unilaterally  to inflict an illegitimate  -  and unconstitutional
-  punishment  on  her.  For  these  reasons,  I must  respectfully
dissent.

DENNIS,  Circuit  Judge,  dissenting:

I respectfully  dissent.
