Respondents,  City  of Lago  Vista,  former  Lago  Vista
Officer  Bart  Turek,  and  Police  Chief  Frank  Miller,
respectfully  submit  this  Brief in Opposition  to the  Petition
for Writ of Certiorari  filed by Petitioners,  Gail  Atwater,  and
Michael  Haas  as  next  friend  of Anya  Savannah  Haas  and
Mackinley  Xavier  Haas.  Petitioners  seek  review  of the
judgment  and opinion of the United  States Court of Appeals
for  the  Fifth  Circuit,  rendered  on  November  24,  1999.
Petitioners’  request  for writ  should  be denied.

STATEMENT  OF THE  CASE

I.  Statement  of Facts.

On  March  26,  1997,  former  Lago  Vista  Police  Officer
Turek  observed  a Dodge  pickup  truck  in which  neither  the
adult driver or the two young  children  were  wearing  seatbeits.
One  of the children  was standing  on the passenger  seat in
the truck.  In accordance
with  the Texas  law, Officer  Turek
arrested  Petitioner  Atwater  for  failing  to  wear  a  seatbelt,
failing  to  seatbelt  her  children,  failure  to  show  proof  of
financial  responsibility  and  failure  to  display  her  driver’s
license.  It is undisputed  that  she  committed  each  of these
offenses.  It is undisputed  that Texas  law authorized  Petitioner
Atwater’s  custodial  arrest.

Petitioner  Atwater  was  transported  to  the  Lago  Vista
Police  Department,  where she waited  about fifty minutes  for
the Magistrate.  It  is undisputed  that  no physical  force  or
threats  of physical  force were used against  Petitioner  Atwater
at  any  time  during  this  incident.  The  Magistrate  read
Petitioner  Atwater  her  rights,  and she was released  after
posting  bond.

2

3

It is undisputed  that  Officer  Turek  had  probable  cause
to  believe  that  Petitioner  Atwater  committed  five  criminal
offenses.  Petitioner  Atwater’s  arrest  was  effected  in a public
place,  and  without  the  use  of excessive  physical  force.  She
was  subjected  to  ordinary  booking  procedures,  and  was
released  within  an  hour.

Il.  The  Proceedings  Below.

On  August  8,  1997,  Petitioners  commenced  an  action
against  Respondents  in the  98th  Judicial  District  Court  of
Travis  County,  Texas,  alleging  a multitude  of constitutional
and  State  law  claims.  Respondents  properly  removed  the
litigation  to the United  States  District  Court  for the Western
~
District  of Texas  at Austin.

On  February  13,  1998,  the  District  Court  granted
Respondent’s  Summary  Judgment  Motion,  wholly
dismissing  Respondents’  claims.  Petitioners  filed  Notice  of
Appeal  to  the  United  States  Court  of Appeals  for the  Fifth
Circuit,  and  a  three-judge  panel  issued  their  opinion  on
January  29,  1999.

The  Panel  reversed  summary  judgment  in  favor  of
Officer  Turek  and  the  City  of Lago  Vista  on  Petitioners’
Fourth  Amendment  unreasonable  seizure  claim,  and
remanded  that  issue  to  District  Court.  The  Panel  upheld
summary  judgment  in  favor  of Respondents  on  all  other
constitutional
and state law claims.  Summary  judgment  was
affirmed  in favor  of Police  Chief Miller  on all grounds.

The  Fifth Circuit  granted  rehearing  en banc,  and vacated
the panel’s  decision.  After briefing  and oral argument, ten

appellate  justices  held  that  Petitioner  Atwater’s  custodial
arrest  did  not  violate  her  Fourth  Amendment  rights,  and
affirmed  the District  Court’s  summary judgment.

REASONS  FOR  DENYING  WRIT

Petitioners  ask this Court to grant a Writ of Certiorari  in
order  to determine  if the Fourth  Amendment  limits  the use
of custodial  arrests  for fine-only  traffic  offenses.  However,
this question  has already been resolved  by the United  States
Supreme  Court.  All  custodial  arrests,  including  those  for
fine-only  traffic  offenses,  must  be reasonable.  An  arrest  is
reasonable if the  officer  had probable  cause  to arrest.

It is undisputed  that  Officer  Turek  had  probable  cause
to arrest  Petitioner  Atwater.  The arrest,  therefore,  complied
with the requirements  of the Fourth  Amendment. The holding
of the  United  States  Court  of Appeals  for the Fifth  Circuit
recognized  and  considered  Fourth  Amendment  limits  on
Petitioner  Atwater’s  arrest.  Thus,  the  Fourth  Amendment
question presented  by Petitioners  does  not  merit  additional
review.

Petitioners  argue that the arrest  was unreasonable  and
violated  the  Fourth  Amendment.  They  demand  additional
restrictions,  beyond probable  cause, to justify an arrest for a
fine-only  traffic  offense.  Only  in  “extraordinary
circumstances”  has the United States Supreme  Court imposed
a heightened  standard  for  reviewing  an  arrest.  Those
circumstances
are not present  here. Further,
the United  States
Supreme  Court, and the federal  Courts of Appeals,  have
rejected  all  attempts  to  create  a  probable  cause  “plus”
standard  for ordinary  arrests.  The Fifth Circuit  opinion is
consistent  with all previous  case law, and correctly  states

4

5

the  law.  Petitioner  Atwater’s  custodial  arrest  complied  with
the  Fourth  Amendment.  As a result,  the Petition  for Writ  of
Certiorari  should  be denied.

I.  THE  FOURTH  AMENDMENT  CONTROLS  ALL

ARRESTS

The  “Question  Presented”  in  the  Petition  for  Writ  of
Certiorari  controls  the issue  raised  for review  by the United
States  Supreme Court.  Petitioners  ask this Court to determine
if custodial  arrests  are  limited  by the Fourth  Amendment  for
fine-only  traffic  offenses.  There  is no dispute that the Fourth
Amendment  limits the use of custodial  arrests  for all offenses,
including  fine-only  traffic  offenses.

The  Constitution  requires  that  all arrests  be reasonable.
U.S.  Const.  Amendment  IV. An  arrest  is reasonable  if it is
made  with  probable  cause.  United  States  v. Robinson,  414
U.S.  218,  235  (1973);  Michigan  v.  DeFillippo,  443  U.S.  31,
36 (1979).

The United  States  Court  of Appeals  for the Fifth  Circuit
found  that  Petitioner  Atwater’s  arrest  complied  with  the
Fourth  Amendment  limitations. The arrest was constitutional,
because  it was  made  with  probable  cause.  If the  Court
answers  Petitioners’  question  in the  affirmative,  the  Fifth
Circuit’s  holding  still  stands.  As  a  result,  the  question
presented  by Petitioners  does  not  merit  United  States
Supreme  Court  review.

II.  FIFTH  CIRCUIT  OPINION  IS  CONSISTENT

WITH  THIS  COURT’S  OPINIONS

The  Fourth Amendment  requires  that  all  arrests  be
reasonable.  Petitioners  argue  that  the Fifth  Circuit  erred  by
failing  to require  more  than  probable  cause  in order  to
determine the reasonableness  of her arrest.  The Fifth Circuit
required  probabie  cause  for  an  ordinary  arrest.  This  is
consistent  with this Court’s  requirements.

A.  -~ Arrest  is Reasonable  if It is Made  with  Probable

ause.

In  its  history  of evaluating  arrests,  the  United  States
Supreme  Court has recognized  that probable  cause justifies
the arrest and ordinary processing of the suspected offender.
Gerstein  v.  Pugh,  420 U.S.  103,  113-14  (1975).

Under  this  practical  compromise,  a policeman’s
on-the-scene  assessment  of probable  cause
provides  legal justification  for arresting  a person
suspected  of crime,  and  for  a  brief  period  of
detention  to take the administrative  steps incident
to arrest.

Id. The  probable  cause  test  accommodates  an  individual’s
right to liberty and the State’s duty to control  crime. Brinegar
v.  United  States,  338  U.S.  160,  176  (1949);  Gerstein,  420
U.S.  at  112.  Probable  cause  is the measure  of a reasonable
arrest  under  ordinary  circumstances.

6

Officer  Turek  had  probable  cause  to  believe  that
Petitioner  Atwater  committed  five  criminal  offenses.'  She
failed  to  seatbelt  herself,  or  her  two  young  children.  She
failed  to  carry  with  her  proof of insurance  or  her  driver’s
license.  Her  arrest  was  effected  in  a  public  place,  with
probable  cause,  and  without  the  use  of excessive  physical
force.  She  was  subjected  to  ordinary  booking  procedures,
and  was  released  within  an  hour.  Petitioner  Atwater  pled
guilty to all of the seatbelt  charges.  The driver’s  license  and
insurance  charges  were  dropped  when  she  produced  proof
of the documents.

Petitioners  do  not  dispute  the  probable  cause  facts.
Probable  cause  authorized  Officer  Turek  to arrest  Petitioner
Atwater,  and  to temporarily  detain  her  until  she  was  taken
before  a magistrate.  Officer  Turek  did not  violate  Petitioner
Atwater’s  Fourth Amendment  rights. As a result, this Petition
should  be denied.

B.  Consideration  of  Additional  Factors,  Only  Under

-  Extraordinary  Circumstances.

Petitioners  argue  that  the  Fifth  Circuit  erred  in failing
to  consider  extra  factors,  other  than  probable  cause,  to
determine  if the arrest was reasonable.  This Court has already
rejected  consideration  of  additional  restrictions  when
evaluating  the reasonableness  of the ordinary  arrest.  Whren
v.  United  States,  517 U.S.  806, 813  (1996) (refusing  to take
into  account  the subjective  intent  of the officer  or  whether

1.  Appellant  violated  Texas  TRANSPORTATION  CODE  ANN.,
§§  545.413(a),  545.413(b),  521.025,  and  601.051,  601.053
and  601.191.

7

other officers  would  have made  the arrest,  in determining  if
an  arrest  is reasonable).

There  are  only  three  “extraordinary”  circumstances
where  arrests  made with  probable  cause required  further
evaluation  by this Court.  See, id. at 818; Robinson, 414 U.S.
at 325. These “extraordinary”  circumstances  involve  arrests
that are either:  1) made  in a  home;  2) involve  excessive
physical force in effecting the arrest; or 3) go beyond ordinary
booking  procedures  to  effect  a  physical  invasion  of the
arrestee’s  bodily integrity. See, e.g.,  Welsh  v.  Wisconsin, 466
U.S.  740 (1984) (warrantless  entry into home);  Tennessee  v.
Garner,  471  U.S.  1 (1985) (use of deadly force);  Winston  v.
Lee, 470 U.S.  753 (1985) (surgical  removal  of a bullet).  It is
undisputed  that  these  “extraordinary”  circumstances  do not
apply  to the case  at bar.

Consistent  with  this  Court’s  rulings,  the  Fifth  Circuit
correctly  decided  that  Petitioner  Atwater’s  arrest  and  short
detention  did  not  violate  her Fourth  Amendment  rights.  As
a result,  the Petition  for Review  should  be denied.

C.  Knowles  v.  Iowa  does  not  conflict  with  the  Fifth

Circuit  opinion.

In 1999, this Court held that an officer  may not  perform
a  full search of an automobile  after the officer has issued the
driver a  citation.  Knowles  v.  Iowa,  525  U.S.  113  (1999).
Petitioner  complains  that  the  Fifth  Circuit  opinion
“eviscerates”  the Knowles opinion.  However,  this
laint
is unfounded.

2

Py

_
Towa state  law  authorized  officers  to  either  issue  a
citation, or to take traffic offenders  into custody.  In Knowles,

the  Iowa  Supreme  Court  upheld  the  constitutionality  of an
officer  issuing  a  citation  and  still  performing  a  custodial
arrest-type  searches  of the driver  and  automobile.  Knowles,
569  N.W.2d  601  (Iowa  1997).  The  United  States  Supreme
Court reversed the lowa Supreme Court.  It is unconstitutional
to perform a custodial  arrest-type  search  if the driver  is not
subjected  to a custodial  arrest.  Knowles,  525 U.S.  at 487-88.

Knowles  is a  search  case,  holding  that  police  officers
must  actually  arrest  the  driver  in order  to  perform a post-
arrest  search.  The  Knowles  Court  neither  addressed  nor
attempted  to restrict  the authority of police officers  to effect
the custodial  arrest  of traffic  offenders.  In the  instant  case,
the Fifth  Circuit  opinion does not address  the same  issues.  It
does not conflict  with the Knowles  decision,  or with any other
holding  from  this  Court.

Ill.  FIFTH  CIRCUIT  OPINION  IS  CONSISTENT
WITH  OPINIONS  OF  STATE  AND  FEDERAL
COURTS

The  United  States  Supreme  Court  never  required  more
than  probable  cause  to  establish  the  constitutionality  of a
public  arrest.  Whren,  517  U.S.  at  813.  The  precedent  from
other federal  and state courts  is consistent  with this approach.
Every  Court  addressing  the  question  upheld  the  federal
constitutionality  of public  custodial  arrests  made  with
probable  cause  and  in  compliance  with  state  law.  These
Courts  refused  to impose  additional  limitations  on  the right
to publicly  arrest  certain  criminal  offenders.

A.  Consistency  Among  the  Federal  Courts  of Appeal.

Federal  Courts  of Appeal  uniformly  reject  all attempts
to require  more than probable  cause  in determining  whether

9

an  ordinary  arrest  constitutes  a civil  rights violation.  These
Courts  declined  to consider  (a) how  serious  the  offense  or
punishment  was;?  (b)  whether  a  citation  could  have  been
issued in lieu of an arrest;’  (c)  whether  state law was complied
with;*  (d) the  subjective  intent  of the  officer  making  the
arrest;>  (¢)  whether  other  officers  would  have  made  the

2.  Ricci  v.  Arlington  Heights,  116  F.3d  288,  290-91  (7* Cir.
1997),  cert.  dism'd,  $23  U.S.  613  (1998);  Fisher  v.  WMATA,  690
F.2d  1133,  1139  (4* Cir.  1982).

3.  Moore  v.  Gwinnett  County,  967  F.2d  1495  (11% Cir.  1992)
cert.  den.  506  U.S.  1081  (1993);  Higbee  v.  City of San  Diego,  911
F.2d  377,  379  (9® Cir.  1990).

4.  See Fields  v.  City of South  Houston,  922  F.2d  1183  (5* Cir.
1991);  Vargas-Badillo  v.  Diaz-Torres,  114  F.3d  3 (1* Cir.  1997);
Pyles v.  Raisor,  60 F.3d  1211,  1215 (6* Cir.  1995); Barry v.  Fowler,
902  F.2d  770,  772-73  (9* Cir.  1990);  Street  v.  Surdyka,  492  F.2d
368,  371-72  (4*  Cir.  1974).  In  each  of these  cases,  the  court
concluded  that  probable  cause  justified  the  warrantless  arrests,
despite  the  violation  of state  laws  and  early  common  law  that
restricted  the arrest of  misdemeanor  offenders  to only offenses  that
were  committed  in the officer’s  presence.

Petitioner’s  reliance on United  States v.  Mota  (982  F.2d  1384
[9* Cir.  1993]})  for the contrary  position  is misplaced.  In Mota, the
Court  addresses  the admissibility  of  evidence  in a  criminal  trial.
The Court explicitly  distinguished  its holding  from claims involving
civil  rights  (such  as  its  holding  in Barry  v.  Fowler,  supra).  Id.  at
1387.  In the context of a § 1983  claim,  the Ninth  Circuit  does not
consider  a violation  of state law as relevant  to determining  if a civil
rights  violation  occurred.  /d.

5.  Holland  v.  City  of Portland,  102  F.3d  6 (1* Cir.  1996);

United  States  v.  Basey,  816 F.2d 980, 990-91  (5* Cir.  1987).

10

11

arrest;®  or  (f)  if the  arrest  complied  with  usual  police
department  policies  or  practices.’

constitutionality  of the  arrest*;  and  whether  arrest  for civil
infractions  is authorized.’

In  each  of  these  cases,  probable  cause  alone  was
sufficient  to establish  the reasonableness  of the arrest.  These
federal  appellate  opinions  are  consistent  with  the  opinion
rendered  by the  Fifth  Circuit  in  this  matter.  There  is no
confusion  among  the  federal  courts  that  justifies  Supreme
Court  intervention.

B.  Consistency  Among  the  State  Courts.

State  courts  have  addressed  a number  of issues  related
to the custodial  arrest  of traffic  offenders.  However,  none  of
these  opinions  control  the question  presented  or  argued  by
the Petitioners.  Petitioners  fail  to cite  even  one  case,  on  the
federal  or  state  level,  holding  that  the  United  States
Constitution  requires  more  than probable  cause  to effect  the
public,  state-authorized  arrest  of a misdemeanor  offender.
As a result,  Supreme  Court  intervention  in this matter  is not
necessary  to resolve  conflict  or  inconsistencies.

State  courts  uphold  the  custodial  arrests  of fine-only
misdemeanor  offenders.  See,  e.g.,  [llinois  v.  Ramirez,  518
N.E.2d  638  (Ill. App. —  1* Dist.  1993).  These  courts  consider
whether  a  violation  of  state  law  should  effect  the

6.  Whren,  517  U.S.  at 813-4;  Moore  v.  Gwinnett  County,  967
F.2d  1495,  1498  (11® Cir.  1992),  cert.  den.,  506 U.S.  1081  (1993).

7.  United  States  of America  v.  Trigg,  878  F.2d  1037,  1041

(7* Cir.  1989),  cert.  den.,  502  U.S.  963  (1991).

For instance,  the Washington  Supreme Court  found that
state public  policy  dictated  that the custodial  arrest of certain
misdemeanor  offenders  was  inappropriate.  Washington  v.
Hehman,  578  P.2d  527,  529  (Wash.  1978).  The  Court
explicitly  acknowledged  that  the  United  States  Supreme
Court  never  required  such  a holding.  It noted that states  were
entitled  to provide  criminal  defendants  greater  rights  than
the minimum  standard  required  by the Constitution.  /d.

State  court  decisions  reflect  the  diversity  with  which
various  states  establish  and enforce  their laws.  It is uniquely
within  these  bodies’  power  and authority  to establish  laws
and dictate  the mode  of their enforcement  and punishment.
Contrary  to Petitioners’  assertion,  these opinions  do not
reflect  “confusion”  in  the  lower  courts  about  the
constitutional  standard.  Rather  they  reflect  differing
to enforcement  of their state’s laws.  The  federal
approaches
constitutional  standard  for a  public  arrest  that is  made  in
compliance  with  state law is not questioned  by these  Courts;
probable  cause  is enough.

8.  See, e.g.,  Thomas  v.  State,  614  So.2d  468, 471  (Fla.  1993).

9.  See,  e.g.,  Barnett  v.  United  States,  525  A.2d  197,  198-99
(D.C.  1987)  (noting  that  the  arrest  would  only  be  justified  if
complainant  committed  a  criminal  infraction,  as opposed  to a
civil  one).

12

13

IV.  FIFTH  CIRCUIT  OPINION  IS  CONSISTENT

WITH  EARLY  AMERICAN  JURISPRUDENCE

Petitioners  claim  that  the  common  law  dictates  a
different  result  in  this  matter.  Petitioners  note  that,  in
England,  common  law  provided  that  an  officer  only  had
authority  to arrest  for misdemeanors  that constituted  a breach
of peace,  and were  committed  in their presence.  As noted by
the Fifth  Circuit,  Petitioners  waived  this claim, by failing to
raise  it  until  this  case was reheard  by that  Court  en banc.
Morever,  the Fifth  Circuit’s  holding  is consistent  with early
American  jurisprudence  authorizing  arrests  for  all
misdemeanors  committed  in the officer’s  presence.

A.  Breach  of Peace  Claim  was  Waived.

It is  a well-recognized  appellate  principle  that  issues  or
questions  that  are  not  raised  to the trial  court,  are  not
considered  in the  reviewing  court.  See,  e.g.,  Becker  Steel
Co.  v.  Cummings,  296 U.S.  74, 82 (1935); Stealgald v.  United
States,  451  U.S.  204,  208  (1981).  Petitioners  argue  that  the
Fifth  Circuit  erred  by  failed  to  consider  that  Petitioner
Atwater’s  offenses  did not constitute  breaches  of the peace.
Petitioners did not raise  this  common  law argument  in their
pleadings,  Summary  Judgment  Response,  or  Motion  for
Rehearing  that  was presented  to the trial court.  It  was not
raised  in the Appellate Brief, or in Reply to Appellee’s  Brief.
It is first  relied  on  by Petitioners  in the  Appellants’  Brief
filed  before  Fifth Circuit en banc consideration
of this matter.
Petitioners  want this  Court  to review  a question  on grounds
that the trial court was not given an opportunity  to consider,
Petitioners  waived  their complaint.

is Consistent.
B.  Early  American  Jurisprudence

Common  law  does  not  dictate  a different  result  in this
matter.  Old English  common  law allowed  officers  to arrest
offenders  committing  misdemeanors  if  the  offense
constituted  a breach  of the peace,  and was committed  in their
presence.  However,  the English  and American  legislatures
have  modified  this  limitation  for over  one  hundred  years.
See,  e.g.,  Burroughs  v.  Eastman,  59  N.W.  817,  818-820
(Mich.  1894),  and  cases  cited  therein.  This  1894  court  in
Burroughs  cites  numerous  courts  that  already  approved  of
legislation  authorizing  police  power  to arrest  on view  for
offenses  that  did not necessarily  constitute  a  breach  of the
peace.

Moreover,  this  Old English  arrest  requirement  is ignored
in  previous  Supreme  Court  decisions  as a probable  cause
for public  arrests. See,  e.g.,  Carroll  v.  United  States,  _
standard
267  U.S.  132,  156-67 €1925);  United States ¥.  Watson,  473
U.S.  411,  418  (1976).  In  1976,  the  United  States  Supreme
Court  relied  upon  the  proposed  Model  Code  of  Pre-
Arraignment  Procedure.  The  Code  permitted  warrantless
arrests  when  misdemeanors  were  committed  in the officer’s
presence,  regardless  of  whether  the offense  constituted  a
breach  of  the  peace.  § 120.1,  Mopet  Cope  or  Pre-
ARRAIGNMENT  PROCEDURE. '°

Early  Anferican  jurisprudence  has long  accepted  the
legislature’s  power  to modify  the old English  common  law

law argument  by failing  to raise  it in a timely  manner.  As a
resujt,  the  Fifth  Circuit  correctly  sustained  summary
judgment,  and the Petition  should  be denied.

10.  See also,  18 U.S.C.A.  § 3052  (since  1951,  giving  F.B.1.
agents  authority  to arrest  for any offense  against  the United  States
in their presence).
committed

15

Respectfully  submitted,

WituiaM  W.  Kruecer,  III
Counsel  of Record
JOANNA  R. LipPMAN
FLETCHER  & SPRINGER
Attorneys for Respondents
823  Congress  Avenue
Suite  510
Austin  Texas  78701
(512) 476-5300

14

CONCLUSION

Petitioner  Atwater  committed  five  violations  of Texas
law.  In accordance  with  his  authority,  Respondent/Officer
Turek  arrested  Petitioner  Atwater,  and  briefly  detained  her
until  she  was  released  by a  magistrate.  The  Fifth  Circuit
correctly  held  that  Petitioner  Atwater’s  Fourth  Amendment
rights  were  not  violated  by this arrest.

Review  on  a writ of certiorari  is a matter  of this Court’s
judicial  discretion.  The  Fourth  Amendment  question
presented  does  not  reflect  a vital  question that justifies  this
Court’s  review.  Obviously,  the  Fourth  Amendments  limits
the  use  of custodial  arrests  for fine-only  traffic  offenses,  as
it does for all offenses.  This  Court has long recognized that
the  existence  of probable  cause  ensures  that  the  State’s
interest  in effecting the arrest  is balanced by the individual’s
right of liberty. The Fifth Circuit’s  opinion is consistent  with
this  Court’s  holdings  regarding  the same.

Moreover,  the  Fifth  Circuit  opinion  does  not  conflict
with the decisions  of other United  States Courts  of Appeals,
or that of the state courts.  In fact, Petitioners
fail to cite even
one  Court  that  required  more  than  probable  cause  to
determine  the constitutionality  of an  ordinary,  public  arrest
of  a  misdemeanor  offender.  Further,  early  American
jurisprudence  has  long  recognized  that  the  common  law
breach  of peace  requirement  could be abrogated  by legislative
action.

An officer’s  right to arrest  when there  is probable  cause
is clearly  established  by this Court,  and the lower courts.
The  Fifth Circuit  opinion  is consistent  with these decisions,
and the Question  Presented  does  not merit further  review.
As a result,  the  Petition  for  Writ  of Certiorari  should
be denied.

ACLU  of  Texas,  Inc.  hereby  respectfully  moves  for
leave  to  file  the  attached  brief  amicus  curiae  in  this  case.
The  consent  of  the  attorney  for  the  petitioner  has  been
obtained.  The  consent  of  the  attorney  for  the  respondent
was  requested  but  refused.

The  interest  of  the  ACLU  of  Texas,  Inc.  in  this  case
arises  from  the  fact  that,  in  addition  to  our  institutional
concern  with  the  Court’s  interpretation  of  fundamental
liberties,  many  of  our  clients  and  those  simply  making
inquiries  about  possible  civil  rights  violations  have
reported  similar  instances  to  the  facts  in  this  case.  The
resolution  of  this  case  will  have  a  direct  impact  on  the
people  we  seek  to  serve.

The  amicus  curiae  is requesting  to  file  a  brief  that  will
discuss  the  broader  policy  implications  of  allowing  the
current  ruling  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  to  stand  and  to
draw  the  court’s  attention  to  general  facts  beyond  the
instant  case.

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE!

The  American  Civil  Liberties  Union  of Texas  (ACLU-
TX)  is  a  non-profit  membership  organization  incorpo-
rated  in the  State  of Texas  with  approximately  8,000  mem-
bers.  It  is  affiliated  with  the  American  Civil  Liberties
Union  headquartered  in  New  York,  NY  and  through  the
national  organization  with  53 other  ACLU  affiliates  in the
United  States  with  a  total  membership  of  275,000  mem-
bers.  The  ACLU-TX  is a  membership  organization  with  a
501(c)(4)  tax  status.  Membership  dues  to  the  ACLU  are
not  tax  exempt.  It is affiliated  with  the  ACLU  Foundation
of  Texas  (Foundation)  which  is  a  tax  exempt  501(c)(3)
organization.

The  ACLU-TX  is  dedicated  to  defending  the  princi-
ples of liberty  and  equality  embodied  in the  Bill  of Rights.
The  ACLU-TX  provides  legal  services  through  cooperat-
ing attorneys  acting  on  a  pro bono  basis  to persons  whose
liberties  have  been  violated,  as  well  as  educating  the
public  on  matters  involving  civil  liberties  and  civil  rights.
The  ACLU-TX  is primarily  involved  in  “impact”  litigation
to  correct  systemic  violations  of  civil  liberties.

The  ACLU-TX  has  specific  and  direct  interest  in  this
litigation  not  only  on  behalf  of  its  membership  but  also
on  behalf  of  the  significant  number  of  people  who  seek
ACLU  assistance  involving  situations  that  present  facts
not  unlike  the  instant  case.  In  the  course  of  a  year  the

!  This  brief  is  made  upon  motion.  Pursuant  to  Rule  37.6
counsel  for  amicus  state  that  no  counsel  for  a  party  authored
this  brief  in  whole  or  in  part  and  no  person  other  than  amicus
made  a monetary  contribution  to  the  preparation  or  submission
of this  brief.

2

ACLU-IX  receives  nearly  6,000  requests  for  assistance,  a
significant  portion  of  which  involve  questions  of  alleged
police  misconduct.  The  interest  of  the  people  that  the
ACLU-TX  represents  and  counsels  is  directly  and
adversely  affected  by  the  decision  below.

S

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

The  common  thread  of  American  political  theory  is
that  government  is, or  ought  to  be,  limited  and  restrained
from  unreasonably  imposing  restraints  upon  its  citizens’
liberties.  The  entire  body  of  law  relating  to  individual
liberties  seeks  to  find  the  balance  between  what  is neces-
sary  for  the  proper  functioning  of  society  and  the  maxi-
mum  freedom  possible  for  the  individual.

This  notion  is  tested  daily  in  applying  the  Fourth
Amendment's  prohibition  against  unreasonable  seizures
of  person  or  property.  That  government  necessarily  must
sometimes  intrude  on  our  lives  does  not  mean  that  we
grant  carte  blanche  approval  of unreasonable  actions.  We
have  traditionally  given  the  greatest  weight  to  protecting
public  and  private  intrusions  into  the  home  under  the
general  “right  to  be  left  alone.”  But  that  right  extends,
albeit  to  a  lesser  degree,  beyond  the  portals  of the  home.
Unreasonable  and  unjustified  detention  of  the  person  by
police  violates  this  basic  right.

The  Fourth  Amendment  frees  us  from  unreasonable
searches  and  seizures.  It  should  be  our  protection  from
abusive  and  harassing  governmental  conduct.  It ought  to
shield  a  woman  driving  with  two  small  children  from
fear  that  a  minor  traffic  infraction  will  result  in  her  being

booked  as  a  criminal  and  confined  in jail until  she  can  be
presented  to  a  magistrate.

That  police  feel  free  to  behave  the  way  Officer  Turek
did  in  the  instant  case  is  the  result  of  police  everywhere
believing  that  this  Court  sanctions  such  practices.  The
line  of cases  that  permit  searches  incident  to  an  arrest  for
a  minor  traffic  infraction  has  led  both  the  police  and  the
majority  of the  Fifth  Circuit  to believe  that  it is acceptable
for  the  police  to  treat  a  minor  traffic  offense  as  a  more
serious  crime  depending  upon  the  whim  of the  arresting
officer.

Because  the  answer  to  the  question  of whether  or  not
the  police  can  incarcerate  a  person  for  a  minor  infraction
is  critical  to  every  person  in  this  country,  this  Court
should  grant  certiorari.

+

ARGUMENT

I.  The  facts  of  this  case  involve  such  an  important
Constitutional  question  of overarching  national  sig-
nificance  that  the  Court  should  grant  certiorari  to  set
national  standards.

Without  question,  the  most  significant  and  frequent
contact  the  average  American  has  with  the  police  is being
stopped  for  a  traffic  infraction.  It  would  be  difficult  to
find  anyone,  other  than  a  neophyte  driver,  who  has  not
been  stopped  and  given a citation  for some  traffic  offense.
The  ramifications  of the  ruling  in  this  case  will  be  felt  by
millions  of people  in  their  ordinary  daily  life.  If allowed  —
to  stand,  the  ruling  below  signals  an  open  season  on

motorists  and  others  who,  for  whatever  reason,  displease
a  police  officer.

A.

It is unreasonable  to  permit  police  to  put  some-
one  in  jail  pending  a  hearing  for  an  offense  for
which  no  jail  time  can  be  imposed.

We  require  police  to  articulate  reasons  giving  rise  to
probable  cause  prior  to  making  an  arrest  or  conducting  a
warrantless  search.  These  decisions  are  subject  to  review
by  a  judicial  officer.  We  require  a  magistrate  to  have
reasons  rising  to  probable  cause  before  issuing  a  search
or  arrest  warrant,  and  the  law  is  well-settled  that  the
issuing  magistrate  may  not  simply  rubber  stamp  the  deci-
sion  of  the  police.  Aguilar  v.  Texas,  378  U.S.  108,  111
(1964).  If  we  were  not  concerned  that  an  overzealous
officer  might  abuse  his  office,  the  law  would  be  other-
wise.  It  is  a  reasonable  and  proper  concern.

How  is it, then,  that  the  law  can  allow a police  officer
to  determine  that  a  person  should  go  to  jail when  the  law
allows  neither  judge  nor  jury,  under  any  circumstances,
to  incarcerate  that  person?  To  be  sure,  the  magistrate  will
release  a  person  so  detained.  There  is  simply  no  other
option.  But  that  does  not  lessen  the  humiliation  of  being
handcuffed,  fingerprinted,  and  photographed,  and
spending  as  much  as  two  days  in jail.  Nor  will  the  magis-
trate’s  release  make  up  for  lost  work  or  a  lost  holiday.

In  the  case  before  the  Court,  Ms.  Atwater  was,  in
some  respects,  fortunate.  She  was  near  enough  to  her
home  that  a  neighbor  saw  what  was  happening  and  was
able  to  take  her  terrified  children  for  her.  She  was  also
fortunate  to  only  spend  an  hour  in  jail.  Circumstances

could  easily  have  been  different.  She  could  have  been
traveling  across  country  with  no  friendly  neighbor  in
sight.  In  many  small  towns  across  this  state,  she  may
have  languished  up  to  forty-eight  hours  in  jail  before
being  released  by  a  magistrate.

This  Court  has  often  written  about  the  “right  to  be
left  alone”  in  the  context  of  the  privacy  of  one’s  own
home.  FCC  v.  Pacifica,  438  U.S.  726,  748  (1978);  Telecommu-

nications  Consortium  v.  FCC,  518  U.S.  727,  804  (1996);
Bolger  v.  Young  Drug  Products  Corp.,  463  U.S.  60,  77  (1983)
(Rehnquist,  O’Connor  concurring);  Carey  v.  Brown,  447
U.S.  455,  477  (1980)  (Rehnquist  dissenting,  joined  by
Chief  Justice  and  Blackmun).  These  cases  accord  great
weight  to  the  privacy  of  the  home  as  a  place  free  from
unwanted  intrusion.  To  be  sure,  the  home  is  to  be
accorded  the  highest  weight.  But  is  it  the  only  place
where  one  has a right  to  be  left  alone?

No  one  would  argue  that  a  person  guilty  of  a  traffic
infraction  has  a  “right  to  be  left  alone”  by a  police  officer.
This  justified  intrusion  should  not  give  rise  to  the  physi-
cal  incapacitation  and  detention  of  a  person.  Surely,  in  a
free  and  just  society,  one  has  the  right  to  expect  one  will
not  be  imprisoned  simply  because a tail  light  is  out  or  a
seatbelt  was  not  buckled.

It  is  reasonable  to  expect  to  be  left  alone  by  police
when  one’s  conduct  comports  with  the  law.  But  even
when  a  person’s  conduct  does  give  rise  to  a  police  stop,  it
is  reasonable  to  expect  the  officer  will  not  be  allowed  to
incarcerate  that  person  for  an  offense  that  does  not  per-
mit  incarceration.

B.  The  purpose  of  the  Fourth  Amendment  is  to
protect  all  people  from  unreasonable  police
behavior  and  free  them  from  abuse  and  the  fear
of  abuse.

The  expansion  of  the  automobile  exception  to  the
Fourth  Amendment  is  well  documented  in  the  amicus
brief  of the  Texas  Criminal  Defense  Lawyers’  Association.
“The  recognition  of exceptions  to  great  principles  always
creates,  of  course,  the  hazard  that  the  exceptions  will
devour  the  rule.”  Ker  v.  California,  374  U.S.  23,  61  (1963)
(Brennan,  separate  opinion  joined  by  The  Chief  Justice,
Douglas,  and  Goldberg).

The  blessings  of  liberty  we  enjoy,  including  freedom
from  arbitrary  and  obtrusive  governmental  action,  is due
to  a  vigilant  judiciary  jealously  protective  of  our  funda-
mental  freedoms.  As  was  noted  by Justice  Harlan;

Though  the  preceding  in  question  is divested  of
many  of  the  aggravating  incidents  of  actual
search  and  seizure,  yet,  as  before  said,  it  con-
tains  their  substance  and  essence,  and  affects
their  substantial  purpose.  It  may  be  said  that  it
is  the  obnoxious  thing  in  its  mildest  and  least
repulsive  form;  but  illegitimate  and  unconstitu-
tional  practices  get  their  first  footing  in  that
way,  namely,  by  silent  approaches  and  slight
deviations  from  legal  modes  of  procedure.  This
can  only  be  obviated  by  adhering  to  the  rule
that  constitutional  provisions  for  the  security  of
person  and  property  should  be  liberally  con-
strued.  A close  and  literal  construction  deprives
them  of half  their  efficacy,  and  leads  to  gradual
depreciation  of  the  right,  as  if it consisted  more
in sound  than  in substance.  Boyd  v.  United  States,
116  U.S.  616,  635  (1886).

If  allowed  to  stand,  the  decision  below  will  be  a
precedent  that  will  eviscerate  the  protection  that  stands
between  the  ordinary  citizen  and  often  overzealous  con-
stabulary.  As  the  Father  of  the  Constitution  noted:

[I]t  is  proper  to  take  alarm  at  the  first  experi-
ment  on  our  liberties.  We  hold  this  prudent
jealousy  to  be  the  first  duty  of Citizens,  and  one
of  the  noblest  characteristics  of  the  late  Revolu-
tion.  The  free  men  of  America  did  not  wait  till
usurped  power  had  strengthened  itself  by exer-
cise,  and  entangled  the  question  in  precedents.
They  saw  all  the  consequences  in  the  principle,
and  they  avoided  the  consequences  by denying
the  principle.  We  revere  this  lesson  too  much
soon  to  forget  it.  Memorial  and  Remonstrance,
James  Madison,  June  20,  1785.

‘

We  are  now  faced  with  the  “usurped  power”  Mad-
ison  warned  against.  We  are  faced  with  the  situation  in
which  the  judicial  efforts  to  accommodate  the  needs  of
law  enforcement  has  accommodated  instead  the  vengeful
officer.  Who,  now,  can  be  free  of  fear  of  full  custodial
arrest  for  the  slightest  infraction?

If the  Court  of Appeals  is correct  in  its  interpretation
of  this  Court's  rulings,  then  stepping  off  a  curb  to  hail  a
cab  can  lead  to  more  than  a  desk  appearance  ticket.  It
may  be  prelude  to  the  humiliation  of being  fingerprinted,
booked  and  incarcerated.  Of  course,  it is highly  doubtful
that  this  will  often  occur  to  people  with  some  status  in
society.  Rarely  does  abusive  police  conduct  target  such
people.  It will  be  primarily  used  on  the  poor,  the  young,
and  minorities.

The  recent  —  or  rather  recently  noted  —-  phenomena,  of
“driving  while  black”  has  lead  to  a  spate  of  lawsuits
providing  more  insight  into  police  behavior.  In  a  survey
conducted  by John  Lambert,  Ph.D.  for  just  such a suit,  it
was  shown  that  in  forty-two  hours  of  observations  on
I-95  in  Maryland,  93%  of the  5,741  observed  drivers  were
violating  some  traffic  law,  thus  subject  to  arrest.  17.5%  of
the  observed  drivers  were  African-American.  Yet  the
records  of  the  Maryland  State  Police  (MSP)  showed  that
of  motorists  stopped  for  traffic  infractions  and  searched
along  this  corridor,  72%  were  black.  It  should  also  be
noted  that  contraband  was  recovered  in  only  28.4%  of
black  motorists  searched,  and  28.8%  of  white  motorists?
disproving  any  validity  of the  common  practice  of  racial
profiling.

These  statistics  are  really  not  startling.  Many  people
expected  that  any  survey  would  turn  up  such  results.%

2  John  Lamberth,  Report  of John  Lamberth,  Ph.D.,  Nov.  8,
1996.  Expert  witness  report  for  plaintiff  in  Wilkins  v.  Maryland
State  Police,  Civil  Action  No.  CCB-93-483,  U.S.  District  Court  for
the  District  of  Maryland.  See  also,  plaintiff’s  brief  in  Maryland
State  Conference  of NAACP  Branches,  et al.  v.  Maryland  State  Police
Civil  Action  No.  CCB-98-1098.  U.S.  District  Court  for the  District
of Maryland  (Northern  Division)  Dr.  Lamberth  is the  immediate
past  Chairman  of  the  Department  of  Psychology  and  the  past
Director  of  the  Division  of  Social  Psychology  at  Temple
University  in  Philadelphia.

3  “Annual  poll  Shows  That  Most  People  Believe  Racial
Profiling  by Cops  Is Common”,  Jet Magazine,  January  10,  2000.
Citing  a  then-recent  Gallup  poll,  the  article  noted  that  “more
than  40%  of Blacks  of all ages  and both  genders  said  they believe
they’ve  been  stopped  because  of their  race”,  “and  three-fourths
of young  Black  men  say  they believe  they have  been  pulled  over
by police  just  because  they  were  Black.”

The  point,  unfortunately,  is  that  we  can  expect  many
police  officers  to  treat  people  differently  for  the  wrong
reasons.  We  can  expect  some  police  officers  with  a  grudge
to  improperly  use  their  authority.  The  possibilities  are
really  limitless.  It  could  be  the  harassment  of  a  fellow
officer’s  ex-spouse  or  campaign  workers  of an  incumbent
sheriff's  opponent  or  merely  someone  who  is  a  thorn  in
the  side  of  the  local  city  council.  In  fact,  it could  simply
be  that  the  officer  got  up  on  the  wrong  side  of  the  bed.
Since  this  court  has  already  indicated  that  it  will  not  go
into  the  underlying  reasons  for  an  otherwise  valid  police
stop,  even  personal  animosity  is  not  subject  to  question.

Abusive  police  behavior  has  been  and  will  always  be
with  us,  but  until  this  decision,  it has  not  had  the  judicial
stamp  of  approval.  This  Court  is  in  its  usual  position  of
being  the  last  clear  chance  to  contain  abusive  police
behavior.  The  Fifth  Circuit’s  opinion  leaves  us  with  a
fundamental  right  that  “consist[s]  more  in  sound  than  in
substance.”

C.  The  correction  of  a  misapplied  or  misun-
derstood  Constitutional  standard  that  has  a  far
reaching  effect  is an  appropriate  reason  for  this
Court  to  grant  certiorari.

This  Court  has  long  held  that  the  misapplication  of
federal  standards  by a  lower  court  is  a  valid  reason  for
granting  certiorari.  Universal  Camera  Corp.  v.  Labor  Board,
340  U.S.  474,  491  (1951);  Mobil  Oil  Corp.  v.  Federal  Power

10

11

Commission,  417  U.S.  283,  310  (1974),  reviewing  misap-
plied  federal  regulatory  standards  even  though  primary
responsibility  rested  with  circuit  courts  of  appeals.

It  is  also  recognized  by the  Court  that  it  will  take  a
close  look  at  the  totality  of the  facts  and  circumstances  in
a  case  involving  a  potential  violation  of  a  fundamental
liberty  interest.

The  Constitution  has  imposed  upon  this  Court
final  authority  to  determine  the  meaning  and
application  of  those  words  of  that  instrument
which  require  interpretation  to  resolve  judicial
issues.  With  that  responsibility,  we  are  com-
pelled  to  examine  for  ourselves  the  statements
in  issue  and  the  circumstances  under  which  they
were  made  to  see  whether  or  not  they do carry  a
threat  of clear  and  present  danger  to  the  impar-
tiality  and  good  order  of  the  courts  or  whether
they  are  of  a  character  which  the  principles  of
the  First  Amendment,  as  adopted  by  the  Due
Process  Clause  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.

Pennekamp  v.  Florida,  328  U.S.  331,  335  (1948).

The  en  banc  opinion  does  not  fairly  report  or  do
justice  to  the  facts  of  this  case.  The  complete  facts  are
found  in  the  dissents  to  this  decision:  Atwater  v.  City  of
Lago  Vista,  195  F.3d  242,  246  et  seq.  (Sth  Cir.1999)  and  in
the  panel  opinion,  Atwater  v.  City  of Lago  Vista,  165  F.3d
380  (5th  Cir.1999).  A  fair  reading  of  the  facts  support
neither  a  routine  traffic  stop  nor  a  pretex)  stop  to  ferret
out  crime.  They  support  a  vindictive  officer  using  his
authority  to  harass  and  intimidate  a  young  mother  and
her  children.  The  en  banc  decision  straitjackets  the  facts  to
make  them  fit  a  narrow  mold.

This  Court's  long-established  recognition  that
standards  of  reasonableness  under  the  Fourth
Amendment  are  not  susceptible  of  Procrustean
application.  .

.

.

Ker,  374  US.at  23.

This  Court  has  viewed  significant  splits  in  en  banc
decisions  on  weighty  federal  questions  as  a  factor  in
deciding  to  grant  certiorari.  Oi!  Workers  Union  v.  Mobile
Oil  Corp.,  426  U.S.  407,  412  (1976);  Miree  v.  DeKalb,  433
U.S.  25,  27  (1977);  Rinaldi  v.  U.S.,  434  U.S.  22,  25  (1977);
but  see  Ratchford  v.  GayLib,  434  U.S.  1080  (1977)  and  Kimble
v.  McDuffy,  454  U.S.  1110  (1981).  In  this  case  the  Court
should  be  drawn  to  the  adamantine  position  of  the  dis-
sent.  The  instant  case  presents  no  mere  difference  of
interpretation  of facts  based  on  a  mutually  accepted  stan-
dard  of  law.  The  difference  between  the  majority  and
dissenters  is profound  and  fundamental.  It is fully  appro-
priate  for  this  Court  to  settle  the  issue  by  granting  cer-
tiorari.

CONCLUSION

Presenting  this  Court  with  a  critical  Constitutional
question  that  goes  to  the  core  of  our  concept  of  ordered
liberties,  the  instant  case  begs  for  Supreme  Court  review.
Nothing  less  is  at  stake  than  the  question  of  whether  or
not  abusive  police  behavior  is to  find  a  comfortable  nest-
ing  place  as  an  exception  to  the  general  rule  of  rea-
sonableness  found  throughout  the  Fourth  Amendment.
The  ultimate  effect  of  which  will  be  felt  by nearly  every
one  in  our  nation.  The  policy  enunciated  by  the  ruling

12

below  gives  unfettered  discretion  to  police  officers  to
decided  who  among  the  many  they  stop  for  minor  infrac-
tions  will  be  selected  for  full-blown  custodial  arrest  and
detention.  Petitioners’  writ  for  certiorari  should  be
granted  to  rectify  this  error.

MOTION  FOR  LEAVE  TO  FILE  BRIEF
AMICUS  CURIAE  AND  BRIEF  AMICUS  CURIAE

The  Texas  Criminal  Defense  Lawyers  Association
respectfully  moves  for  leave  to  file  the  attached  brief
amicus  curiae  in  this  case.  The  consent  of the  attorney  for
the  petitioner  has  been  obtained.  The  consent  of the  attor-
ney  for  the  respondents  was  requested  but  refused  “at
this  time.”

The  Texas  Criminal  Defense  Lawyers  Association  is
an  organization  of  over  2000  attorneys  across  Texas  who
represent  citizens  accused  of  crimes  in  both  state  and
federal  courts.  TCDLA  is  the  Texas  affiliate  of  the
National  Association  of  Criminal  Defense  Lawyers.  As
such,  TCDLA  takes  an  active  interest  in  important  issues
before  this  Court.

The  instant  case  is  brought  under  42  U.S.C.  § 1983
and  therefore  is  a  civil  case.  The  issue  it raises,  however,
could  have  important  implications  in constitutional  crimi-
nal  procedure.  TCDLA’s  amicus  brief  more  fully
addresses  some  of these  criminal  procedure  implications.

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE*

The  Texas  Criminal  Defense  Lawyers  Association
(TCDLA)  is  a  Texas  nonprofit  corporation  with  a  mem-
bership  of more  than  2000  attorneys  covering  the  State  of
Texas.  TCDLA  was  organized  almost  three  decades  ago
with  the  stated  purposes  of  protecting  and  insuring  by
rule  of  law  those  individual  rights  guaranteed  by  the
Texas  and  Federal  Constitutions  in  criminal  cases,  to  re-
sist  efforts  to  curtail  such  rights,  to  encourage  coopera-
tion  between  lawyers  engaged  in  the  defense  of  citizens
accused  of  crimes  through  educational  programs  and
other  assistance,  and  through  such  cooperation,  educa-
tion  and  assistance  to  promote  justice  and  the  common
good.

STATEMENT

Amicus  adopts  petitioner’s  statement  of  the  case.

+

*  Pursuant  to  Rule  37.6,  counsel  for  amicus  states  that  no
counsel  for a party  authored  this  brief  in whole  or  in part and  no
person,  other  than  amicus,  its  members,  or  its  counsel  made  a
monetary  contribution  to  the  preparation  or  submission  of this
brief.

eT

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

This  Court  has  before  it an  opportunity  to  review  the
reasonableness  under  the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth  Amend-
ments  of  custodial  arrests  for  minor  traffic  offenses  that
do  not  rise  to  the  level  of breaches  of  the  peace.  Through
the  evolution  of the  law  surrounding  searches  incident  to
arrest,  we  have  reached  a  point  where  a  police  officer
may  pull  a  person  over  for a traffic  violation  and  imme-
diately  conduct  a  full  search  of  his  automobile.  The  rea-
son  for  this  is  that,  because  the  officer  has  the
discretionary  power  to  arrest  for  the  traffic  violation,  he
has  automatic  “probable  cause  to  arrest”  for  purposes  of
the  search  incident  to  arrest  doctrine.  As  explained  below,
this  is  creating  documented  abuses,  particularly  against
racial  minorities.

It  is  clear  that  through  this  evolution  in  the  law,  the
doctrine  of  cases  such  as  Terry  v.  Ohio,  392  U.S.  1  (1968)
and  Michigan  v.  Long,  463  U.S.  1032  (1983)  has  been  ren-
dered  inapplicable  to  the  vast  majority  of  traffic  stops.
The  solution  is  to  hold  that  custodial  arrests  on  minor
traffic  offenses  not  amounting  to  breaches  of  the  peace
are  unreasonable  seizures  under  the  Fourth  Amendment.
This  will  remove  the  “automatic  probable  cause  to  arrest”
that  accompanies  these  traffic  stops  and  re-establish  the
traditional  requirements  of  reasonable  suspicion  and
probable  cause  enunciated  in  Terry  and  its  progeny.

This  is clearly  an  important  question  of constitutional
law  and  TCDLA  joins  Petitioner  in  requesting  that  this
Court  grant  her  Petition.

ARGUMENT

CUSTODIAL  ARRESTS  FOR  MINOR  TRAFFIC
OFFENSES  NOT  RISING  TO  BREACHES  OF  THE
PEACE  ARE  UNREASONABLE  UNDER  THE  FOURTH
AND  FOURTEENTH  AMENDMENTS

All  too  often  today a traffic  stop  becomes  a  night-
mare  for  some  hapless  driver  who  fails  to  use  a  turn
signal,  fails  to  wear  his  seatbelt  or  dim  his  high  beams,  or
commits  any  number  of other  minor  traffic  offenses.  That
is  because  for  many  drivers,  the  stop  will  not  result  in  a
ticket,  but  in  a  full-scale  search  incident  to  arrest,
whether  he  is  arrested  or  not.

In  Texas  a  resident  may  be  arrested  for  a  myriad  of
moving,  non-moving  and  equipment  violations  hardly
rising  to  the  level  of  breaches  of  the  peace.  See  Tex.
Transp.  Code  Ann.  Subtitle  C (Vernon  1999)  (the  “Rules  of
the  Road”).  The  way  the  law  stands  right  now,  in  Texas
(as  in  many  other  states)  a  police  officer  may,  in  his
discretion,  arrest  a  driver  when  he sees  the  driver  commit
a  traffic  violation  (with  the  exception  of  speeding).  See
Tex.  Transp.  Code  Ann.  § 543.001(Vernon  1999)  (“Any
peace  officer  may  arrest  without  warrant  a  person  found
committing  a  violation  of  this  subtitle.”);  Tex.  Transp.
Code  Ann.  § 543.004(a)(1)  (Vernon  1999)  (must  ticket  for
speeding).  Because  he  instantly  has  probable  cause  to
arrest  upon  witnessing  the  traffic  violation,  the  police
officer  may  immediately  conduct a full  search  incident  to
arrest  rather  than  having  to  adhere  to  the  more  restrictive
requirements  of  Terry  v.  Ohio,  392  U.S.  1  (1968)  and  its
progeny.  Time  and  time  again,  we  are  seeing  people
stopped  for  traffic  offenses,  but  prosecuted  on  evidence

aa  ae  eee

recovered  during  such  a  search.  Also  coming  to  light  are
the  many  instances  where  the  police  conduct  such  a
search,  find  nothing,  and  let  the  driver  go.  This  is  most
frequently  affecting  minority  drivers.

Under  the  dictates  of  Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.
806  (1996),  we  cannot  even  question  the  police  officer
about  his  actual  motives  in  conducting  the  stop,  but  the
increasingly  available  information  suggests  that  these
abuses  are  actually  occurring.  The  position  of  the  Texas
Criminal  Defense  Lawyers  Association  is  that  custodial
arrest  for  minor  traffic  violations  is  an  unreasonable  sei-
zure  under  the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments.  The
purpose  of  this  Brief  is  to  demonstrate  the  abuses  atten-
dant  with  the  fact  that,  because  an  officer  may  arrest  for
virtually  any  traffic  offense,  he  has  automatic  probable
cause  to  arrest  in  virtually  any  traffic  stop  from  the  very
time  he  sees  the  infraction.  These  abuses  come  mainly  in
the  form  of  “pre-arrest  searches  incident  to  arrest,”  which
have  become  sanctioned  under  this  Court’s  more  recent
precedent.

A.  The  Changing  Basis  of  the  Search  Incident  to

Arrest.

In  the  past,  this  Court  has  always  impliedly,  if  not
expressly,  tied  the  power  to  conduct  a  search  incident  to
arrest  to  the  fact  that  a  person  is arrested.  For  instance,  in
United  States  v.  Robinson,  414  U.S.  218  (1973),  the  defen-
dant  was  actually  arrested  for  operating  his  car  without  a
license.  See  id.  at  220.  The  issue  in  that  case  was  whether
the  police  officer  could  then  conduct  a  search  incident  to
the  arrest.  See  id.  at  235.  The  Court  held  that  the  officer

could.  See  id.  In  setting  out  the  law,  the  Court  stated  as
follows:

It  is  well  settled  that  a  search  incident  to  a
lawful  arrest  is  a  traditional  exception  to  the
warrant  requirement  of  the  Fourth  Amendment.
This  general  exception  has  historically  been  for-
mulated  into  two  distinct  propositions.  The  first
is  that  a  search  may  be  made  of  the  person  of
the  arrestee  by  virtue  of the  lawful  arrest.  The
second  is  that  a  search  may  be  made  of  the  area
within  the  cdntrol  of  the  arrestee.

Id.  at  224  (emphasis  added).

Decided  the  same  day  as  Robinson  was  Gustdfson  v.

Florida,  414  U.S.  260  (1973),  also  a  case  in  which  the
defendant  was  arrested  for  driving  without  a  license  and
searched.  See  id.  at  262.  The  Court  again  expressed  its
reasoning  justifying  a  search  incident  to  arrest  in  terms
likewise  suggesting  that  it was  the  fact  of  the  arrest  that
gives  rise  to  the  power  to  conduct  a  search  incident  to
that  arrest:

:

We  hold,  therefore,  that  upon  arresting  peti-
tioner  for  the  offense  of  driving  his  automobile
without  possession  of a  valid  operator’s  license,
and  taking  him  into  custody,  [the  officer]  was  enti-
tled  to  make a full  search  of  petitioner’s  person
incident  to  that  lawful  arrest.  Since  it is the fact of
custodial  arrest  which  gives  rise  to  the  authority  to
search,  it  is  of  no  moment  that  [the  officer]  did
not  indicate  any  subjective  fear  of the  petitioner
or  that  he  did  not  himself  suspect  that  the  peti-
tioner  was  armed.

Id.  at  266  (emphasis  added);  see  also  New  York  v.  Belton,
453  U.S.  454,  460-63  (1981)  (search  took  place  after  the

arrest).

With  the  1980  decision  of  Rawlings  v.  Kentucky,  448
U.S.  98  (1980),  however,  the  power  to  conduct  a  search
incident  to  arrest  became  tied  not  to  the  fact  of the  arrest
itself,  but  to  the  presence  of probable  cause  to  arrest.  The
genesis  of  this  evolution  was  the  following  language  in
Rawlings:

Once  petitioner  admitted  ownership  of  the
sizable  quantity  of  drugs  found  in  [her  compan-
ion’s]  purse,  the  police  clearly  had  probable
cause  to  place  petitioner  under  arrest.  Where  the
formal  arrest  followed  quickly  on  the  heels  of
the  challenged  search  of  petitioner’s  person,  we
do  not  believe  it  particularly  important  that  the
search  preceded  the  arrest  rather  than  vice
versa.

Id.,  448  U.S.  at  111.  Even  though  this  language  still  con-
templates  a  “formal  arrest”  actually  taking  place,  it
expressly  dispensed  with  any  kind  of  requirement  that
the  arrest  happen  before  the  search  incident  to  that  arrest.
This  language  has  been  universally  taken  to  heart  by the
lower  courts.

Since  Rawlings,  the  pre-arrest  search  incident  to
arrest  has  further  evolved  into  two  general  types:  (1)
Those  where  the  initial  probable  cause  to  arrest  (that
justifying  the  search)  is the  reason  for  both  the  search  and
the  ultimate  arrest;  and  (2)  those  where  the  person's
actual  arrest  and  subsequent  prosecution  are  based  not
upon  the  reason  for  the  initial  probable  cause,  but  upon
evidence  found  during  the  search.

One  example  of  the  first  category  is  United  States  v.
Tavolacci,  704  F.Supp.  246  (D.D.C.  1988).  Tavolacci  is a  drug
courier  case.  See  id.  at  247-48.  The  defendant,  a  passenger
on a train,  fit certain  aspects  of a  drug  courier  profile  and
thereby  got  the  attention  of the  authorities.  See  id.  During
his  trip,  some  law  enforcement  officers  questioned  him
and  asked  him  if they  could  search  or  have  a  dog sniff  his
suitcase.  See  id.  at  248.  He  declined,  but  was  detained
anyway  while  the  agents  waited  for  a  dog.  See  id.  At  one
point  while  he  waited,  he  attempted  to  bribe  one  of  the
agents  with  cocaine.  See  id.  Ultimately,  a  dog  did  sniff  his
suitcase,  but  did  not  alert.  See  id.  at  248-49.  Nonetheless,
one  agent,  without  defendant's  consent,  opened  the  suit-
case,  at  which  time  the  dog  alerted  and  the  agents  found
cocaine.  See  id.  at  249.  Defendant  was  then  arrested  for
possession  of  cocaine.  See  id.  The  court  held  that  the
search  of the  suitcase  was a valid  search  incident  to  arrest
because  there  existed  probable  cause  to  arrest  the  defen-
dant  for  possession  of  cocaine  at  the  point  he  made  his
admission  to  the  agent.  See  id.  at  248,  251;  see  also  United
States  v.  Donaldson,  793  F.2d  498,  502-03  (2nd  Cir.  1986)
(probable  cause  to  arrest  which  supported  pre-arrest
search  of defendant’s  apartment  was  based  on  defendant
giving  a  conflicting  story  about  a  fugitive’s  whereabouts,
after  fugitive  found  in  his  apartment,  defendant  arrested
and  prosecuted  for  harboring  a  fugitive);  United  States  v.

Thornton,  733  F.2d  121,  128  n.9  (D.D.C.  1984)  (probable
cause  to  arrest  which  supported  pre-arrest  search  was
based  on  defendant,  during  a  drug  raid  at  apartment
complex  and  after  somebody  yelled,  “Police,”  shoving
something  that  looked  like  drugs  into  his  back  pocket,

defendant  searched,  arrested  and  prosecuted  for  posses-
sion  of  drugs);  United  States  v.  Jaramillo,  714  F.Supp.  323,
332-33  (N.D.  Ill.  1989)  (probable  cause  to  arrest  which
supported  pre-arrest  search  was  based  on  defendant  fit-
ting  drug  courier  profile  and  bulkiness  around  defen-
dant’s  waist,  defendant  arrested  and  prosecuted  for
possession  of  drugs);  United  States  v.  Pace,  709  F.Supp.
948,  956-57  (C.D.  Cal.  1989)  (probable  cause  to  arrest
which  supported  pre-arrest  search  was  based  on  officer
feeling  two  kilo  bricks  of cocaine  strapped  to  defendant's
back  during  a  consensual  pat-down  search  of  outer  gar-
ments,  defendant  arrested  and  prosecuted  for  possession
of  drugs).

Cases  falling  into  the  second  category,  on  the  other
hand,  are  cases  where  the  pre-arrest  search  incident  to
arrest  is  supported  by  probable  cause  to  arrest  on  one
basis,  but  the  arrest  is  made  after  the  “pre-arrest  search
incident  to  arrest”  turns  up  something  else,  usually  some
kind  of contraband.  This  has  come  to  be  most  commonly
seen  where  the  probable  cause  to  arrest  is based  on  some
traffic  offense,  but  the  ultimate  arrest  (after  a  search)  is
based  on  contraband  that  was  found  during  the  search.

A  good  example  in  this  category  is the  recent  case  of
United  States  v.  Lugo,  170  F.3d  996  (10th  Cir.  1999).  In  this
case,  the  defendant  was  stopped  by a police  officer  for
speeding.  See  id.  at  1000.  Once  stopped,  the  officer
learned  that  the  defendant  had  no  driver’s  license.  See  id.
In  Utah  (as in Texas)  a  person  can  be  arrested  for  driving
without  a  license.  See  id.  at  1003.  The  officer  asked  defen-
dant  to exit  the  car  and  patted  him  down  for weapons.  He
then  did  a  complete  search  of the  passenger  compartment
of the  car,  finding  cocaine  in  a  secret  compartment  under

the  carpet  in  the  back  seat  area.  See  id.  The  defendant  was
then  arrested  for  possession  of a  controlled  substance.  See
id.

In  analyzing  the  legality  of  the  search,  which  most
definitely  took  place  prior  to  the  arrest,  the  Tenth  Circuit
Court  of Appeals  set  out  a  framework  very  reminiscent  of
Rawlings:  “A  warrantless  search  incident  to  arrest  is valid
so  long  as  (1) there  existed  a  legitimate  basis  for  the  arrest
before  the  search;  and  (2)  the  arrest  took  place  shortly
after  the  search.”  Id.  at  1003  (citing  United  States  v.
Anchondo,  156  F.3d  1043,  1045  (10th  Cir.  1998));  see  also
Rawlings,  448  U.S.  at  111.  The  court  went  on  to  apply  the
first  prong  of  this  analysis,  observing,  “Here,  the  officer
observed  that  [the  defendant]  was  speeding  and  operat-
ing a  vehicle  without  a  valid  driver's  license.  Under  Utah
law,  these  offenses  are  class  C  misdemeanors,  and  the
officer  had  the  authority  to  lawfully  arrest  [the  defen-
dant]  for  committing  these  offenses.  Thus,  probable  cause
and a legitimate  basis  to  arrest  [the  defendant]  existed.”
Id.  (citations  omitted).

The  reported  case  law  in  Texas  is dotted  with  similar
examples.  For  instance,  in  Williams  v.  State,  726  S.W.2d  99,
99-101  (Tex.  Crim.  App.  1986),  the  defendant  was  arrested
and  prosecuted  for  unlawfully  carrying  a  weapon.  A
police  officer  witnessed  the  defendant  in his  truck,  which
was  parked  on  the  wrong  side  of  the  road.  With  the
intention  of  investigating  for  drugs,  he  walked  up  to  the
defendant’s  truck.  Once  at  the  truck,  the  officer  saw  a
paper  bag on  the  floorboard  of the  truck.  See  id.  at  100.  He
reached  down,  seized  the  bag and  opened  it, finding  two
pistols.  The  defendant  was  then  arrested  for  the  pistols.
The  Court  of Criminal  Appeals  held  that  the  search.  was  a

10

11

valid  search  incident  to  arrest  because  the  officer  could
have  arrested  the  defendant  for  parking  on  the  wrong
side  of  the  road.  See  id.  at  101.

The  defendant  in  State  v.  McCall,  929  S.W.2d  601  (Tex.
App.  -  San  Antonio  1996,  no  pet.)  was  also  arrested  and
prosecuted  for  unlawfully  carrying  a  weapon.  See  id.  at
602.  He  was  stopped  in  his  car  for  failure  to  have  a
license  plate  light,  which,  under  Texas  law,  also  gave  the
officer  probable  cause  to  arrest,  if he  chose  to  do  so.  See
id.;  see  also  Tex.  Transp.  Code  Ann.  §§  547.322(f),
542.301(a),  543.001  (Vernon  1999)  (rear  license  plate  must
be  illuminated;  failure  to  comply  is  an  offense;  general
authorization  to  arrest).  He  searched  the  car  and  found
weapons,  and  thereafter  arrested  the  defendant  for  pos-
session  of  those  weapons.  See  McCall,  929  S.W.2d  at  602.

B.  The  Abuses  Resulting  from  this  Evolution  in  the
Law  Serve  to  Demonstrate  That  an  Arrest  for  a
Minor  Traffic  Violation  Is  “Unreasonable”  Under
the  Fourth  Amendment.

These  are  the  cases,  however,  where  the  police  actu-
ally found  something  in  their  search.  The  abuses  inherent
in  this  practice  are  demonstrated  in  the  instances  where
the  officers  stop  a  driver  for  a  traffic  offense,  conduct  a
search,  find  nothing,  and  let  the  driver  go.  The  most
grievous  abuse  is  not  where  the  police  officer  follows  a
hunch  that  proves  right,  but  when  he  follows  a  hunch
that  proves  to  be  wrong.  Technically,  the  search  incident
to  arrest  would  then  have  been  illegal  because  the  “for-
mal  arrest”  did  not  “follow[]  quickly  on  the  heels  of
the  ...  search,”  Rawlings,  448  U.S.  at  111,  but  it would  be

up  to  the  driver  to  tell  somebody.  These  abuses  are  vis-
ited  most  often  on  minorities.  See  David  A.  Harris,  Driv-
ing  While  Black:  Racial  Profiling  on  Our  Nation's  Highways
(June  1999)  at  18-24,  available  at  www.aclu.org  /profil-
ing/report  (extensive  treatment  of  statistics  from  a
number  of  different  cities  showing  the  number  of  total
traffic  stops  versus  the  number  of those  stops  that  involve
minorities).  Victims  of  this  type  of  police  abuse  tend  to
not  often  tell  their  stories.  As  two  authors  recently
observed,  “[A]n  illegally  searched  innocent  minority
member  is  less  likely  to  have  the  resources  to  mount  a
lawsuit  to  expose  inadequate  internal  investigation  pro-
cedures  than  a  white  yuppie.”  William  H.  Buckman  &
John  Lambreth,  Challenging  Racial  Profiles:  Attacking  Jim
Crow  on  the  Interstate,  Tue  CHampion,  Sept./Oct.  1999,  at
14.

Shifting  the  focus  from  the  fact  of  the  arrest  to  the
mere  presence  of probable  cause  to  arrest  on  any  ground
has  in  a  great  number  of cases  turned  the  search  incident
to  arrest  into  an  instrument  of  oppression.  See  generally
Harris,  supra,  at  9-18  (detailing  several  specific  accounts
where  persons’  cars  were  searched,  and  sometimes  even
dismantled,  after  being  pulled  over  by  the  police  for
traffic  violations  but  where  ultimately  no  arrest
occurred).  The  way  the  law  stands  right  now,  if a  police
officer  decides  that  he  just  wants  to  check  to  see  if  a
particular  driver  happens  to  have  any  drugs  on  him,  he
pulls  in  behind  him  in  traffic  and  waits.  Eventually,  the
driver  will  fail  to  use  a  turn  signal  to  indicate  a  lane
change  or  commit  some  other  minor  traffic  violation.  At
that  point,  the  police  officer  has  probable  cause  not  only

12

13

to  stop,  but  to  arrest.  He  pulls  the  driver  over  and  imme-
diately  conducts  a  full-scale  search  incident  to  arrest.
Finding  drugs,  he  arrests  the  driver  on  a  drug  charge.
Not  only  is  this  scenario  not  prohibited  by  this  Court's
pronouncements,  it is  positively  sanctioned  by them.  See
Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.  806,  813  (1996);  New  York
v.  Belton,  453  U.S.  454,  460-63  (1981);  Rawlings  v.  Kentucky,
448  US.  98,  110-11  (1980).  In  light  of  Whren,  an  officer
cannot  even  be  questioned  about  his  motivation  for  the
stop.  See  id.,  517  U.S.  at  813.  All  that  matters  is  the
existence  of  probable  cause  to  arrest,  which,  as  it  hap-
pens,  is  automatically  present  at  virtually  every  traffic
stop  that  is made  anywhere  by reason  of an  arcane  collec-
tion  of  traffic  regulations.

This  Court  stated  in Sibron  v.  New  York,  392  U.S.  40, 63
(1968),  “It  is  axiomatic  that  an  incident  search  may  not
precede  an  arrest  and  serve  as  part  of  its  justification.”
This  axiom  has  been  reaffirmed  in  later  cases,  see,  ¢.g.,
Smith  v.  Ohio,  494  U.S.  541,  541-43  (1990),  and  was  even
stated  in  Rawlings  itself.  See  id.,  448  U.S.at  111  n.6.  We
have  begun,  however,  to  see  case  after  case  in  which
officers  stop  a  driver  for  a  traffic  offense,  conduct  a
search,  and  arrest  the  driver  for  what  they  find.  This
turns  the  Sibron  “axiom”  into  nothing  more  than a legal
fiction.

David  Harris  recounts  an  incident  in  his  article  on
racial  profiling  where  Nelson  Walker,  a  black  college
student,  was  stopped  in  North  Carolina  in  1998  for  not
wearing  a  seatbelt:

The  officers  detained  him  and  his  two  pas-
sengers  for  two  hours  as  they  searched  for  ille-
gal  drugs,  weapons,  or  other  contraband.

Finding  nothing  in  the  car,  they  proceeded  to
dismantle  the  car  and  removed  part  of  a  door
panel,  a  seat  panel  and  part  of the  sunroof.  The
officers  found  nothing  and  in  the  end  handed
Walker  a  screwdriver,  saying,  “You're  going  to
need  this,”  as  they  left  the  scene.

Harris,  supra,  at  13.  Even  more  stunning  than  the  level  of
abuse  Mr.  Walker  suffered  at the  hands  of those  officers  is
the  fact  that  if  the  search  had  turned  up  just  one  mari-
juana  cigarette,  everything  those  officers  did  on  that  day
would  not  only  have  been  vindicated,  but  not  even  sub-
ject  to  question!

C.  Traffic  Stops  Should  Be  Subject  to  the  Same  Rea-
sonable  Suspicion  and  Probable  Cause  Standards  as
All  Other  Police/Citizen  Encounters.
The  fact  that  a  police  officer  may  arrest  for  a  traffic
offense  has  created  a  shortcut  around  the  Fourth  Amend-
ment  and  the  requirements  this  Court  has  otherwise  set
for  police-citizen  encounters,  searches  and  arrests.  See
Michigan  v.  Long,  463  U.S.  1032,  1049-50,  1049  n.14  (1983)
(setting  out  the  limits  of a  search  under  Terry  v.  Ohio,  392
U.S.  1 (1968)  as  they  apply  to  automobiles  and  drawing  a
distinction  between  a  Terry  search  and  a  search  incident
to arrest).  So long as  a  law  is on  the books  allowing  police
officers  the  discretion  to  arrest  drivers  for  minor  traffic
offenses,  there  will  always  be  probable  cause  to  arrest
and  therefore  always  be  an  automatic  basis  for  a  search.  —
See  Whren,  517  U.S.  at  813  (quoting  Scott  v.  United  States,
436  U.S.  128,  136  (1978)).  What  has  happened  is  that  the
rules  enunciated  in  Terry  have  been  abrogated  in perhaps
the  vast majority  of vehicle  stops.  While  a  search  incident

14

15

v.  Williams,  407  U.S.  143,  145-49  (1972);  Terry  v.  Ohio,  392
U.S.  at  21-22,  27.  So  long  as  he  can  justify  his  actions
under  the  Terry  standard,  his  subjective  intentions  still
would  not  matter.  Otherwise,  he  should  not  be  given
carte  blanche  to  conduct  an  invasive,  full-blown  search
incident  to  arrest,  before  an  arrest  is affected,  on  a  minor
traffic  offense  for  which  he would  in all  likelihood  issue  a
ticket.  That  he  can  presently  do  so  only  serves  to  further
highlight  the  fact  that  custodial  arrests  for  minor  traffic
violations  which  are  not  breaches  of the  peace  are  unrea-
sonable  seizures  under  the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth
Amendments.

to  arrest  is  supported  by a  governmental  interest  in  pre-
serving  evidence,  such  a  search  cannot  be  legitimately
supported  by an  interest  in blindly  searching  for evidence
of  any  type.  See  Long,  463  U.S.  at  1049  n.14;  Chimel  v.
California,  395  U.S.  752,  762-63  (1969).  There  is no  societal
need,  nor  a  reasonable  law  enforcement  need,  that  is met
by denying  a  citizen  the  protections  of  Terry  just  because
he  is driving  his  car  rather  than  walking  down  the  street.
To  think  that  evidence  of  a  traffic  offense  might  be  con-
cealed  in the  driver's  car,  or  that  the  driver  could  actually
destroy  such  evidence,  is  patently  ridiculous  in  any
event.  The  more  rational  approach  would  be  to  re-apply
the  traditional  standards  of  reasonable  suspicion  and
probable  cause  to  traffic  stops.

This  Court  should  recognize  that  it  is  unreasonable
under  the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  for  a
police  officer  to  have  the  power  to  affect  a  custodial
arrest  for  minor  traffic  violations  -  especially  where  the
decision  to  arrest  is  left  to  that  officer’s  discretion  -
unless  those  violations  amount  to  a  breach  of  the  peace.
Where  a  police  officer  observes  a  traffic  offense,  he should
have  probable  cause  to  stop  and  give a citation.  If, during
that  stop,  additional  reasonable  suspicion  or  probable
cause  develops,  he may  investigate  further  as  appropriate
under  the  established  standards.  See  Long,  463  U.S.  at
1049-50;  Florida  v.  Royer,  460  U.S.  491,  497-501  (1983);
Michigan  v.  Summers,  452  U.S.  692,  699  (1981);  United
States  v.  Brignoni-Ponce,  422  U.S.  873,  881-82  (1975);  Adams

16

PRAYER

For  these  reasons,  amicus  prays  that  this  Court  will
grant  review  in  the  above  cause  to  examine  this  impor-
tant  issue.

Dated:  March  20,  2000

Fort  Worth,  Texas

Respectfully  submitted,

QUESTION  PRESENTED

Petitioner  Gail  Atwater  was  placed  under  custodial  arrest  for
not  wearing a seat  belt,  a misdemeanor  that  carries a fifty-
dollar  fine  as  its maximum  penalty.  Does  the Fourth
Amendment  limit  the use  of custodial  arrests  for fine-only
traffic  offenses?

Pursuant  to Rule  37 of this  Court,  amici  curiae,  the

National  Association  of Defense  Lawyers  and the
Association  of Federal  Defenders  move  for leave  to  file the
accompanying  brief amici  curiae  in support  of the petition
for certiorari.

The  petitioners,  who  are  represented  by legal counsel,

have  consented  to the  filing of this brief amici  curiae.  The
respondents,  similarly  represented  by counsel,  have  not
consented  to the  filing of this brief.

The  National  Association  of Criminal  Defense
Lawyers  (NACDL)  is a professional  bar association  founded
in 1958  for the purpose  of advancing  the mission  of the
nation's  criminal  defense  lawyers  to ensure  justice  and due
process  for persons  accused  of a crime  or other  misconduct.
The  Association  of Federal  Defenders  is a nation-wide,  non-
profit,  volunteer organization  whose  membership  includes
attorneys  and support staff of Federal  Defender  Offices,  all
created  by the Criminal  Justice  Act,  18 U.S.C.  § 3006A.

Consistent  with  their  goals of advancing  the
protections  of persons  against  arbitrary  treatment  at the hands
of the criminal  justice  system,  NACDL  and AFD  offer their
tegai  analysis  of an  arrest  for the petty offense  of driving
without  a license.’  Both  organizations regularly  appear  before
this  Court  as  amicus  curiae,  have  an  expertise  in criminal
law,  and  a commitment  to the rights of the accused.

Amici  curiae  therefore  are  able to provide  a unique

perspective  to this Court  on  practical  and  legal probletMs  with
misdemeanor  arrests  that  involve  no  breach  of the peace,  the
_  issue  raised  in the petition  for writ of certiorari.  Amici  curiae
