The National  Association  of Criminal  Defense  Lawyers
(NACDL)  is a professional  bar association  founded  in 1958
for the purpose  of advancing  the mission  of the nation's
criminal  defense  lawyers  to ensure justice and due process
for persons  accused  of a crime  or other  misconduct.  Today,
NACDL  has almost  10,000  members  and  80 affiliates
representing  another  28,000  members,  who  include  private
criminal  defense  lawyers,  public  defenders,  active  U.S.
military  personnel  and  law professors.  NACDL  has
members  in all fifty states,  and the American  Bar Association
recognizes  NACDL  as  an  affiliate  organization  and awards  it
full representation  in the House  of Delegates.

Among  NACDL's  objectives  is to deter overreaching
conduct  by law enforcement  officers  by vigorously  defending
the protections  guaranteed  by the Constitution.  NACDL
urges  this Court  to review  the decision  of the United  States
Court  of Appeals  for the Fifth  Circuit  as the lower court
decision  provides  for vast  officer  discretion,  permitting  the
overzealous  officer  to arrest  any person  he chooses  for the
pettiest  of offenses.  The Fourth  Amendment  is designed  to
constrain  the discretion  of the officer  in the field  and assure
that his conduct  in seizing citizens,  when  permissible,  is
reasonable.  The Fifth  Circuit  decision  provides  neither
assurance.  Accordingly,  NACDL  files this brief in support
of the petition  for writ of certiorari

The Association  of Federal  Defenders  ("AFD")  was
formed  in 1995  to enhance  the representation  under  the
Criminal  Justice  Act,  18 U.S.C.  § 3006A,  and the Sixth

'  No counsel for any party has authored this brief in whole or in part, and
no person or entity,  other than amici, has made a  monetary  contribution  to
or submission  of this brief.
the preparation

is a

Amendment  of the United  States  Constitution.  The  AFD
nation-wide,  non-profit,  volunteer  organization  whose
membership  includes  attorneys  and support  staff of Federal
Defender  Offices.  One of the AFD's  missions  is to file
amicus  briefs  to ensure  that the position  of indigent
defendants  in the criminal  justice  system  is adequately
represented.  We believe  that  this mission  calls  upon  us  to
join in asking the Supreme  Court  to the review  the Atwater
case.  Although  it is a civil  rights action  rather  than  a
criminal  case,  Atwater  presents  an  issue  of great importance
in criminal  law:  whether  the Fourth  Amendment  prohibits  the
use  of custodial  arrests  for fine-only  traffic  offenses.  A full
custodial  arrest  invades  one's  privacy  interests  and one's
dignity,  and can  often  lead to criminal  prosecution  on  other
charges.  It is in the interest  of our  clients  to ask the Supreme
Court  to review  the Fourth  Amendment's  requirements  in this
area.

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

This  Court  has held  that  even  where  an  officer  has
probable  cause  to believe  a suspect  has committed  a crime,
the reasonableness  of the manner  of seizure  is governed  by
the Fourth  Amendment.  Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1
(1985).

Reasonableness  is first viewed  through  the lens of the

common  law.  "[{A]n examination  of the common-law
understandings  of an  officer's  authority  to arrest  sheds  light
on  the obviously  relevant,  if not  entirely  dispositive,
consideration  of what  the Framers  of the Amendment  might
have  thought  to be reasonable."  Payton  v.  New  York, 445
U.S.  573,  591  (1980).  At common  law, a misdemeanor  arrest
was  not  permitted  absent  a reason  to believe  that the suspect
would  occasion  a breach  of the peace  if not  arrested.  Sir

‘

James  Stephen,  History  of the Criminal  Law  of England  193
(1883).

;

The  reasonableness  of a seizure  is further  considered  by

weighing  the state's  interests  in the seizure  against  the
individual's  liberty  interest.  Garner,  471  U.S.  at  8.  The
state's  interest  in detaining  a person  charged  with  a crime  are
ensuring  the defendant's  presence  at trial  and  preventing
further  harm  at the defendant's  hands  in the interim.  See
United  States  v.  Salerno,  481  U.S.  739 (1987).  A defendant
charged  with  a fine-only  misdemeanor  offense  is unlikely  to
attempt  to evade  the justice  system  given the very  minimal
potential  punishment  and  the relatively  onerous  sanctions  for
failure  to appear.  Failure  to appear  is a separate  offense  and
failure  to answer a traffic  charge can  lead  to suspension  of
one's  driver's  license  in her state  of origin,  irrespective  of the
location  of the offense.  See Barbara  C. Salken,  The  General
Warrant  of the  Twentieth  Century?  A Fourth  Amendment
Solution  to  Unchecked  Discretion  to Arrest for Traffic
Offenses,  17 Pace  L. Rev.  97,  159 n.  320 (1997)  (noting
many  states  have joined the Non-Resident  Violator  Compact
permitting  the suspension  of a driver's  license  in her home
state  for failure  to answer a traffic  charge  in another  state).

Finally,  this Court's  holding  in Knowles  v.  Iowa,  525  U.S.

113  (1999)  demonstrates  this Court's  assumption  that a
misdemeanor  that involves  no  breach  of the peace does not
justify a custodial  arrest.  This  Court  held  in Knowles  that a
search  incident  to arrest  could  not  occur  if a motorist  is
merely  given a citation  for a traffic  offense.  Jd. at  118.
Long-standing  precedent  of this Court  permits  a search
incident  to arrest  prior to the actual  arrest,  so  long as  an
officer  has probable  cause  to make  the arrest  at the time of
the search.  Rawlings  v.  Kentucky,  448  U.S.  98,  111  (1980).
If probable  cause  to believe  a motorist  had committed  a
traffic  offense  permitted  a custodial  arrest,  then  the search  of

Mr.  Knowles  would  have  been justified  as  an  arrest  could
have  taken  place.

Notwithstanding  the  fairly clear  application  of this

Court's  doctrines  to the issue  of misdemeanor  arrests
involving  no  breach  of the peace,  a variety  of results  have
appeared  in the  lower  courts.

The  First  Circuit  has recognized  that the Fourth
Amendment  requires  that the seizure  of a person  for a
misdemeanor  offense  must  be reasonable,  that probable  cause
does  not  end the inquiry  into  whether  an  arrest  is reasonable.
Diaz  v.  City of Fitchburg,  176  F.3d  560 (1st Cir.  1999).  The
Ninth  Circuit  has held  that  the Fourth  Amendment  forbids  an
arrest  for a misdemeanor  if state  law prohibits  such  an  arrest.
United  States  v.  Mota,  982  F.2d  1384  (9th Cir.  1993).

The Court  of Appeals  decision  in the instant  case  is to the
contrary,  holding  that  so  long as officers  have  probable  cause
to believe  a misdemeanor  infraction  has occurred,  an  arrest  is
permissible  so  long as  the suspect  is not  killed  or maim  in the
seizure.  Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,  195  F.3d  242, 245
(Sth Cir.  1999)  (en banc).

A patchwork  of official  discretion  is left in the wake  of
these  decisions  --  citizens  in the Ninth  Circuit  are  subject  to
misdemeanor  arrests  unless  their  state  legislatures  provide
otherwise,  citizens  in the First  Circuit  are  subjected  only to
"reasonable"  misdemeanor  arrests,  while  citizens  in the Fifth
Circuit  enjoy no  federal  constitutional  protection  from  such
seizures.

This  Court  should grant  certiorari  so  that this  important
question  of whether  citizens  may  be subjected  to such  official
discretion  may  be considered.

ARGUMENT

I.

THE  FEDERAL  CONSTITUTION'S
LIMITATIONS  ON  THE  POWER  TO  CONDUCT
A MISDEMEANOR  ARREST  HAVE  BEEN
DIVERSELY  INTERPRETED  BY  FEDERAL
COURTS  OF APPEAL  AND  STATE  COURTS  OF
LAST  RESORT.

Some  federal  courts  of appeal  and  state  courts  of last

resort  have  held  that  the Fourth  Amendment  imposes  no
limitations  on  an  officer's  discretion  to arrest  for a
misdemeanor.  Others  have  held  that  an  officer  may  never
arrest  for a simple  misdemeanor.  In short,  every  possible
manner  of applying  the Fourth  Amendment  to this question
appears  in the reported  decisions.  A hodgepodge  of officer
authority  exists  --  in Tennessee,  a motorist  may  not  be
arrested  for reckless  driving,  traveling  92 miles  per hour  in a
65 mile  per hour  zone.  See State  v.  Colzie,  No.  M1998
00253  CCA  R3  CD,  1999  WL  1074111  (Tenn.  Crim.  App.,
November  30,  1999).  The  same  motorist,  in Texas,  may  be
arrested,  searched,  the contents  of her car  inventoried  for
failure  to wear  her seat  belt.  Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,
195  F.3d  242  (Sth Cir.  1999) (en banc).

Fourth  Amendment  limitations  on  the power  to arrest
for a misdemeanor  involving  no  breach  of the peace  could
take one  of three  forms:

(1) The amendment  may prohibit  the arrest  as  such  an

arrest  was  impermissible  under  the common  law  and  involves
an  intrusion  into  individual  liberty exceeding  the state's
interest  in the custodial  arrest.  See State  v.  Hehman,  578
P.2d  527 (Wash.  1978) (finding  as  a matter  of state  public
policy the degree of intrusion  exceeds  the state's  interest  in
prosecuting  an  ordinary  traffic  offense);  Diaz  v.  Fitchburg,

176  F.3d  560  (1st Cir.  1999)  (recognizing  Fourth
Amendment  may  prohibit  certain  category  of misdemeanor
arrests,  but  not  where  the crime  is trespass  which  under
Massachusetts  law  requires  a person  to refuse  an  official
request  to leave,  demonstrating  a violation  that  will  not
conclude  absent  an  arrest).

(2) Statutory  provisions  on  the power  to arrest  may
define  those  intrusions  which  are  unreasonable.  See  United
States  v.  Mota,  982  F.2d  1384  (9th Cir.  1993)  (state
prohibition  on  custodial  arrest  for minor  infraction  renders
such  an  arrest  violative  of the Fourth  Amendment);  State  v.
Bland,  884  P.2d  312  (Colo.  1994)  (en banc)  (custodial  arrest
for misdemeanor  marijuana  possession  not  permitted  in light
of statute  providing  for citation  for offense);  State  v.  Chearis,
995  S.W.2d  641  (Tenn.  Crim.  App.  1999)  (arrests  for
misdemeanors  unreasonable  given state  statute  prohibiting
arrest  except  where  prosecution  would  be jeopardized  by
citation  or breach  of peace  is threatened).

(3) The  amendment  imposes  no  limitations  on  arrests

based  on  probable  cause  that occur  outside  the home  that do
not  involve  deadly  force.  Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,  195
F.3d  242, 244  (Sth Cir.  1999) (en banc).  This  interpretation
expands  the doctrine  of Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.  806
(1996)  beyond  facts  ever  contemplated  by this Court.  This
interpretation  assumes  that  since  a seizure  may  always  occur
whenever  an  officer  has probable  cause  to believe a citizen
has committed  a crime,  that the manner  and degree of his
seizure  are  beyond  the scope  of the Fourth  Amendment.

These  widely  varying  interpretations  of the Fourth
Amendment  leave  citizens  of varying jurisdictions  at the
hands  of officers  with  vastly different  degrees  of discretion.
The  approach  taken  by the lower  court  permits  an  officer  to
take  a motorist  into  custody,  lead her away  in handcuffs,
have  her booked,  leave  her with  an  arrest  record,  and  require

her to remain  incarcerated  until  posting  bond  for an  offense
so  minor  that even  a judicial  officer  could  not  order  the
sanctions  imposed  by the officer.  The  reasonableness  of this
intrusion,  under  present  law, turns  on  the  location  of the
arrest.

This  Court  must  grant  certiorari  lest officer  discretion

to perform  intrusions  turn  on  geography  and jurisdiction.
This  Court  has clearly  stated  that  the reasonableness  of an
intrusion  is considered  in light of the limitations  imposed  by
the common  law and  in consideration  of a balance  of a state's
interest  in the intrusion  against  the individual's  liberty
interest.

The common  law prohibition  on  arrests  for
misdemeanors  is compelled  by balancing  the interests  of the
state  in the arrest  against  the individual's  liberty  interest.  The
State  simply  has no  interest  in arresting  those  who  face only
a fine and pose  no  danger to the community.  Certiorari
should  be granted  as  contrary  holdings  permit overzealous
officers,  or officers  desiring  suspicionless  searches,  to
perform  full custodial  arrests  for the most  minor  of
violations.

II.  THE  COMMON  LAW  PROHIBITED  TAKING  A
MISDEMEANANT  INTO  CUSTODY  ABSENT
AN  ANTICIPATED  BREACH  OF THE  PEACE.

This  Court  looks  first to the common  law to determine

whether  a governmental  intrusion  satisfies  the Fourth
Amendment.  Wyoming  v.  Houghton,  526  U.S.  295, 299
(1999).  The common  law did not  permit  a constable  to make
an  arrest  for  a misdemeanor  unless  it was  committed  in his
presence  and  involved  a breach  of the peace  that was  likely
to continue.  Sir James  Stephen,  History of the Criminal  Law
of England  193  (1883).

This  Court  has previously  recognized  limitations
imposed  by the common  law on  the power  to arrest.  "A
peace  officer...  has at common  law no  power  of arresting
without  a warrant  except  when a breach  of the peace  has
been  committed  in his presence  or there  is reasonable  ground
for supposing  that  a breach  of the peace  is about  to be
committed  or  renewed  in his presence."  Carroll  v.  United
States,  267  U.S.  133,  157 (1925).

Misdemeanor  arrests  at common  law were  for the
purpose  of preventing  the harm  that would  flow  from  a
breach  of the peace.  As Sir James  Stephen  observed  in his
commentary  on  the English  criminal  law:

The  common  law did not  authorise  the arrest
of persons  guilty or  suspected  of misdemeanors,
except  in cases  of an  actual  breach  of the peace
either  by an  affray or by violence  to an
individual.  In such  cases  the arrest  had to be
made  not  so  much  for the purpose  of bringing
the offender  to justice as  in order  to preserve
the peace,  and the right to arrest  was
accordingly  limited  to cases  in which  the
person  to be arrested  was  taken  in the fact or
immediately  after  its commission.

Sir  James  Stephen,  History of the Criminal  Law of England,
193  (1883).

Lord  Holt  observed  in 1710  that  "a constable  cannot
arrest,  but when  he sees  an  actual  breach  of the peace;  and if
the affray be over,  he cannot  arrest.”  Regina  v.  Tooley,  2
Lord  Raymond  1296,1301,  92 Eng. Rep. 349, 352 (K.B.
1710).  As one  modern  commentator  has noted,  "[tJhis is to
say that the arrest  was  not  strictly part of the punishment
process.  It was  mostly  for the purpose  of separating  the

combatants  and detaining  them  to prevent  further  violence."
Edward  C. Fisher,  Laws  of Arrest  189 (1967).

The offense  of driving  without  a seat  belt  is not  one  of
violence  --  no  breach  of the peace  is intended  or occasioned
by one  who  elects  to travel  the highway  less than  secure  in
the event  of an  accident.  Certainly  nothing beyond  the fact
the motorist  was  once  caught without a seat  belt  suggests  that
even  this minor  crime  will  continue  lest she is immediately
removed  from  the roadway.  The common  law, the starting
point in this Court's  analysis  of the reasonableness  of an
intrusion,  simply did not  contemplate  an  arrest  for such  a
minor,  non-violent  misdemeanor.

In Garner,  this  Court departed  from the common  law
rule permitting  deadly  force to seize  a fleeing  felon. The
Court  recognized  that advances  in technology  and changes  in
public  opinion  as reflected  in limitation  on officer's  use of
deadly  force compelled  a departure.  The effectiveness  of
modern  weapons  made use of a  deadly  weapon  to apprehend
a fleeing  felon far more likely to kill than an attempt  to seize
a suspect  with  the weapons  contemplated  at the time  of the
common
law rule.  Garner,  471  U.S. at 11. The individual
interest  implicated  by permitting  the use of deadly  force was
therefore  more  compelling  than under  a regime  where the
weapons  were  much  more  likely to wound,  or miss  entirely,
than  kill.  Modern  limitations
further  illustrated  that all reasonable  means of seizing  a non-
violent  fleeng  felon at common  law  were  not reasonable  in
modern  times.  /d.  at 16-19.

on the use of official  force

Modern  legislation  and technology  has made the use of a
custodial  arrest  for a  misdemeanor  even less compelling  than
it was at the time  it  was forbidden  by the common  law.  The
Non-Resident  Violator  Compact  has given a state  the power
to suspend  the driver's  license  of a  motorist  of another  state
who fails to appear  in court  to answer  a  misdemeanor  traffic

charge.  See Salken,  supra,  at  159.  Computer  technology
permits  an  officer  stopping  such  a motorist  to rapidly  learn
the status  af such  motorist's  license.  Technology  and
legislation  have  given states  a way  of compelling  appearance
in misdemeanor  traffic  offenses  unknown  at common  law  --
at a time  when  the common  law nonetheless  prohibited  a
custodial  arrest  for misdemeanors.

Garner  further  looked  to comtemporary  limitations  on

police  use  of force  to determine  whether  deadly  force  ought
to be permitted.  Finding  the modern  trend  to prohibit  deadly
force  for non-violent  fleeing  felons,  the Court  found
reasonableness  should  be assessed  differently  than  it was
under  the common  law.  Garner,  471  U.S.  at  16-19.

Many states  limit  the power  of officers  to arrest  citizens

for misdemeanor  violations.  For some  time,  an  arrest  for a
misdemeanor  has typically  been permitted  by state  statute
only where  releasing  the citizen  upon  a citation  would
threaten  a breach  of the peace  or jeopardize  the prosecution.”

Limitations

on the power to arrest  for misdemeanors  not
a breach  of the peace reveal  that  modern  society

constituting
finds  it unreasonable  to vest  law enforcement  officers  with
such  unlimited  discretion.  Interestingly,  Texas  law, by

? See,  e.g.  Alaska  Stat.  § 12.25.180(b)  (1984);  Cal.  Vehicle  Code  § 40504
(West  1985); Colo.  Rev.  Stat.  § 42-4-1501(4)(a)  (1988);  Ind. Code Ann.
§ 9-4-131  (Burns  1987);  Ky. Rev.  Stat.  Ann.  § 431.015(2)  (Baldwin
1985);  La. Rev.  Stat.  Ann.  § 32:391  (West  1988);  Md.  Trans.  Code  Ann.
§ 26-202(a)(2);  Mass.  Gen.  Laws  Ann.  § ch. 90 § 21 (West Supp.  1988);
Minn.  Stat.  Ann.  § 169.91  (West  1986 & Supp.  1989); Neb.  Rev.  Stat.  §§
29-427,  -432, -435, -39-6,  105 (1984-1985);  N.M.  Stat.  Ann.  § 66-8-123
(1987);  N.D.  Cent.  Code  § 39-07-07  (1987);  Okla. Stat. Ann.  tit. 22 §
1115.1(A)  (West Supp.  1989); Or. Rev.  Stat.  §§ 133.310(1),  810.410,
811.140  (1987);  S.C. Code  § 56-25-30  (Law Co-op.  Supp.  1988); S.D.
Codified  Laws Ann.  § 32-33-2  (1984); Tenn.  Code Ann.  § 40-7-118(b)(1)
(Supp.  1988); Va. Code Ann.  § 46.1-178  (Supp.  1988); Wis.  Stat.  Ann.
§§ 345.22  to .23 (West Supp.  1988); Vt. R. Crim.  P. 3(a), (c).

statute,  prohibits  a custodial  arrest  for speeding.  Tex.  Trans.
Code  Ann.  § 543.003  (West  1999).  Speeding  carries  a higher
fine under Texas  law than the crime  for which  the petitioner
was  arrested,  failure  to wear  a seat  belt.  And while  speeding
potentially  endangers  others,  failure  to wear  a seat  belt
imposes  a risk only  to those who elect to violate  the law.

Ill.  THE  MANNER  OF A SEIZURE  MUST  BE

REASONABLE  IN LIGHT  OF THE  STATE'S
INTERESTS

behavior  is reasonable.  This  Court has previously

This  case squarely  presents  the issue  of  whether
probable  cause  ends the analysis  of whether  a seizing
officer's
held that probable  cause does not end that inquiry,  that while
an officer  may have the right to seize a citizen,  the quality
and character  of that seizure  is still subject  to the limitations
of the Fourth  Amendment.  Tennessee v. Garner, 471 U.S.  1,
8 (1985).  Probable  cause permits  a  seizure,  Whren  v.  United
States,
517 U.S. 806, 813  (1996),  but the manner  in which
the seizure  is carried  out must be reasonable  in light  of the
government's
interest  in the seizure.  Garner,  471  U.S. at 8.
The lower  court decision  would  permit  an  officer  to seize a
citizen  in any manner  he saw fit so long as the officer  had
probable  cause  to believe  that some  offense  -- however  petty
-- had been  committed  and the arresting  officer  did not
actually  kill or maim  the suspect.  Atwater,  195  F.3d  at 245.

A.  The State's  Interest  in Ensuring  the Defendant's
Presence  at Trial  and Protecting  Society  from  a
Criminal  Suspect  Does Not Justify  a  Custodial
Arrest  for a  Fine-Only  Misdemeanor.

Once a state has probable  cause to believe  a  citizen
guilty  of a criminal  infraction,  the state's  interests  in seizing
her consist  of ensuring  her presence  at trial and ensuring  that

10

11

the citizenry  is safe  from  her pending  trial.  See  United  States
v.  Salerno,  481  U.S.  739,  748 (1987)  (recognizing  these
interest  to justify  pretrial  detention).  It has been  recognized
that  the seriousness  of the offense  is a key indicator  of the
likelihood  of the defendant  appearing  to answer  the charges
against  her.  Fisher,  supra,  at  189.  Further,  this Court  in
Salerno  recognized  that  the seriousness  of the offense  was  a
valid  indicator  of whether  the defendant  posed a threat  to
society  if released  pending  trial.  Salerno,  481  U.S.  at 749.  If
a goal of punishment  is incapacitation,  society  needs
protection  from  the violent  once  their dangerous  tendencies
become  evident.

For  most  modern misdemeanors,  pretrial  release  is all

but certain,  making  the relevant  inquiry  whether  the
misdemeanor  suggests  the defendant  presents  a danger he
will  not  later  present  once booked  and bonded  out.  The
common  law rule prohibiting  misdemeanor  arrest  recognizes
the legitimate  interests  of ensuring  the offender's  presence  at
trial and protecting  society  from the suspect.  For ordinary
misdemeanors,
where  the penalty  is less than severe,  the
suspect  is not likely  to fail to appear  as often the sanction  for
failure  to appear  exceeds  the sanction  for the underlying
misdemeanor.  And, unlike the crimes  of extortion  and
conspiracy
in Salerno,’  the
to commit  murder  contemplated
commission  of a non-violent  misdemeanor  does not  suggest
that society  needs immediate  protection  from the suspect.

Where,  however,  the state  illustrates  that the

misdemeanant,  despite the relative  minor classification  of his
crime,  does  pose an immediate  threat  to public  safety, the
common
law rule  provides  for his immediate  incarceration.
If the constable  reasonably  believed  that the misdemeanant

would  cause  a breach  of  the peace  if not arrested,  then an
arrest  was  permitted.

A modern  example  of a misdemeanor  that  itself suggests

the necessity  of incarceration  is driving  under  the influence
where  the driver  poses  a risk to society  because  of his
intoxication,  a condition  unchanged  by his initial  seizure.
Modern  statutes  universally  recognize  an  officer's  right to
arrest  a motorist  suspected  of being intoxicated.‘

Whren  v.  United States,  517  U.S.  806 (1996)  stands  for

the rather  unremarkable  principle  that any person  who
violates  the traffic  code may be forced
to suffer  the
consequences  established  by the legislature,  a very modest
fine.  Whren  did not  overrule  this Court's  holding  that the
degree of the intrusion  occasioned  to prosecute  the offense
must be balanced  against  the state's interest  in that degree  of
intrusion.

—_—_

B.  Absent  a Fear  for Public  Safety or  Inability  to

Otherwise  Bring Offender  to Justice,  the Means
of Seizure  May Not Exceed  the Punishment
Authorized  for the Offense.

As this  Court  recognized  in Garner,  at common  law,
was a capital crime.  Garner,  471  U.S.  at  13.

burglary
Imploring  deadly  force to stop an unarmed  burglar  therefore
involved  no greater  consequence  than  the burglar  would face
if convicted.  Modern  laws provide much  more  lenient
penalties  for burglary  and this Court accordingly  held in
Garner  that the consequence  of death  was disproportionate to

®  In Salerno, the defendants  were charged with 35  acts of racketeering

murder.  Salerno, 481 U.S. at 743.

* See e.g.  Tenn.  Code  Ann.  § 40-7-118(c)(7)  (requiring  misdemeanor
arrest  “the person  is so intoxicated  that such person  could be a  danger  to
such  person  or to others.”)

12

13

the state's  interest  in ensuring  prosecuting  of a non-capital
felon  who  posed no  risk  to the community.

Judge  Easterbrook  has recognized  that  an  arrest  for a
misdemeanor  offense  amounts  to more  punishment  than  will
typically  be assigned  by a neutral  judicial  officer.  Gramenos
v.  Jewel  Companies,  Inc.,  797  F.2d  432, 441  (7th Cir.  1986).
A warrantless  arrest  for such  an  offense  is a decision  by a
member  of the law enforcement  community  to bring about  a
$s anction  exceeding  that  likely imposed  by a neutral  and
detached  judicial  officer.  When  the offense  is a fine-only
misdemeanor,  the law enforcement  officer  has visited  a
sanction  upon a citizen  exceeding  even a judicial  officer's
authority  to punish.

C.  This  Court's  Decision  in Knowles  v.  Iowa

Assumes  Constitutional  Limitations  on  an
Officer's  Power  to Arrest  for  a Misdemeanor
Traffic  Offense.

This  Court  recently  and unanimously  held  that an

officer  may  not  perform a search  incident  to citation  for an
does not
-- that is, if an officer
ordinary  traffic offense
actually  arrest  a suspect,  a search  incident  to arrest  is
unconstitutional.  Knowles  v.  lowa,  525  U.S.  113  (1999).
This decision  assumes  that a custodial  arrest  for a
misdemeanor  traffic  offense  alone  does  not  permit  a custodial
arrest.

This Court  has held that where  police have probable

the search  incident  to  arrest occurs  before  or after

Cause  to arrest  a suspect,  it is constitutionally  irrelevant
whether
the suspect  is actually placed under  arrest.  Rawlings  v.
Kentucky,  448  U.S.  98, 111  (1980).  As Justice  Harlan  wrote,

If the prosecution  shows  probable  cause
to arrest prior to a search of a  man's person,

- a

it has met  its total burden.  There  is no  case
in which  a defendant  may  validly  say,
"Although  the officer had a right to arrest
me  at the moment  when  he seized  me  and
searched  my person,  the search  is invalid
because  he did not  in fact arrest  me  until
afterwards.”

Peters  v.  New  York,  392 U.S.  40, 77 (1968)  (Harlan,  J.,
concurring).

If the traffic  offense  in Knowles,  speeding 42 miles

per hour in a 25 mile-per-hour  zone,  had permitted  a
custodial  arrest,  then the officer  who  searched  Mr.  Knowles
would  have had probable cause  to make an arrest.  In
Rawlings  and Peters,  this Court  applied an objective  test  to
determine  whether  the pre-requisites
to arrest.  The Court looked  to whether
search incident
probable cause existed for an arrest, not whether  the officer
had actually  announced  the arrest and placed the suspect in
formal  custody.

had been met for a

If probable  cause  to believe  a suspect  committed  the

misdemeanor  traffic  offense  of speeding permitted  a
custodial  arrest  under the constitution,  then  it would  have
been  irrelevant  whether  the officer performed  the search
incident  to arrest before  or after the arrest.  Probable  cause to
believe  the suspect  has committed  a  crime serious  enough  to
justify a custodial  arrest is the only pre-requisite  to a search
incident  to arrest  under Rawlings  and Peters.

This Court's  unanimous  decision  in Knowles  therefore

does place  limitations on an
assumes  that the constitution
officer's  power to arrest  for an ordinary  misdemeanor  traffic
offense.

14

15

The  Court's  analysis  of the doctrine  of search  incident

to arrest  in Knowles  further  demonstrates  it assumed  that  a
custodial  arrest  for an  ordinary  traffic  violation  is
constitutionally  unreasonable.  The  State  of lowa  argued  that
a search  incident  to a traffic  violation  was  permitted  because
the stopped  motorist  might try to destroy  evidence  of another
crime  or destroy  evidence  relating  to his identity.  This  Court
found  the possibility  that  an  officer  would  stumble  onto
evidence  of an  unrelated  crime  to be remote.  The  Court
further  found  that  "[a]s for the destruction  of evidence
relating  to identity,  if a police officer  is not  satisfied  with  the
identification  furnished  by the driver,  this may  be a basis  for
arresting  him  rather  than  merely  issuing a citation."
Knowles,  525  U.S.  at  118.

The clear  inference  is that  there  must  be a basis  for

distinguishing  between  those  arrested  and those  cited.  If
there  were  no  basis  for the distinction  then  every  minor
traffic  offender  would  be liable  to arrest  and the search  of
Mr.  Knowles'  vehicle  would  have  violated  no  legitimate
expectation  of privacy.

The  Court's  conclusion  that the search  of Mr.
Knowles’  vehicle  violated  his right to be secure  in his person,
house,  papers,  or effects  clearly reflects  the assumption  by
this unanimous  Court  that the state's  interest  in prosecuting  a
misdemeanor  traffic  offense  does  not justify a custodial
arrest.

IV.  THE  POWER  TO ARREST  FOR  MINOR
TRAFFIC  OFFENSES  GRANTS  BROAD
AUTHORITY  TO  SEARCH  NEARLY  EVERY
AUTOMOBILE  MERELY  BECAUSE  IT IS ON
THE  HIGHWAY

"Very  few drivers  can  traverse  any appreciable  distance
without  violating  some  traffic  regulation."  B. James  George,
Constitutional  Limitations  on  Evidence  in Criminal  Cases  65
(1973  ed.)  Given  the extensive  regulation  of motor  vehicles
and their operation,  the Fifth Circuits's  en  banc decision
means  that an  officer  could  select  any motorist  he desires,
place him under  arrest  for a minor  traffic  violation,  a
violation  no  more  serious  than those  committed  by the bulk
of all automobiles  on  the highway,  and thereby  acquire  the .
right to search  the entire  car.  See New  York  v.  Belton,  453
U.S.  454  (1981)  (search  of cabin  of car  permitted  incident  to
lawful  arrest);  South  Dakota  v.  Opperman,  428  U.S.  364
(1976)  (inventory  séarch  of impounded  cars of arrested
drivers  permitted  provided  search  is consistent  with  standard
inventorying  procedures).  ~

Beyond  an  officer's  interest  in aggressively  engaging  in  -  -

"the  often  competitive  enterprise  of ferreting  out  crime,"
Johnson  v.  United  States,  333  U.S.  10, 13 (1948),  forfeiture
laws  have  created  a powerful  incentive  for officers  to inspect
as  many  automobiles  as  possible  for the presence  of drugs.
Even a relatively  small  amount  of unlawful  contraband
permits  the forfeiture  of an  automobile  carrying  the
contraband.  The effect  of this bounty  to the state  on  an
individual  officer  cannot  be overstated.  The  financial
existence  of many  of the drug interdiction  units  depends  on
the bounty  from  forfeiture.  Eric  Blumenson  and  Eva Nrisen;—-- -~- ~~
Policing for Profit:  The Drug  War's  Hidden  Agenda,  65 U.
Chi.  L. Rev.  35 (1998).

17

*  Counsel  of Record

Dated:  March  24, 2000

In United  States  v.  Freeman,  _  F.3d ___,  2000  FED

App.  0102P  (6th Cir.  March  23, 2000),  Judge Clay observed
"a troubling  pattern  or practice by some  members  of the
Shelby  County  Sheriff's  Department  drug interdiction  squad
of stopping  ‘target’  vehicles  on  questionable  probable  cause
grounds...  in order  to search  for contraband.”  The Fifth
Circuit's  opinion  in Atwater  would  make  such  practices  even
more  pervasive  as  the quantum  of suspicion  required  for the
stop would  alone justify the search.

The  Fifth  Circuit's  opinion  licenses  the search  of
virtually  every  car  on  the road  where  the state's  interest
consists  merely  of ensuring  the motorist  pays  a small  fine
and where  institutional  pressures  encourage  the officer  to
perform a full-scale  arrest  despite the minimal  infraction.

CONCLUSION

The decision  of United  States  Court  of Appeals  for

the Fifth  Circuit  misconstrued  this Court's  precedent
regarding  limitations  on  the manner  of seizure  an  officer  may
perform  once  he has probable  cause.  The  Fifth  Circuit's
opinion  highlights  a diversity  of approaches  taken  to the
question  of an  officer's  authority  to perform  a misdemeanor
arrest.  The  federal  constitution  forbids  a misdemeanor  arrest
for driving  without  a seat  belt  in California,  but not  Texas.
Vast degrees  of officer  discretion  to decide  whether a fine-
only misdemeanor  warrants  an  arrest  are  permitted  in some
states,  but not  others.  This  Court  should  grant certiorari  to
determine  whether  the citizenry  may  have  their  liberty
subjected  to the discretion  of officers  with  the power  to arrest
for any offense  --  a power  they now  have  in some
jurisdictions,  but not  others.

CAUSE  NO.  97-09063

§
GAIL  ATWATER,
INDIVIDUALLY,  and
§
MICHAEL  HAAS,  M.D.,  §
§
as  Next  Friend  of  Anya
§
Savannah  Haas  and
§
Mackinley  Xavier  Haas,
§
Plaintiffs,
§
:
§
,
THE  CITY  OF  LAGO
§
VISTA,  BART  TUREK;
§
and  FRANK  MILLER,
§
Defendants
§

IN  THE  DISTRICT
COURT
98th  JUDICIAL
DISTRICT

TRAVIS  COUNTY,
TEXAS

§SSSSSSSSSSSESSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS
PLAINTIFFS’  FIRST  AMENDED  PETITION
(Filed  Sept.  3,  1997)

TO  THE  HONORABLE  JUDGE  OF  SAID  COURT:

COME  NOW,  GAIL  ATWATER,  individually,  and
MICHAEL  HAAS,  M.D.,  as  Next  Friend  of  his  minor
children  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  now  age  6,  and
MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  now  age  4, as  Plaintiffs  in
the  above-styled  and  numbered  cause  of  action,  and  for
themselves  and  all  other  members  of  the  class  herein
described,  file  this,  their  First  Amended  Petition,  against
THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  BART  TUREK,  and  FRANK
MILLER,  pursuant  to 42  U.S.C.  21  §§1983,  1985,  and  1988,
the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  United
States  Constitution,  and  the  laws  of  the  State  of  Texas,
and  in  support  thereof  would  show  the  Court  as  follows:

14

I.

PARTIES

1.  Named  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER  and  MICHAEL
HAAS  are  individuals  residing  in  Lago  Vista,  Travis
County,  Texas;  Plaintiffs  are  husband  and  wife,  their
minor  children,  actual  parties  in  interest,  are  ANYA
SAVANNAH  HAAS,  now  age  6,  and  MACKINLEY
XAVIER  HAAS,  now  age  4; Plaintiff  MICHAEL  HAAS  is
qualified  to  serve  and  is not  disqualified  from  serving  as
Next  Friend  of  his  minor  children.

2.
Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  is  a  munici-
pality  located  [sic]  Travis  County,  Texas,  and  may  be
served  with  process  by serving  the  current  mayor,  Glen
Hartman,  at 5803  Thunderbird,  Lago  Vista,  Travis  County,
Texas  78645.

3.  Defendant  BART  TUREK  is,  and  at  all  times  men-
tioned  was,  a  resident  of  the  City  of  Lago  Vista,  Travis
County,  Texas.  At  all  times  relevant  to  this  action,  Defen-
dant  BART  TUREK  was  a  duly  appointed  and  acting
Police  Officer  appointed  by The  City  of Lago  Vista  Police
Department.  Defendant  BART  TUREK  may  be  served
_  with  process  at The  City of Lago  Vista  Police  Department,
7207  Bar  K Ranch  Road,  Lago  Vista,  Travis  County,  Texas
78645.

4.  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  was,  at  all  times  relevant
to  this  action,  the  Chief  of  Police  for  the  City  of  Lago
Vista  Police  Department.  FRANK  MILLER  may  be served
with  process  at The  City of Lago  Vista  Police  Department,
7207  Bar  K Ranch  Road,  Lago  Vista,  Travis  County,  Texas
78645.

15

5.  The  City  of Lago  Vista  has  actual  notice,  pursuant  to
all  relevant  statutes.

II.

JURISDICTION  AND  VENUE

a a ns Ee

6.  This  Court  has  jurisdiciion  over  the  controversy
because  it is within  the  jurisdictional  limits  of this  Court.

ee s

7.  This  Court  has  jurisdiction  over  the  Defendants  in
this  matter  because  all  defendants  are  residents  of  the
State  of  Texas.

8.  Venue  is proper  in Travis  County  because  the  [sic]  all
defendants  either  currently  reside  in  Travis  County,  or
resided  in  Travis  County  at  the  time  the  causes  of action
accrued,  and  because  all  of  the  events  giving  rise  to  the
Plaintiffs’  allegations  occurred  in  Travis  County.

Ill.

CLASS  ALLEGATIONS

9.  This  action  is  brought  by Plaintiffs  as  a  class  action,
on  their  own  behalf  and  on  behalf  of  all  others  similarly
situated,  under  the  provisions  of  Texas  Rule  of  Civil
Procedure  42  (a)  and  Texas  Rule  of  Civil  Procedure  42
(b)(4).

10.  The  class  so  represented  by Plaintiffs  in  this  action,
and  of which  Plaintiffs  are  members,  consists  of persons
who  have  been  subjected  to  detainment  without  probable
cause,  false  arrests,  false  imprisonments,  malicious  pros-
ecution,  and/or  excessive  use  of  force,  by police  officers
employed  by the  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Department,

16

17

who,  at  all  times  relevant  to  this  action,  were  acting
under  color  of law,  or  as  a  result  of  a municipal  policy,  or
as  a  result  of  custom  and/or  usage  of  such  policy.

11.  The  exact  number  of class  members,  here  identified
and  described,  is not  known,  but  it is estimated  that  there
are  no  less  than  500.  The  class  is so  numerous  that  joinder
of individual  members  is impractical.  Plaintiffs  allege  and
will  show  that  many  people  who  live  in  or  visit  Lago
Vista  live  in  fear  and  distrust  of  the  Lago  Vista  Police
Department.

12.  There  are  common  questions  of  law  and  fact  in  the
action  that  relate  to  affect  the  rights  of  each  member  of
the  class,  and  the  relief  sought  is  common  to  the  entire
class.  The  common  questions  of  law  and  fact  pertain  to
arrest,  detainment  and/or  seizure  of  individuals  without
probable  cause  or  exigent  circumstances,  unlawful  search
and  seizure,  false  arrest,  false  imprisonment,  malicious
prosecution,  and/or  excessive  use  of  force,  by  one  or
more  police  officers  employed  by the  City  of  Lago  Vista
Police  Department  and  supervised  by either  the  current
or  former  Chief  of Police  and  by other  officials  and  agents
of the  City  of Lago  Vista,  including  but  not  limited  to  the
mayor,  the  city  attorney,  and  the  city  manager.  At  all
times  relevant  to  this  action,  the  police  officers  were
acting  under  color  of  law,  or  as  a  result  of  a  municipal
policy,  or  as  a  result  of  custom  and/or  usage  of  such
policy.

13.  The  claims  of  Plaintiffs,  who  are  representatives  of
the  class  here,  are  typical  of the claims  of the class,  in that
the  claims  of  all  members  of  the  class,  including  Plain-
tiffs,  depend  on  the  showing  of  the  acts  or  omissions  of

Defendants  giving  rise  to the right  of Plaintiffs  to the
relief  sought  herein.  The named  Plaintiffs  do not know
and are unaware  of  any conflict  between  themselves  and
other  members  of the class  designated  with respect  to this
action,  or with respect  to the claims  for relief  set forth
herein.

14.  This action  is properly  maintained  as a  class action
inasmuch  as  the questions  of law  and  fact  common  to the
members  of  the  class  predominate  over  any  questions
affecting  only  individual  members,  and a class  action  is
superior  to  other  available  methods  for  the  fair  and  effi-
cient  adjudication  of  the  controversy.

15.  The  named  Plaintiffs  are  the  representative  parties
for  the  class,  and  are  able  to,  and  will,  fairly  and  ade-
quately  protect  the  interests  of the  class.  Charles  Edward
Lincoln,  attorney  for  Plaintiffs,  has  experience  in  and
knowledge  of the  field  of civil  rights  litigation,  both  as  a
judicial  law  clerk  in  the  United  States  District  Court  for
the  Southern  District  of  Florida,  as  an  extern  for  the
United  States  Court  of Appeals  for  the  Ninth  Circuit,  and
as  an  associate  of  other  law  firms.  Charles  Edward  Lin-
coln  will  actively  conduct  and  be  responsible  for  Plain-
tiffs’  case  here.

V.

FACTS  OF  NAMED  PLAINTIFFS’  CLAIM  WHICH
MAY  BE  TYPICAL  OF  CLASS  MEMBERS

23.  On  or  about  March  26,  1997,  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWA-
TER,  accompanied  by  her  two  minor  children,  ANYA
SAVANNA  HAAS  AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,
was  driving  on  the  public  streets  of  the  City  of  Lago
Vista,  when  Defendant  BART  TUREK  stopped  her  for  not
having  her  children  properly  seatbelted  in  the  vehicle.

24.  As  Defendant  BART  TUREK  approached  Plaintiff
GAIL  ATWATER’s  car,  he  began  yelling  at  her,  using
words  to  the  effect,  “We've  met  before!”  He  then  pro-
ceeded  to  stick  his  finger  in  her  face  and  state  “You're
going  to  jail!”  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER’s  first  words  to
Defendant  BART  TUREK  were  “Please  don’t  yell,”  as  his
rude  approach  and  loud,  abusive  voice  were  frightening
her  children,  at  that  time  aged  5 and  3.

25.  Defendant  BART  TUREK  called  for  back  up,  and
then  asked  for  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER’S  driver’s
license  and  insurance  information.  As  Plaintiff's  purse
had  been  stolen  the day before,  she  told  Defendant  BART
TUREK  that  she  did  not  have  them  with  her.  Defendant
BART  TUREK  only  laughed  at  her,  using  words  to  the
effect  of  “I’ve  only  heard  that  story  two-hundred  times.”

26.  Because  her  children  were  frightened,  upset  and
crying,  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER  asked  Defendant  BART
TUREK  if she  could  take  her  kids  to  her  friend’s  home,
which  was  approximately  two  houses  from  where  Defen-
dant  BART  TUREK  stopped  her.  Defendant  BART  TUREK
replied,  “You’re  not  going  anywhere.  You  don’t  care
about  these  kids  or  you  would  have  had  them  in  seat-
belts!”  By  this  time,  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER’S  friend,
who  had  been  alerted  to  the  situation  by  bystanders,
walked  up  and  took  the  children  to  her  house.

27.  At  this  point,  Defendant  BART  TUREK  continued  to
yell  at  and  browbeat  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER,  by mak-
ing statements  such  as  “You  just had  to get these  kids  in a
car  and  go  somewhere,  didn’t  you!  You  couldn’t  walk!
Why  didn’t  you  get  your  girlfriend  to  come  get  you?!
You're  going  to  jail!”  He  then  handcuffed  Plaintiff  GAIL
ATWATER  and  took  her  to  jail,  where  she  spent  approxi-
mately  one  hour.  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER’S  vehicle,
which  was  parked  only  two  houses  from  her  friend’s
house,  was  towed  to Cedar  Park.  At  no  time  was  Plaintiff
GAIL  ATWATER  informed  that  she  was  under  arrest,  nor
did  Defendant  BART  TUREK  inform  Plaintiff  GAIL
ATWATER  of her  “Miranda”  rights.  Plaintiff's  person  was
thus  unreasonably  and  unlawfully  seized,  in  violation  of
the  Fourth  and  Fifth  Amendments  to  the  United  States

22

23

Constitution,  incorporated  to  the  States  by the  Fourteenth
Amendment  to  the  United  States  Constitution.  The
Defendants  seized  Plaintiff  without  due  process  or  proba-
ble  cause,  also  in  violation  of  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth
Amendments.

SF

VI.

FIRST  CAUSE  OF  ACTION:
DEPRIVATION  OF  CONSTITUTIONAL  RIGHTS

28.  Paragraphs  (1)-(27)  of  this  First  Amended  Petition
are  incorporated  by reference  into  this  section  as  if fully
set  forth  herein.

29.
Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER,  individually,  and  Plaintiff
MICHAEL  HAAS,  M.D.,  as  Next  Friend  his  minor  chil-
dren  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS  and  MICHAEL  XAVIER
HAAS,  do  hereby  sue  and  bring  this  action  on  behalf  of
themselves  and  others  similarly  situated  against  Defen-
dants  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  BART  TUREK,  and
FRANK  MILLER  for  deprivation  of  constitutional  rights
within  the  meaning  of  42  U.S.C.  §1983.

30.
At  all  relevant  times,  Defendants  BART  TUREK  and
FRANK  MILLER  were  the  agents,  servants,  and/or
employees  of Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA.  At
all  relevant  times,  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  supervised
Defendant  BART  TUREK  as  part  of  his  duties  as  Police
Chief  of  the  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Department.

31.  The  above-described  actions  subjected  Plaintiffs  to  a
deprivation  of  rights,  privileges  and  immunities  secured
to  Plaintiffs  by  the  Constitution  and/or  laws  of  the
United  States,  including  the  due  process  clause  of  the

Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution  of
the  United  States,  within  the  meaning  of 42 U.S.C.  §1983,
and  the  right  to  be  free  from  unreasonable  seizure  pro-
vided  by  the  Fourth  Amendment,  incorporated  to  the
States  by the  Fourteenth  Amendment.

32.  As  a  direct  and  proximate  result  of the  above-men-
tioned  unconstitutional  acts  of Defendants  THE  CITY  OF
LAGO  VISTA,  BART  TUREK,  and  FRANK  MILLER,
Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,
AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  sustained  emotional
and  psychological  injuries,  as  well  as  loss  of time,  incon-
venience,  the  cost  of towing  her  vehicle,  and  other  costs
and  damages.  In all  likelihood,  as  a  direct  and  proximate
result  of the  above  described  actions  of these  Defendants,
Plaintiffs  will  continue  to  suffer  emotional  distress  and
mental  anguish  well  into  the  future.

24

constitutional  rights  within  the  meaning  of  42  U.S.C.

ATWATER  during  the  course  of  a  routine  traffic  stop.
Such  force  was  in no  manner  or  way  necessary  to conduct
this  traffic  stop  or  to  restrain  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER,
nor  were  there  any  other  exigent  or  extenuating  circum-
stances.

At  all  times  relevant  to  the  incident  in  question,
36.
Defendant  BART  TUREK  was  acting  within  the  course
and  scope  of  his  employment  and  authority  as  a  police
officer  for  Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA.

37.  Defendant’s  actions,  as  describe  [sic]  above,
deprived  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH
HAAS,  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  of  equal  pro-
tection  of the  law,  and  other  rights,  privileges  and  immu-
nities  protected  by the  United  States  Constitution  and/or
other  laws.  As  such,  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA
SAVANNAH  HAAS,  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,
are  entitled  to  recover  their  actual,  consequential,  and
special  damages  from  Defendants  BART  TUREK  and  the
CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  pursuant  to  42  U.S.C.  §1983,  42
U.S.C.  §1985,  and  42  U.S.C.  §1988.

38.  Additionally,  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA
SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,
are  entitled  to  recover  against  Defendants  BART  TUREK
and  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  pursuant  to  the  Texas
Tort  Claims  Act,  Texas  Civil  Practice  and  Remedies  Code,
§101.001  et  seq.

39.
In  committing  the  acts  alleged  above,  Defendant
BART  TUREK  either  acted  intentionally,  recklessly,  wan-
tonly  and/or  with  malice  toward  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWA-
TER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKINLEY
XAVIER  HAAS,  thus  entitling  Plaintiffs  to  an  award  of
exemplary  damages.  Defendant  BART  TUREK’s  actions
were  not  the  result  of  mere  momentary  thoughtlessness
or  inattention,  but  constituted  that  kind  of  conduct  for
which  the  law  provides  punishment.

was  under  arrest,  Defendant  BART  TUREK  placed  her

ATWATER  free  to  leave,  or  even  to  make  adequate  provi-
sions  to  take  care  of her  children.  At  no  time  in  question
did  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER  commit  an  offense  justify-
ing  an  arrest.

43.
At  all  relevant  times,  Defendants  BART  TUREK  and
FRANK  MILLER  were  the  agents,  servants,  and/or
employees  of Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA.  At
all  relevant  times,  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  supervised
Defendant  BART  TUREK  as  part  of  his  duties  as  Police
Chief  of  the  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Department.

OF  LAGO  VISTA,  with  the  knowledge  and  consent  of

dants.

GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND

duty  properly  to  train  and  instruct  the  police  officers

cers,  including  Defendant  BART  TUREK,  constitute  negli-

50.  The  above-described  actions  subjected  Plaintiffs  to  a

to  Plaintiffs  by  the  Constitution  and/or  laws  of  the
United  States,  including  the  right  to  be  free  from  unrea-
sonable  seizure  secured  by  the  Fourth  Amendment  and
the  rights  provided  by the due  process  clauses  of the  Fifth
and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  Constitution  of  the
United  States,  within  the  meaning  of  42  U.S.C.  §1983.

51.  As  a  direct  and  proximate  result  of  the  above-men-
tioned  unconstitutional  acts  of Defendants  THE  CITY  OF
LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER,  Plaintiffs  GAIL
ATWATER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKIN-
LEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  sustained  emotional  and  psychologi-
cal  injuries,  as  well  as  loss  of  time,  inconvenience,  the
costs  of wrongfully  towing  her  car,  and  other  and  further
costs  and  damages.  In  all  likelihood,  as  a  direct  and
proximate  result  of  the  above  described  actions  of  these
Defendants,  Plaintiffs  will  continue  to  suffer  mental
anguish  well  into  the  future.

x.

TO SUPERVISE
"FAILURE

§101.001  et.  seq.

54.  At  all  times  relevant  to  this  Complaint,  Defendant
FRANK  MILLER  was  responsible  for  supervising  Defen-
dant  BART  TUREK.  Furthermore,  at  all  times  relevant  to
this  complaint,  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  acted  as  the
agent,  servant,  or  employee  of  Defendant  THE  CITY  OF
LAGO  VISTA.

55.  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  had a duty,  as  Chief  of
Police,  properly  to  supervise  the  police  officers  working
for  The  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Department,  including
Defendant  BART  TUREK,  to  prevent  the  police  officers
from  violating  the  constitutional  rights  of  the  citizens  of
Lago  Vista.

30

31

56.  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  was  negligent  and/or
grossly  negligent  in  fulfilling  his  duty  to  properly  super-
vise  the  police  officers  of  the  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police
Department,  including  Defendant  BART  TUREK.  Said
negligence  and  gross  negligence  constituted  a  pattern  or
practice  on  the  part  of  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER.

57.  Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  although
aware  of Defendant  FRANK  MILLER’S  improper  supervi-
sion  of  the  police  officers  under  his  command,  failed  to
take  any  corrective  action.  Said  actions  by  Defendant
THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  constitute  negligence  and
gross  negligence.

58.  The  above-described  actions  subjected  Plaintiffs  to  a
deprivation  of  rights,  privileges  and  immunities  secured
to  Plaintiffs  by  the  Constitution  and/or  laws  of  the
United  States,  including  the  right  to  be  free  from  unrea-
sonable  seizure  secured  by  the  Fourth  Amendment  and
the  rights  secured  by the  due  process  clauses  of the  Fifth
and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  Constitution  of  the
United  States,  within  the  meaning  of  42  U.S.C.  §1983.

59.  As  a  direct  and  proximate  result  of  the  above-
mentioned  unconstitutional  acts  of Defendants  THE  CITY
OF  LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER,  Plaintiffs  GAIL
ATWATER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACK-
INLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  sustained  emotional  and  psycho-
logical  injuries,  as  well  as  loss  of time  and  inconvenience,

and  other  and  further  costs  and  damages.  In  all  likeli-
hood,  as  a  direct  and  proximate  result  of  the  above
described  actions  of these  Defendants,  Plaintiffs  will  con-
tinue  to  suffer  mental  anguish  well  into  the  future.

XI.
SIXTH  CAUSE  OF  ACTION:  INTENTIONAL
OF EMOTIONAL  DISTRESS
-INFLICTION

60.  Paragraphs  (1)-(59)  above  are  incorporated  by refer-
ence  as  if  fully  set  forth  herein.

61.  This  Cause  of Action  is brought  by Plaintiff’  GAIL
ATWATER,  INDIVIDUALLY,  AND  MICHAEL  HAAS,
M.D.,  AS  NEXT  FRIEND  OF  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS
AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  on  behalf  of  them-
selves  and  of  all  persons  similarly  situated,  against  the
Defendants  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  BART  TUREK,  and
FRANK  MILLER,  for  intentional  infliction  of  emotional
distress  pursuant  to  the  Texas  Tort  Claims  Act,  Texas
Civil  Practice  and  Remedies  Code,  §101.001  et.  seq.,  and
the  common  law  of  the  State  of  Texas.

62.  At  all  relevant  times,  Defendants  BART  TUREK  and
FRANK  MILLER  were  the  agents,  servants,  and/or
employees  of Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA.  At
all relevant  times,  Defendant  FRANK  MILLER  supervised
Defendant  BART  TUREK  as  part  of  his  duties  as  Police
Chief  of  the  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Department.

63.  Defendant  BART  TUREK  acted  intentionally  or  reck-
lessly  by harassing,  humiliating,  browbeating,  handcuff-
ing,  and  improperly  forcing  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER  to
go  to  the  Lago  Vista  Police  Station,  and  by  violating
Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER’S  civil  rights  as  discussed
above.  Furthermore,  he  terrorized  her  children  ANYA
SAVANNAH  HAAS  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS  by
unjustly  and  unreasonably  screaming  at  their  mother  in
front  ot them,  all the while  knowing  how  much  this  upset
the  children;  suggesting,  in  front  of  the  children,  that

32

their  mother  did  not  care  about  them;  and  threatening  to
take  their  mother  to jail in  front  of them.  He  additionally
traumatized  them  by not  allowing  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWA-
TER  to  remove  her  children  from  this  frightening  situa-
tion  when  she  reasonably  asked  if she  could  do  so.  Said
conduct  was  extreme  and  outrageous,  especially  for  offi-
cers  sworn  to  uphold  the  law  and  protect  the  citizens  of
this  State,  and  should  be  regarded  as  atrocious  and
utterly  intolerable  in  a  civilized  community.  His  actions
directly  and  proximately  caused  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWA-
TER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKINLEY
XAVIER  HAAS,  to  suffer  severe  emotional  distress,  for
which  they  are  entitled  to  recover  damages.

64.  Defendants  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK
MILLER  acted  intentionally  or  recklessly  in the  manner  in
which  they  supervised  and/or  trained  their  police  offi-
cers,  including  Defendant  BART  TUREK.  These  Defen-
dants  knew  of  and/or  condoned  the  reprehensible  acts
committed  by  several  police  officers  employed  by  The
City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Department,  including  Defen-
dant  BART  TUREK,  and  yet they did  nothing  to stop  it.  In
fact,  their  actions  only  encouraged  the  officers  to  violate
more  constitutional  rights  of the  citizens  of Lago  Vista,  all
in  the  name  of  upholding  the  law.  Said  conduct  is
extreme  and  outrageous,  and  utterly  intolerable  in  a  civi-
lized  community.  The  actions  of these  Defendants  directly
and  proximately  caused  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,
ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER
HAAS,  to  suffer  severe  emotional  distress,  for which  they
are  entitled  to  recover  damages.

XII.
SEVENTH  CAUSE  OF  ACTION:

ASSAULT  & B

65.  Paragraphs  (1)-(64)  are  hereby  incorporated  by refer-
ence  as  if fully  set  forth  herein.

66.
This  cause  of  action  is  brought  by  Plaintiff  GAIL
ATWATER,  INDIVIDUALLY,  and  MICHAEL  HAAS,
M.D.,  AS  NEXT  FRIEND  OF  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS
AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  on  behalf  of  them-
selves  and  of  all  persons  similarly  situated,  against
Defendants  BART  TUREK  AND  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO
VISTA.

67.  On  or  about  March  26,  1997,  Defendant  BART
TUREK,  an  agent,  employee,  and/or  servant  of  Defen-
dant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  intentionally  or  know-
ingly  threatened  to  cause  and  in  fact  caused  physical
contact  between  himself  and  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER  by
touching  her  or  laying  his  hands  upon  her  for  the  pur-
pose  of depriving  her  of her  freedom  of  movement,  pre-
tending  at all  times  as  if he, BART  TUREK,  were  acting  to
uphold  the  law.  Defendant  BART  TUREK  knew,  or  rea-
sonably  should  have  known,  that  no  emergency  or  exi-
gent  circumstances  justified  his  conduct,  in  that  Plaintiff
GAIL  ATWATER  at  no  time  posed  any  threat  to  BART
TUREK’s  person,  or  to  the  peace  and  public  order  of
LAGO  VISTA.  Defendant  BART  TUREK  further  knew
that  such  touching  and/or  physical  contact  would  be
offensive  to  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER,  in  addition  to  the
injuries  caused  by  the  handcuffing  and  deprivation  of
freedom  of  movement,  as  described  above  (See  “False

34

Imprisonment,”  Third  Cause  of  Action,  supra).  Further-
more,  Defendant  BART  TUREK  knew  or  should  have
known  that  said  offensive  contact  would  be  extremely
upsetting  to  the  minor  children,  Plaintiffs  ANYA  SAVAN-
NAH  HAAS  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,  who  at
the  time  were  5 and  3 years  old,  respectively.

68.
At  all  times  relevant  to  this  incident,  Defendant
BART  TUREK  was  acting  under  color  of  law,  and  in  the
curse  and  scope  of his  employment  as  a  police  officer  for
Defendant  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA.

69.  As  a  direct  and  proximate  result  of  this  assault  by
Defendant  BART  TUREK,  Plaintiff  GAIL  ATWATER  was
humiliated  and  in  front  of  her  children  and  members  of
her  community,  and  was  unwarrantedly  subjected  to  the
extreme  embarrassment  and  humiliation  of being  seized
and  taken  to  jail.  Furthermore,  Plaintiffs  ANYA  SAVAN-
NAH  HAAS  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS  were
severely  traumatized  by  watching  Defendant  BART
TUREK  assault  and  falsely  arrest  and  imprison  their
mother.

70.
Plaintiff’  GAIL  ATWATER,  as  well  as  Plaintiffs
ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER
HAAS,  minor  children  represented  here  by  their  father
and  Next  Friend  MICHAEL  HAAS,  M.D.,  are  entitled  to
recover  damages  from  Defendants  BART  TUREK  AND
THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  for  the  emotional  and  psy-
chological  injuries  they  sustained  as  a  direct  and  proxi-
mate  result  of  this  assault  and  battery.

71.  Defendants’  actions,  as  described  above,  deprived
Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER  and  her  minor  children  ANYA
SAVANNAH  HAAS  and  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS,

represented  by their father  and Next  Friend  MICHAEL
HAAS, M.D., of the equal protection  of the law, and other
rights,  privileges  and  immunities  protected  by the United
States  Constitution  and/or  other  laws.  As such,  Plaintiffs
GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND
MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS  are  entitled  to recover  dam-
ages  against  Defendants  BART  TUREK  AND  THE  CITY
OF LAGO  VISTA,  pursuant  to 42 U.S.C.  §§1983,  1985,  and
1988.

72.  Additionally,  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,  ANYA
SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER  HAAS
are  entitled  to recover  damages  against  Defendants  BART
TUREK  AND  THE  CITY  OF LAGO  VISTA  pursuant  to the
Texas  Tort  Claims  Act,  Texas  Civil  Practice  and  Remedies
Code,  §§101.001  et.  seq.

73.
In  committing  the  acts  described  above,  Defendant
BART  TUREK  acted  intentionally,  recklessly,  wantonly,
and/or  with  malice  toward  Plaintiffs  GAIL  ATWATER,
ANYA  SAVANNAH  HAAS,  AND  MACKINLEY  XAVIER
HAAS,  thus  entitling  Plaintiffs  to  an  award  of exemplary
damages.

forth  and  copied  herein.

MICHAEL  HAAS,  as  Next  Friend  of  his  minor  children

Plaintiffs  will  show  that  so  many  complaints  have
76.
been  lodged  against  BART  TUREK  that  his  incompetence
and  unsuitability  to  serve  in  the  Lago  Vista  Police  Depart-
ment  were  actually  known  or  should  in  the  exercise  of
minimal  diligence  have  been  well-known  to  the  Defen-
dants  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER.

77.
Plaintiffs  will  show  that,  in  a  June  19,  1997  news-
paper  article  quoting  the  Defendants  or  their  agents
herein,  concerning  the  Defendants’  handling  of the  events
described  above  which  gave  rise  to  this  lawsuit,  Defen-
dants  MILLER  and  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  referred  to  the
Defendant  BART  TUREK  as  one  of  “the  finest  young
officers  I have  seen  in  my  entire  career.”

78.  The  attitude  and  policy  of  Defendants  CITY  OF
LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER  evinces  no  mere  inat-
tention  or  momentary  thoughtlessness,  but  a  purposeful
disregard  of  the  rights  and  dignity  of  the  residents  of
Lago  Vista  and  the  citizens  of  the  State  of  Texas.  In  sum
the  behavior  of  Defendants  is  that  kind  of  wilful  and
grossly  negligent  conduct  for  which  the  law  of  the  State
of  Texas  allows  punitive  and  exemplary  damages.

79.  A  municipality  and  police  department  of  reasonable
prudence  would  not  have  hired  or  retained  Defendant
BART  TUREK.

|

80.  As  a  direct  and  proximate  result  of  the  negligent
hiring  and  retention  of  Defendant  BAR  [sic]  TUREK  by
Defendants  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER,
.  Plaintiffs,  including  all  class  members  have  suffered

XIV.

NINTH  CAUSE  OF  ACTION:
CONSPIRACY  TO  FORMULATE  AND
ENFORCE  A  MUNICIPAL  POLICY
TO  VIOLATE  CONSTITUTIONAL  RIGHTS

Plaintiffs  reallege  each  and  every,  all  and  singular,  of
81.
the  facts  stated  heretofore  in  paragraphs  (1)-(80)  above  as
if  the  same  were  fully  copied  and  restated  herein.

82.
Plaintiffs  aliege  that  Defendants  CITY  OF  LAGO
VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER  did  agree  among  themselves
and,  with  a  meeting  of  the  minds  and  a joint  purpose,
conspire  to  institute  and  maintain  a  municipal  policy  of
police  abuse  in  general  disregard  and  derogation  of  the
rights  guaranteed  to  the  people  of  the  United  States  by
the  Fourth,  Fifth,  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the
United  States  Constitution,  in  violation  of  42  U.S.C.
§1983.

83.
Plaintiffs  allege  that  Defendants  CITY  OF  LAGO
VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER  employed  Defendant  BART
TUREK  as  a  willing  agent  and  instrument  of  this  munici-
pal  policy.

Plaintiffs  allege  and  will  show  that  the  CITY  OF
84.
LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER  further  conspired
with  each  other  to  disguise  and  cover  up  their  policy  by

38

39

making,  by and  through  their  agents,  such  as  that  made
by a  certain  city  attorney  BARNEY  KNIGHT,  that  “losses
from  traffic  outweigh  those  of  crime.”

85.
Plaintiffs  allege  and  will  show  that  many  individuals
in  Lago  Vista  live  in  fear  and  distrust  of  the  Defendants
CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  and  its  police  department  under
Defendant  Chief  FRANK  MILLER.

86.
Plaintiffs  allege  and  will  show  that  they,  individu-
ally,  and  all  persons  similarly  situated  have  suffered  and
continue  to  suffer  actual,  consequential,  and  special  dam-
ages  from  the  municipal  policy  of  the  Defendants  CITY
OF  LAGO  VISTA  and  FRANK  MILLER,  in  excess  of  the
minimum  jurisdictional  limits  of  this  Court.

87.  The  conduct  of  the  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  and
FRANK  MILLER,  in  this  connection,  is the  kind  of wilfull
[sic]  and  intentional  conduct  for  which  the  law  of  the
State  of  Texas  provides  punitive  and/or  exemplary  con-
duct  to  punish  such  conduct  as  the  conspiracy  herein
alleged,  and  to  deter  such  conduct  in  the  future.

XV.

TENTH  CAUSE  OF  ACTION:  COMMON  FUND

Plaintiffs  reallege  each  and  every,  all  and  singular,  of
88.
the  facts  stated  heretofore  in  paragraphs  (1)-(87)  above  as
if  the  same  were  fully  copied  and  restated  herein.

89.
Plaintiffs,  by filing  this  their  First  Amended  Petition
against  the  Defendants  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  FRANK
MILLER,  and  BART  TUREK,  seek  to  create  a  common
fund  or  common  resource  for  the  benefit  of all  victims  of
civil  rights  abuse  and  illegal  activities  by the  Lago  Vista

Police  Department.  This  would  include  victims  of  police
abuse,  harassment,  mistreatment,  false  arrest,  false
imprisonment,  and/or  use  of  excessive  or  unreasonable
force,  and  all  other  persons  situated  similar'y  to  the
Plaintiffs.

90.
Plaintiffs  allege  and  will  show  that  the  CITY  OF
LAGO  VISTA,  FRANK  MILLER,  and  BART  TUREK  have
each  individually  and  in  concert  with  one  another  acted
in  with  malice  and  in  bad  faith  towards  all  victims  of
police  abuse  and  harassment.  Furthermore,  these  defen-
dants  lack  just  cause  in  the  treatment  of  the  citizens  and
visitors  to  Lago  Vista  in  the  first  place,  and  it  would  be
equitable,  fair,  and  just  to  grant  to  Plaintiffs  their  attor-
ney’s  fees  in  this  matter  incurred.

PRAYER  FOR  RELIEF

WHEREFORE,  PREMISES  CONSIDERED,  Plaintiffs
move,  request,  and  pray,  for  themselves  and  all  other
members  of  the  class,  that  Defendants  be  cited  to  appear
and  answer,  that  as  a  preliminary  matter  the  class  of
persons  deprived  by the  Lago  Vista  Police  Department  of
their  constitutional  rights  under  color  of  law  be  certified
for  purposes  of bringing  this  class  action,  that  a common
fund  be  created  for  all  victims  of  police  abuse  in  Lago
Vista,  Texas,  and  that  on  final  trial,  Plaintiffs  have  the
following:

1.  Judgment  against  Defendants  for  actual
damages  and  consequential  damages  in  an
amount  in  excess  of  the  minimum  jurisdic-
tional  limits  of  this  Court;

2.  Special  damages;

40

Exemplary  damages  in  an  amount  sufficient
to  punish  Defendants  and  to  deter  such  mis-
conduct  in  the  future;

Further  damages  by  reason  of  Defendants’
knowing  and  willful  misconduct;

Prejudgment  interest  and  postjudgment
interest  as  provided  by  law;

Attorney's  fees;

Costs  of  suit;

An  order  determining  how  damages  are  to
be  distributed  among  class  members;  and

All  such  other  and  further  relief,  at  law  or
in  equity,  to  which  Plaintiffs  may  show
themselves  to  be  justly  entitled.

SPECIAL  EXCEPTIONS  AND ORIGINAL  ANSWER
OF  DEFENDANTS  THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA

~~BART  TUREK AND FRANK  MILLER

(Filed  Sept.  8,  1997)

TO  THE  HONORABLE  JUDGE  OF  SAID  COURT:

THE  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA  (“Defendant  City”),
BART  TUREK  (Defendant  “Turek”)  and  FRANK  MILLER
(“Defendant  Miller”)  make  and  file  their  Special  Excep-
tions  And  Original  Answer  To  Plaintiffs’  Original  Peti-
tion,  and  would  respectfully  show  unto  the  Court  the
following:

SPECIAL  EXCEPTIONS

I.

Defendant  City  excepts  and  objects  to  Plaintiffs’
Original  Petition  and  any  and  all  causes  of action  therein
in  their  entirety  because  they  fail  to  state  a  proper  cause
of action  for  which  recovery  is warranted.  Defendant  City
is  a governmental  entity.  Consequently,  Plaintiffs’  allega-
tions  as  to  Defendant  City  must  comport  with  the  Texas

42

Tort  Claims  Act,  the  Texas  Constitution,  and/or  common
law  proprietary  functions.  Plaintiffs’  allegations  as  to
Defendant  City  fail  to  comply  with  the  law.  Defendant
City  respectfully  requests  that  Plaintiffs  properly  plead
their  cause  of  action  pursuant  to  the  law  and  the  Texas
Rules  of  Civil  Procedure.

Defendant  City excepts  and  objects  to  Plaintiffs’  dam-
ages  claims  that  do  not  comport  with  the  Texas  Tort
Claims  Act.  Said  Act  allows  recovery  only  for  personal
injury  and/or  death.  Defendant  City  respectfully  requests
that  Plaintiffs  properly  plead  their  cause  of  action  pur-
suant  to  the  law  and  the  Texas  Rules  of Civil  Procedure.

Defendants  City,  Reid  and  Miller  except  and  object  to
Plaintiffs’  Original  Petition  in  its  entirety  because  it fails
to specify  the  total  amount  for which  Plaintiffs  sue  Defen-
dants,  in  violation  of  Rules  47  and  56  of  the  Texas  Rules
of  Civil  Procedure.

IV.

GENERAL  DENIAL

Defendant  City,  Defendant  Turek  and  Defendant  Mil-
ler  generally  deny  each  and  every,  all  and  singular,  the
allegations  contained  in  Plaintiffs’  Original  Petition,  and
upon  their  denials,  demand  strict  proof  by  a  prepon-
derance  of  the  credible  evidence.

43

AFFIRMATIVE  DEFENSE

Vv.

Defendant  City  affirmatively  pleads  the  exemptions
from  liability  contained  in  Sections  101.021,  101.022,
101.026,  101.055,  101.056  and  101.057  of  the  Texas  Civil
Practice  &  Remedies  Code.

Vi.

Defendant  City  would  show  that  liability  of  Defen-
dant  City  in  this  case,  if  any,  is  limited  pursuant  to
Sections  101.023  and  101.024  of the  Texas  Civil  Practice  &
Remedies  Code.

Vil.

Defendants  Turek  and  Miller  would  show  that  at  all
times  relevant  to  the  occurrence  described  in  Plaintiffs’
pleadings,  Defendants  were  acting  in  their  official  capaci-
ties  for The  City  of Lago  Vista.  Defendants  were  perform-
ing  discretionary  functions  in  good  faith,  and  therefore,
are  immune  from  suit  and  liability  under  the  doctrine  of
official  immunity.

Vill.

Defendants  City,  Turek  and  Miller  allege,  by way  of
affirmative  defense,  that  the  conduct  of Gail  Atwater  and
Michael  Haas,  M.D.,  whether  acting  singly  or  together,  or
in  concert  with  others,  was  the  sole  proximate  cause,  or
alternatively,  a  proximate  cause  of  Plaintiffs’  injuries,  if
any.  Defendants  City,  Turek  and  Miller  allege  that  the

45

foregoing  and  attached  Special  Exceptions  And  Original
Answer  Of  Defendants  The  City  Of  Lago  Vista,  Bart
Turek  And  Frank  Miller  has  been  provided  to the offices
of:

)

by Certified  Mail  Return  Receipt  Requested,  by facsimile

with  the  Texas  Rules  of Civil  Procedure,  on  9/8/97,  1997.

/s/  William  W.  Krueger  III
William  W.  Krueger  III

44

conduct  of Plaintiffs  at  the  time  and  on  the  occasion  made
the  subject  of this  lawsuit  resulted  in  the  injuries  claimed
herein  by Plaintiffs,  and  that  said  conduct  was  willful  and
intentional,  and  caused  Plaintiffs’  injuries.

WHEREFORE,  PREMISES  CONSIDERED,  Defen-
dants,  CITY  OF  LAGO  VISTA,  BART  TUREK  and  FRANK
MILLER,  pray  this  matter  be  set  for  hearing  with  proper
notice  to  all  parties;  and  upon  hearing,  that  the  Court
sustain  Defendants’  Special  Exceptions;  and  that  judg-
ment  subsequently  enter  in  favor  of said  Defendants;  and
for  such  other  and  further  relief  to  which  these  Defen-
dants  may  be  justly  entitled.

QUESTION  PRESENTED

Petitioner  Gail  Atwater  was  placed  under  custodial
arrest for not wearing  a seat belt, a  misdemeanor  that carries  a
fifty-dollar  fine  as  its  maximum  penalty.  Does  the  Fourth
Amendment  limit  the  use  of custodial  arrests  for  fine-only
traffic  offenses?

OPINIONS  BELOW

The decision  of the United  States  Court  of Appeals  for
the Fifth  Circuit,  sitting en  banc,  is reported  as Atwater  v.  City
of Lago  Vista,  195  F.3d  242  (Sth Cir.  1999)  (en banc),  and  is
reproduced  in Appendix A to the petition  for writ of certiorari.

The  decision  of  the  three-judge  panel  of  the  Fifth
Circuit  is reported  as Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,  165  F.3d
380  (Sth  Cir.  1999),  and  is reproduced  in Appendix  B to  the
petition  for writ  of certiorari.

The  order  of the  United  States  District  Court  for  the
Western  District  of Texas  was  not published,  but is reproduced
in Appendix C to the petition  for writ of certiorari.

JURISDICTION

The  court  of  appeals’  judgment  was  entered  on
November  24,  1999.  The jurisdiction  of this Court  is invoked
under  28  U.S.C.  § 1254(1).  Petitioners’  petition  for  writ  of
certiorari  was  filed  on  February  22,  2000,  and  this  Court
granted  the petition  on  June  26, 2000.

CONSTITUTIONAL  AND  STATUTORY  PROVISIONS

The  Fourth  Amendment  to the Constitution  provides:

The  right  of the  people  to  be secure  in their  persons,
houses,  papers,  and  effects,  against  unreasonable
searches  and seizures,  shall  not be violated,  and no
Warrants  shall  issue,  but  upon  probable  cause,
supported  by  Oath  or  affirmation,  and  particularly
describing  the place  to be searched,  and the persons  or
to be seized.
things

ap

—— —_———— OO OE —

Tex.  Transp.  Code  Ann.  §  545.413  (West  1999)
(subsequently  amended  effective  Sept.  1, 1999).

The statute  is set out, in relevant  part, in the petition  for

writ  of certiorari  at p. 2.

STATEMENT  OF THE  CASE

This case  concerns  a constitutionally  unreasonable  use
of  a  full  custodial  arrest  for  a  fine-only  traffic  offense.
Following a traffic  stop,  a Lago  Vista  police  officer  placed
petitioner Gail Atwater  under  full custodial  arrest  for violating
a  Texas statute  that requires  the use of seat belts.

L.

The  Facts

Gail  Atwater  and her family have been  residents  of the
small  town  of Lago  Vista,  Texas  for sixteen  years.  (R. 376).
Ms.  Atwater  is a  full-time  mother  and  her  husband,  Michael
Haas,  is an  emergency  room  physician  at a local  hospital.  (R.
376-77).  Their  daughter  Anya  was  almost  six  years  old  and
their  son Mac  was almost  four  years  old at the time  of this
incident.  (R. 377).

On the afternoon  of March  26, 1997, Gail  Atwater  was
driving  Anya  and  Mac  home  from  soccer  practice.
After
leaving  the soccer  field,  one of the children  realized  they had
lost a favorite  toy,  a winged  bat made  of rubber  with  a suction
cup that had been affixed  to the outside  of the truck  window.
(R. 380).  The children  asked their mother if they could  look  for
it, so  Ms.  Atwater  back-tracked  along the route  they had taken
to drive  to the soccer  field.  The children  had previously  been
wearing  seat  belts, but Ms.  Atwater  permitted  them  to take off
their belts  so they could  look  out the truck’s  windows  in  search
(R.  380).  There  was  no  traffic  on  the
of their  lost  toy.

residential  street  near  their  home  on  which  they were  driving,
and their speed was  approximately  fifteen  miles-per-hour.  (R.
380).

Respondent  Bart  Turek,  a  Lago  Vista  police  officer,
observed  the seat-belt  violation  and stopped  them.  While  Ms.
Atwater and her children remained  in her vehicle, Officer Turek
approached,  then  stood  by  the  driver's  window  and
aggressively jabbed  his finger toward  her face.  Officer  Turek
screamed  either  that  they had  met  before  or  that they had  had
this  conversation  before.
Officer
Turek’s  conduct  frightened  Ms.  Atwater’s  children  and  they
began to cry;  she then  calmly  and  in  a normal  tone  of voice
requested  that  Officer  Turek  lower  his  voice.  (R.  382,  703).
Officer  Turek  responded  by telling  Ms.  Atwater  that  she  was
going to jail.  (R. 382).

(R.  381-82,  384,  703).

There  is no  evidence  in this  summary  judgment  record
indicating  in  any  way  that  Gail  Atwater  was  sarcastic  or
belligerent,  or  that  she  challenged  Officer  Turek’s  authority.
(R. 388-89,  414).  Ms.  Atwater  was  not  under the influence  of
drugs  or  alcohol.  She  was  not  acting  suspicious  in any  way,
she did  not pose  any threat  to Officer  Turek,  and she was not
engaged  in any illegal conduct  in the officer’s  view,  other than
her  violation  of the  seat  belt  law,  when  he  announced  his
intention  to arrest  her.

Despite  the  lack  of any  provocation,  Officer  Turek
continued  to  speak  to  Gail  Atwater  in  a  verbally  abusive
her of not caring  for her children.  (R. 384-85,
manner,  accusing
Ms.  Atwater’s  children  and  several
388-89,  704-05).
bystanders,  including  friends  and  neighborhood  residents,
witnessed  the incident.  (R. 382, 385,  393, 399-400,  405).
