STATEMENT  OF INTEREST  OF AMICI  CURIAE'

The National  Association  of Criminal  Defense  Lawyers
(NACDL)  is a professional  bar association  founded  in 1958
for the purpose  of advancing  the mission  of the nation's
criminal  defense  lawyers  to ensure  justice  and due process
for persons  accused  of a crime  or other misconduct.  Today,
NACDL  has almost  10,000 members  and 80 affiliates
representing  another  28,000 members,  who  include  private
criminal  defense  lawyers,  public defenders,  active  U.S.
military  personnel  and law professors.  NACDL  has
members  in all fifty states,  and the American  Bar Association
recognizes  NACDL  as an  affiliate  organization  and awards  it
full representation  in the House  of Delegates.

Among  NACDL's  objectives  is to deter overreaching
conduct  by law enforcement  officers  by vigorously  defending
the protections  guaranteed  by the Constitution.  NACDL
urges  this Court  to reverse  the decision  of the United  States
Court  of Appeals  for the Fifth Circuit  as the lower court
decision  provides  for vast  officer discretion,  permitting  the
overzealous  officer  to arrest  any person  he chooses  for the
pettiest of offenses.  The Fourth  Amendment  is designed  to
constrain  the discretion  of the officer  in the field and assure
that his conduct  in seizing citizens,  when permissible,  is
reasonable.  The Fifth Circuit  decision  provides  neither
assurance.  Accordingly,  NACDL  files this brief on behalf of
the Petitioners.

The Association  of Federal  Defenders  ("AFD")  was
formed  in 1995  to enhance  the representation  under  the

'  Both parties have consented  to the filing of this brief.  No counsel for
any party has authored  this brief in whole or in part, and no person  or
entity,  other than amici,  has made a  monetary  contribution  to the
or submission  of this brief.
preparation

Criminal  Justice  Act,  18 U.S.C.  § 3006A,  and the Sixth
Amendment  of the United  States  Constitution.  AFD  is a
nationwide,  non-profit,  volunteer  organization  whose
membership  includes  attorneys  and support  staff of Federal
Defender  Offices.  One of the AFD's  missions  is to file
amicus  briefs  to ensure  that the position  of indigent
defendants  in the criminal  justice system  is adequately
represented.  We believe  that this mission  calls  upon  us  to
join in asking the Supreme  Court  to reverse  the lower  court's
decision  in the instant  case.  Although  it is a civil  nghts
action  rather  than  a criminal  case,  the instant  case  presents  an
issue  of great  importance  in criminal  law:  whether  the Fourth
Amendment  prohibits  the use  of custodial  arrests  for fine-
only traffic  offenses.  A full custodial  arrest  invades  one's
privacy  interests  and one's  dignity,  and can  often  lead  to
criminal  prosecution  on  other charges.  It is in the interest  of
our  clients  to urge  this Court  to prohibit  custodial  arrests  for
ordinary  traffic  offenses.

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

This  Court's  Fourth  Amendment  doctrines  prohibit  an

officer  who  issues a traffic  citation  from  searching  the
motorist's  vehicle  absent  consent  or probable  cause  of other
wrongdoing.  Knowles  v.  Jowa,  525  U.S.  113  (1998).  During
the stop,  the officer  is further  prohibited  from  patting down
the motorist,  or  the interior  of her vehicle,  for weapons
without  reasonable  suspicion  to believe  the motorist  is
armed.  Michigan  v.  Long, 463  U.S.  1032,  1049  (1983);
Terry v.  Ohio,  392  U.S.  1 (1968).  If the search  of the
stopped  motorist's  car  is to be based  on  consent,  that consent
must  be voluntary.  Schneckloth  v.  Bustamonte,  412  U.S.
218, 223  (1973);  Bumper  v.  North  Carolina,  391  U.S.  543,
548-50  (1968).

The  lower court's  opinion  in the instant  case  permits  an
officer,  upon  the mere  observation  of a traffic  offense  to take

the motorist  into custody.  Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,  195
F.3d  242, 244-45  (Sth Cir.  1999) (en banc).  As a result  of
this arrest,  a search  of the motorist's  person  is permitted  as
well  as a search  of the cabin  of the automobile.  Chimel  v.
California,  395  U.S.  752, 763  (1969);  New  York v.  Belton,
453  U.S.  454 (1981).  Neither  consent  nor  probable  cause
stand  as prerequisites  to a complete  search  of a traffic
offender's  person  or her car  under the reasoning  of the lower
court.  Contrary  to this Court's  holding  that a traffic  stop is
analogous  to  Terry stop, and therefore  limited  in its length
and scope,  Berkemer  v.  McCarty,  468  U.S.  420, 439 (1984),
the lower court's  decision  permits  every  degree of intrusion
associated  with  a full arrest to be a part of any traffic  stop.

The  lower court's  opinion does not make a custodial
arrest  for the offense  of failure  to wear  a seat belt mandatory,
but allows  an officer  to use  his discretion  in determining
whether  the offense  warrants  a custodial  arrest.  This
discretion  permits  an officer to search  the person  and vehicle
any traffic  offender  of his choosing.  The recently
documented  phenomenon  of racial  profiling  has
demonstrated  that drug interdiction  officers  select  persons  of
certain  races  in much  higher frequency  to search  for drugs.
See Tracey Maclin,  Race and the Fourth  Amendment,  51
Vand.  L. Rev.  333, 341-354  (1998).  Permitting  officer
absolute  discretion  to search  any traffic  offender  for drugs
will doubtless  lead to the same  sort  of racial  biases  that have
infected  drug interdiction  efforts  thus  far.  The lower court
places no  barrier  in the way of the discriminatory  searches,
but simply requires  the officer  to subject the motorist  he
desires  to search  to the indignity  of a full custodial  arrest.

The arrest  the lower court permitted  -- the arrest that

would  permit an officer  to search  any traffic  offender  of his
choosing  --  is simply  forbidden  by the Constitution.  The
common  law, the starting point of this Court's  Fourth
Amendment  analysis,  permitted  a misdemeanor  arrest  only

where  the offender  threatened  a continuing  affray if left at
large.  See Sir James  Stephen,  History of the Criminal  Law  of
England  193  (1883).

The  intrusion  is further  utterly  unreasonable  when  the

degree  of the intrusion  is weighed  against  the State's  interest
in the custodial  arrest.  See  Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1,
8 (1985)  (weighing  state's  interest  in intrusion  against  the
effect  of the manner  of seizure).  The  State  may  revoke  the
license  of any traffic  offender  who  fails  to appear  on  the
charge  or  fails  to pay  the fine.  See Barbara  C. Salken,  The
General  Warrant  of the  Twentieth  Century?  A Fourth
Amendment  Solution  to  Unchecked  Discretion  to Arrest for
Traffic  Offenses,  62 Temp.  L. Rev.  221, 267  (1989).  In the
instant  case,  the maximum  penalty  for the offense  was  a
$50.00  fine.

The  lower  court  decision  ignores  the common  law,  fails

to balance  the individual's  interest  in liberty and privacy
against the State's  meager  interest  in this custodial  arrest,  and
would  eviscerate  virtually  all of this Court's  doctrines  dealing
with  the interaction  of officers  and traffic  offenders.

ARGUMENT

I.  STANDARDLESS  DISCRETION  TO ARREST
OR  ANY  TRAFFIC  OFFENSE  PERMITS  AN
OFFICER  TO SEARCH  THE  PERSON  AND
VEHICLE  OF ANY  TRAFFIC  OFFENDER  OF
THE  OFFICER'S  CHOOSING.

The power  to make  a  custodial  arrest  for a misdemeanor

traffic  offense  permits  an  officer to select,  in his discretion,
any minor  offender,  search  her car and subject  her to the
indignity  of an arrest  and search  of her person.  This  power

removes  any constitutional  constraint  on  officers  performing
traffic  stops.  This power  permits personal  and institutional
prejudices  about  the sort  of people who are  transporting
contraband  to dictate  who  shall  suffer  the inconvenience  and
insult  of a custodial  arrest.  This power,  conveying  unbridled
discretion  and a license  to discriminate,  cannot  withstand
constitutional  scrutiny.

A.  Arresting  a Motorist  for a Traffic  Offense

Amounts  to an  End-Run  Around  this  Court's
Doctrines  Forbidding  a Search  Absent  Consent
or  Probable  Cause.

A traffic  stop is analogous  to a Terry stop and therefore
the "stop  and inquiry  must be reasonably  related  in scope to
their justification  for the  intrusion."  Berkemer  v.  McCarty,
468  U.S.  420, 439 (1984) (quoting  United States  v.  Brignoni-
Ponce,  422 U.S.  873, 881  (1975)  and  Terry v.  Ohio,  392  U.S.
1, 29 (1968)).  An arrest,  in lieu of a citation,  for a
misdemeanor  traffic  offense  greatly  expands  the scope of a
traffic  stop allowing  intrusions  otherwise justified  only on  a
showing  of probable  cause  or the motorist's  consent.

When  an  officer  issues  only a citation  for a traffic
offense,  this Court  permits  the officer  to search  the vehicle
only if the motorist  consents  or if the officer  develops
probable cause  to suspect  other wrongdoing.  See Knowles  v.
Towa,  525  U.S.  113 (1998) (forbidding  search  incident  to
arrest).  Consent  must  be voluntary.  Schneckloth  v.
Bustamonte,  412 U.S.  218, 223  (1973).  Undue  police
coercion  will not yield a valid consent.  Bumper v.  North
Carolina,  391  U.S.  543, 548-50  (1968).  Limited  intrusions
to protect  the officer  from an armed  motorist  are permitted,
but only if the officer  has reasonable  suspicion  that the
motorist  is armed  and the officer  may intrude  no more  than
in the reach  of
to discover  weapons  immediately
necessary
the motorist.  Michigan  v.  Long, 463  U.S.  1032,  1049  (1983);

————

oe

Terry v.  Ohio,  392  U.S.  1 (1968).  If an  officer  may  simply
arrest  any traffic  offender,  he may  circumvent  these  doctrines
merely by electing  to subject the misdemeanant  to a custodial
arrest.

A search  incident  to arrest  is permitted  whenever  a
motorist  is taken  into custody,  regardless  of the seriousness
of the offense.  United States  v.  Robinson,  414  U.S.  218
(1973).  The motorist's  person  may then be searched  incident
to arrest.  Chimel  v.  California,  395  U.S.  752,  763  (1969).
The  entire  cabin  of the automobile  may be searched  incident
to the arrest.  New  York  v.  Belton,  453  U.S.  454 (1981).  Any
part of the automobile  may  then  be searched  by officers  if
officers  are  instructed  by their  superiors,  as  a matter  of
routine,  to inventory  the items  in that part of the automobile.
See Florida  v.  Wells,  495  U.S.  1, 4-5  (1990);  United States  v.
Lumpkin,  159 F.3d  983, 987 (6th Cir.  1998) (permitting
inventory  search  of engine compartment  of vehicle);  United
States  v.  Kordosky,  921  F.2d  722,  723  (7th Cir.  1991)
(permitting  inventory  search  of locked  sidewall  compartment
of trunk).

This  Court  recently  and  unanimously  held  that an  officer
may not  perform a search  incident  to citation  for an  ordinary
traffic  offense  --  that  is, if an officer  does  not  actually  arrest  a
suspect,  a search  incident  to arrest  is unconstitutional.
Knowles  v.  Iowa,  525  U.S.  113  (1999).  This  decision
assumes  that a custodial  arrest  for  a misdemeanor  traffic
offense  alone  does  not  permit  a custodial  arrest.

This  Court  has held that where  police have  probable

cause  to arrest  a suspect,  it is constitutionally  irrelevant
whether  the search  incident  to arrest  occurs  before  or after
the suspect  is actually  placed under  arrest.  Rawlings  v.
Kentucky,  448  U.S.  98,  111  (1980).  As Justice  Harlan  wrote,

If the prosecution  shows  probable  cause
to arrest  prior to a search  of a man's  person,
it has met  its total  burden.  There  is no  case
in which  a defendant  may  validly  say,
"Although  the officer  had a right to arrest
me  at the moment  when  he seized  me  and
searched  my person,  the search  is invalid
because  he did not  in fact arrest  me  until
afterwards."

Peters  v.  New  York,  392  U.S.  40, 77 (1968)  (Harlan,  J.,
concurring).

If the lower  court's  decision  in the instant  case,

permitting  an  officer  to arrest  any traffic  offender,  accurately
states  the law then  the officer  may  search  any motorist,  so
long as he does  so before  issuing the citation.  Under
Rawlings  and Peters,  if an  officer  has the power  to perform  a
custodial  arrest,  he may perform a search  incident  to arrest
even before  he  announces  that the suspect  is in  a state of
custody.  If the lower court  opinion  is correct  then the officer
who  has seized  a motorist,  but has yet to issue  a citation  or
perform  a custodial  arrest,  has the power  to take the motorist
into custody  and thus may conduct  a search  incident  to arrest
under Rawlings  and Peters.

This Court's  unanimous  opinion  in Knowles  is

eviscerated  if a search  incident  to citation  is forbidden  after
the issuance  of a citation,  but allowed  moments  before  its
issuance.  The Court's  analysis of the doctrine  of search
incident  to arrest  in Knowles  further  demonstrates  it assumed
that a custodial  arrest  for an  ordinary  traffic  violation  is
constitutionally  unreasonable.  The State  of lowa  argued that
a search  incident  to a traffic  violation  was  permitted  because
the stopped  motorist  might try to destroy  evidence  of another
crime  or destroy evidence  relating to his identity.  This Court
found  the possibility  that an officer would  stumble  onto

evidence  of an unrelated  crime  to  be remote.  The Court
further  found  that "[a]s for the destruction  of evidence
relating to identity,  if a police officer  is not satisfied  with  the
identification  furnished  by the driver,  this may be a basis  for
arresting  him rather than merely  issuing a citation.”
Knowles,  525  U.S.  at 118.  The Court  thus signaled  that an
officer  must  have a basis  for a custodial  arrest  --  a principled
reason  for a significant  intrusion  on  the motorist's  liberty and
a search  otherwise  forbidden.

A debate  raging in the lower courts  illustrates  the nearly
universal  acceptance  of the idea that the Fourth  Amendment
limits  an  officer's  power  to intrude  on  the liberty of a mere
traffic  offender.  The lower courts  are  deeply divided  on
whether  an  officer  who  stops a traffic  offender  may make
inquiries  unrelated  to the traffic  offense,  specifically  whether
an  officer  may request  consent  to search  for drugs absent
suspicion  of wrongdoing  beyond  the traffic  infraction.  The
nature  of the debate  demonstrates  the near-universal
assumption  that an  arrest  for a mere  traffic  offense  is
forbidden  by the Fourth  Amendment.

A majority  of the courts  hold that questions  during a
traffic  stop must be limited  to the purpose  of the traffic  stop,
or to issues  related  to suspicion  reasonably  raised  during the
stop.’  These courts  rely upon this Court's  doctrine  that a

Mitchell  v.  United States,  746 A.2d 877, 887-90  (D.C. 2000); Almond  v.
State,  530 S.E.2d  750, 752 (Ga. Ct. App. 2000); State v.  Myers,  798 P.2d

(Ind. Ct. App.  1999);  State  v.  McNaughton,  924 S.W.2d  517, 523 (Mo.

traffic  stop, like a  Terry  stop, is bounded  in its scope  and
inquiry  to the basis for the seizure.  Berkemer
v. McCarty,
468 U.S.  420,  439  (1984).  The remainder
of the courts  to
consider  this issue hold that an officer  is free to ask whatever
questions  he pleases  so long as the length  of the traffic  stop is
not prolonged  by the officer's questions.’  These courts draw
from  this Court's  teachings  that questioning  of a citizen  at
v. Royer,
liberty  is neither  a search  nor seizure.  See Florida
460 U.S.  491, 497 (1983)  (plurality  opinion).  If police
questioning  is not a  Fourth  Amendment  event,  then it  may
not expand  the scope  of a seizure.

Either  position  assumes  that there are limits on traffic
stops,  that an officer  may not place  a  motorist  in handcuffs
and transport  her to the police  station  for booking  merely
because  the motorist  committed  a traffic  offense.  The
majority  view considers  questioning  unrelated  to the stop that
leads to a search  to exceed  the bounds  of a legitimate  traffic
stop.  The lower court's  opinion  in the instant  case  would  not
even  require  an officer  to engage  in questioning  to obtain
consent  or  probable  cause  to search  the car -- a  search  could

instead  of a citation.

The minority  view  recognizes  that police  questioning
may not prolong  the length of a traffic  stop, unless  the officer
has suspicion  of wrongdoing
beyond  the traffic offense.  The
lower court's  opinion  would  permit  officers  to extend  the
length
beyond  the traffic  offease,  not merely  for the length  of
question  unrelated  to the offense,  but for the length  of time
required  for the motorist  to be booked,  fingerprinted,  and
on bond.
released

of a traffic  stp,  absent  suspicion  of  any wrongdoing

The debate  in the lower courts  concerns  the sort of
questions  an officer  may ask in order  to obtain  probable
cause or consent  to  search  a traffic  offender's  car.  The debate
would  be utterly  preempted  by the doctrine  accepted  by the
lower  court -- a passkey  to search  the person  and vehicle  of
any traffic  offender.

If an officer  may conduct  a  custodial  arrest  for any

traffic  violation,  then he need not obtain  valid  consent  to
search  a car, he need  only decide  to arrest  the minor traffic
offender.  His search  then is not bounded  by a search  to
discover  weapons  and is not predicated  on any suspicion.
And,  according  to the lower court's  holding,  the decision  to
arrest is entrusted  to the unguided  discretion  of the officer
who is aware that issuing  a citation  forbids  a search  of the car
while an arrest,  for the same  offense,  entitles a full search.

B.  The Recently  Documented  Phenomenon  of

Profiling  would  be Permitted  and Perpetuated
by the Doctrine  the  Lower  Court Announced.

The lower court's  decision  in the instant  case  gives
officers  the ability  to select  any minor traffic  offender  from
the universe  of traffic  offenders,  arrest  the motorist  and

her automobile.  This vast power will lead to even

search
more  egregious  examples  of the over-reaching  by drug
interdiction  officers  thoroughly  documented  in the lower
courts  and in the academic  literature.

"Very  few drivers  can traverse  any appreciable  distance
without  violating  some traffic regulation.”  B.  James  George,
Constitutional  Limitations  on Evidence  in  Criminal  Cases  65
(1973  ed.)  Whren v. United  States,  517  U.S.  806 (1996),
permits  an officer  to stop any motorist  who has committed
the extensive
however  minor. Given
any traffic  infraction,
regulation  of motor vehicles  and their operation,  and the
human  imperfections  of all drivers,  the lower court's  opinion
permits  an officer  to select  any motorist  he desires,  arrest  her
and thereby  acquire  the right to search the entire car.

Beyond  an officer's  interest  in aggressively  engaging  in

"the often competitive  enterprise  of ferreting  out crime,"
Johnson  v.  United States,  333 U.S.  10, 13 (1948),  forfeiture
laws  have created  a  powerful  incentive  for officers  to inspect
as many automobiles  as possible  for the presence  of drugs.
Even a relatively  small  amount  of  unlawful  contraband
permits  the forfeiture  of an automobile  carrying  the
contraband.  The  effect of this bounty  to the state  on an
individual  officer  cannot  be overstated.  The financial
existence  of many  of the drug  interdiction  units  depends  on
the bounty  from  forfeiture,  creating  a  powerful  economic
incentive  for officers  to search  a  many  cars as possible  for
drugs.  See Eric  Blumenson  and  Eva Nilsen,  Policing  for
Profit:  The Drug War's  Hidden  Agenda,  65 U. Chi.  L. Rev.
35,  76-84  (1998)  (noting  impact  of forfeiture  laws  on patterns
of prosecution).

In  United  States v.  Freeman,  209 F.3d 464, 467 (6th Cir.

2000),  Judge  Clay observed  "a troubling  pattern  or practice
by some  members  of the Shelby  County  Sheriff's  Department
drug interdiction  squad  of stopping  ‘target’  vehicles  on

11

questionable  probable  cause  grounds  . .  .  in order to search
for contraband.”  In United States  v.  Mesa,  62 F.3d  159 (6th
Cir.  1995),  the Sixth  Circuit  offered  a caution  to officers:

we  [have given] the green  light to police officers
to stop vehicles  for any infraction,  no matter  how
slight,  even  if the officer's  real purpose  was to hope
that narcotics  or other  contraband  would  be found
as a result  of the stop[,  and because]  .
.  we  have
extended  this authority  to the broadest  extent
possible,  .
abused.

.  we  have a duty to see that  it is not

.

.

Freeman,  209 F.3d  at 471  (citing Mesa,  62 F.3d  at  162)
(emphasis  added).

Freeman  noted  a systemic  pattern  of police  perjury  and

questionable  traffic  stops  by a drug interdiction  team in its
efforts  to search  automobiles  it had no basis to suspect
contained  contraband.  209 F.3d at 467-70  (Clay,  J.,
concurring).  Systemic  abuses  of the power  to enforce  traffic
laws have  been  well documented.  The incentive
to search
cars for drugs has further  led officers  to disproportionately
stop and search  minority  motorists  with no suspicion  of
wrongdoing  beyond  an ordinary  traffic  violation.  See Tracey
Maclin,  Race and the Fourth Amendment, 51 Vand. L. Rev.
333, 341-354  (1998);  David  A. Harris,  "Driving  While
Black"  and All Other  Traffic  Offenses:  The Supreme  Court
and Pretextual  Traffic Stops, 87 J. Crim.  L. and Criminology
544,  560-69  (1997).  In the search  for drugs,  officers  have
substituted

race for suspicion.  See Maclin,  supra at 344-46.

pattern  of racial profiling
To  curb  one well-documented

by police  officers  on the New Jersey  Turnpike,  the United
States  Department  of Justice  entered  into a  consent  decree
the New
with  the New  Jersey  State  Police.  In that decree,
Jersey  State Police  were prohibited  from allowing  a  drug dog

to sniff the exterior  of an automobile  for drugs unless  there
was  a basis for using the dog and prohibited  from requesting
consent  to search  for drugs without  a reasonable  suspicion
that drugs  would  be discovered.  Consent  Decree, United
States  v.  New Jersey,  Civ.  No 99-5970,  F¥ 28, 32-33  (MLC)
(D.N.J.)  (visited  Sept.  10, 2000)<http://www.doj.gov/
crt/split/documents/jerseycomp.htm>.

By limiting  the ability  of officers  to search  for drugs
when  they lack suspicion,  the incentive  of officers  to stop
motorists  because  of inarticable  hunches  about the sort of
people  who transport  drugs is diminished.  Substantial
hurdles  stand  between  the officer  and the search  he desires
but  may not  legally  justify. To  even request consent, under
the New  Jersey  Consent  Decree,  he must  develop  reasonable
suspicion  and even then the will of the motorist  can prevent
his search unless  the officer  develops  probable  cause  during
the seizure.

Permitting  the mere observation  of a traffic  offense  to

allow an arrest  of the motorist,  thus a  complete  search  of her
person  and car, has the opposite  effect.  Officers,  under  the
lower court's  holding,  are permitted  in their  unguided
discretion,  to search the car of any person  committing  any
traffic  offense.  Driven  by a powerful  economic  incentive  to
discover  contraband-carrying  forfeitable  cars,  officers  given
the power to arrest  and search  any traffic  offender  could  be
expected  to do no better than their predecessors.  Arrests  for
traffic  offenses,  carrying  with them the right to examine  the
contents  of the motorists’  automobiles,  will turn on popular
myths about  the sort of people  who deal drugs.

Responding  to the Petitioner's  argument  in Whren  v.
United  States  that officers  may use illegitimate  factors  to
determine  which  infractions  to prosecute,  this Court held that
practical  realities  prevented  limiting  officer  discretion  to
prosecute

any traffic  offender.  "[W]e think there is no

12

13

realistic  alternative  to the traditional  common-law  rule that
probable cause  justifies a search  and seizure."  Whren,  517
U.S.  at 819.  The scope  of the seizure  justified by the
observation  of an ordinary traffic  infraction,  however,  may
be readily  limited  by a  common  law rule --  an arrest  for a
misdemeanor
is forbidden  absent  a threat  that the
misdemeanant  will engage  in an act of violence  if left at
large.  Sir James  Stephen,  History of the Criminal Law of
England  193 (1883)  By limiting the officer's  power  to arrest
traffic  offenders,  he is denied  the ability to search  traffic
offenders  absent  suspicion  or consent.

The common

law rule  therefore  promotes  enforcement

of traffic  laws based on an officer's  assessment  of which
traffic  offense  warrants  prosecution,  rather than the selection
of offenders  based on hunches  or profiles  of the sort of
people  who might  be committing  more serious  crimes.
While  not limiting  the type of offender  the officer  may
initially  seize,  the common  law rule limits  the scope  of the
officer's  intrusion  and investigation  of an ordinary
misdemeanant.  And by removing  the officer's  power to
automatically  investigate  crimes  he has no reason  to suspect,
his initial  seizure,  limited  to issuing  a citation  for the traffic
offense,  is far less likely to turn on his conceptions  about the
drugs.
type of motorist  transporting

Il.  THE  COMMON  LAW  AT  THE  TIME  OF  THE

FOURTH  AMENDMENT'S  ADOPTION  AND  THE
MORES  OF MODERN  SOCIETY  FORBID  THE
ARREST  OF AN ORDINARY  TRAFFIC
OFFENDER.

This Court looks first to the common  law to determine

whether  a governmental  intrusion  satisfies  the Fourth
Amendment.  Wyoming  v.  Houghton,  526 U.S.  295, 299
(1999).  The common  law did not permit  a constable  to make
an arrest  for a  misdemeanor  unless  it  was committed  in his

presence  and involved  an  affray that was  likely to continue.
Sir  James  Stephen,  History of the Criminal Law of England
193  (1883).  Changes  in societal  mores  or technology  permit
deviations  from  the common  law's starting  point, Tennessee
v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1, 11 (1985),  but contemporary  society
finds an arrest  for a  misdemeanor  repugnant  and has used  its
computer  technology  to assure  that traffic  offenders  may be
prosecuted  without  resort  to arrest.  Contemporary  legislation
in many states  has adopted  the common  law rule.  Modern
technology  provides  means  of immediately  determining  a
misdemeanant's  identity and if his license  is suspended  in any
jurisdiction  for failure  to appear,  this information  is
immediately  available  to any officer  in the country.
Technological  advancements  and legislative  indications  of
contemporary  mores  therefore  demonstrate  the modern
efficacy  of the common  law limitation  on  an officer's  power
to arrest  for a misdemeanor.

A.  The Common  Law  Permitted  a Misdemeanor
Arrest  Only  Where  an Affray  was  Likely  to
Continue.

The decisions  of English courts  prior to, and roughly

contemporaneous  with, the adoption  of the Fourth
Amendment  illustrate  that an officer  was  permitted  to arrest
only in the event  of an affray that would  continue  unless  the
parties were  arrested.  Regina  v.  Tooley,  92 Eng. Rep. 349,
352 (K.B.  1710);  Cook v.  Nethercote,  172 Eng. Rep.  1443,
1445  (1835).

The  common  law did not authorise  the arrest
of persons  guilty  or suspected  of misdemeanors,  .
except  in cases of an actual  breach  of the peace
either  by an affray  or by violence  to  an
individual.  In such cases the arrest  had to be
made not so much  for  the purpose  of bringing

14

15

the offender  to justice  as in order to preserve
the peace, and the right to arrest was
accordingly
limited  to cases in which  the
person  to be arrested  was taken in the fact or
:

after its

—

fiately

Sir James  Stephen, History of the Criminal Law of England,
193 (1883) (emphasis added).

Lord Holt observed in 1710  that "a constable
cannot
arrest,  but when he sees an actual  breach  of the peace; and  if
the affray be over, he cannot arrest."  Regina  v.  Tooley, 92
Eng. Rep. 349, 352 (K.B.  1710).  In 1835,
Baron  Alderson
described  an officer's  power to arrest for a  misdemeanor  as
limited  to suppressing  breaches  of the peace  that would  recur
unless  the parties  were arrested.

.  there had been an affray,  and that affray

If.  .
were  over,  then the constable  had not  and ought
the persons
not have  the power  of apprehending
engaged in it; for the power  is given him by law
to prevent  a breach  of the peace;  and where  a
breach  of the peace  has been  committed,  and was
over,  the constable  must  proceed  in the same  way
as any other  person, namely,  by obtaining a
from  the magistrate.
warrant

Cook v.  Nethercote,  172  Eng. Rep.  1443,  1445  (1835).  Not
surprisingly,  Baron  Alderson  used the example  of an  affray
as a  breach  of the peace that could continue  absent  an  arrest.
As one  modern  commentator  has noted,  "[t}his is to say that
the arrest  was  not  strictly part  of the punishment  process.  It
was  mostly  for the purpose  of separating  the combatants  and
detaining  them  to prevent  further  violence."  Edward  C.
Fisher,  Laws  of Arrest  189 (1967).

Indeed  this  Court  has recognized  that  the common  law

.

imposed  limitations  on an officer's  power to arrest  for a
misdemeanor.  "A  peace officer  .
power  of arresting  without  a warrant  except  when  a breach  of
the peace has been  committed  in his presence  or there  is
reasonable  ground  for supposing  that a breach  of the peace  is
about to be committed or renewed  in his presence."  Carroll
v.  United States,  267  U.S.  133,  157  (1925).*

.  has at common law no

the public  tranquility

The  offense  of driving  without  a seat belt is not one that
-- no violence  is intended  or
threatens
occasioned  by one who elects  to travel  the highway  less than
secure  in the event of an accident.  Certainly  nothing  beyond
the fact the motorist  was once  caught  without  a seat belt
suggests  that even this minor  crime will continue  lest she is
immediately  removed  from  the roadway.  The common  law,
the starting point in this Court's  analysis  of the reasonable-
ness  of an  intrusion,  simply did not contemplate  an  arrest  for
such  a minor,  non-violent  misdemeanor.

*  Sir James Stephen,  Lord Holt and Baron Alderman  use the terms breach
of the peace  and affray  interchangeably.  Stephen,  supra,  at  193;  Tooley,
92 Eng. Rep. at 352; Nethercote,  172  Eng. Rep. at 1445.  Sir William
Blackstone  in his Commentaries  on  the Laws  of England  labeled  an
affray  as one subset  of the category  of crimes  under the umbrella  "breach
of the peace",  a  category  also including  libel,  false prophesies  and
spreading  false news about  "any  great man of the realm."  4 Blackstone,
Commentaries,  ch.  11,  142-53  (Garland  1978).  The common law
authorities  on misdemeanor  arrests, Holt, Alderman,  and Stephen,  often
interchangeably
authorities  nevertheless  make  clear  only those misdemeanors  that pose  a
continuing  threat  of public  violence  justify  an arrest  as opposed  to

use the terms  affray  and breach  of the peace. These

16

17

officer/citizen  interactions.  Wyoming v. Houghton,  526 U.S.
299 (1999).  Changes  in society  may warrant
295,
reconsideration
of the common  law's baseline,  changes  in
either the technology  affecting police/citizen  encounters  or
changes  in societal  views  regarding  police/citizen  encounters.
Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1, 11 (1985).  In the instant
case,  modern  technology  and contemporary  mores reinforce
the common  law's limitations  on an officer’s  power to arrest
for a misdemeanor.  Modern  technology
has provided  new
means  of assuring  a  misdemeanant's  presence  at trial without
arrest  and contemporary  legislation  demonstrates  society's
support  for limits  on an officer's  power to arrest  for  a  mere
traffic  infraction.

.

that advances  in

In Garner,  this Court  departed  from  the common  law
rule permitting  deadly  force  to seize  a fleeing  felon.  471  .
U.S. at 11. The Court recognized
technology  and changes  in public opinion  compelled  a
departure.  The effectiveness  of modern  weapons  made  use  >
of a deadly weapon  to apprehend a fleeing  felon  far more
likely to kill than an attempt  to  seize a suspect  with the
weapons  contemplated  at the time of the common  law rule.
Id.  at  11.  The  individual  interest  implicated  by permitting  the
use  of deadly  force  in the modern  world  was  therefore  more
compelling  than  the interest  implicated  in a world  where
weapons  were  more  likely to merely wound,  or miss  entirely.
Id.  Modern  statutory  limitations  on  the use  of official  force
further  illustrated  that all reasonable  means  of seizing a non-
violent  fleeing  felon  at common  law were  not reasonable  in
modern  times.  /d. at 16-19.

Similarly,  modern  legislation  and technology  has made

traffic  charge.  See Barbara C. Salken,
A Fourth
of the Twentieth  Century?

the use of a custodial  arrest for a  misdemeanor  even less
compelling  than it was at the time it was forbidden  by the
common  law.  The Non-Resident  Violator  Compact has
given  a state the power to suspend  the driver's license  of a
motorist  of another  state  who fails to appear in court to
answer  a misdemeanor
The General  Warrant
Amendment  Solution  to  Unchecked  Discretion  to Arrest for
Traffic Offenses,  62 Temp.  L. Rev.  221, 267 (1989).
Computer  technology  permits  an officer  stopping  such a
motorist
See Darlene  Cedrés,  Note Mobile  Data  Terminals  and
Random  License  Plate  Checks:  The Need for Uniform
Guidelines  and a Reasonable  Suspicion  Requirement,  23
Rutgers  Computer  & Tech.  L. J. 391, 396-97  (1997) (noting
that officers  in patrol cars can access the National  Crime
Information  Center  and determine  whether  a driver's  license
has been  suspended  or revoked).  Technology  and legislation
have  given states  a way of compelling  appearance  in
misdemeanor  traffic offenses  unknown  at common  law --  at a
time when  the common  law nonetheless  prohibited  a
custodial  arrest  for misdemeanors.

to rapidly  learn the status of such motorist's  license.

«  Garner  further  looked  to contemporary  limitations  pn
police use of force  to determine  whether  deadly  force ought
to be permitted.  Finding  the modern  trend  to prohibit  deadly
force  for non-violent  fleeing  felons,  the Court  found
reasonableness  should  be assessed  differently  than  it was
under  the common  law.  Garner,  471  U.S.  at  16-19.

A growing  number  of states  prohibit  an officer  from
taking a misdemeanant  into castody  where  the officer  has no
basis  for believing  the offender  will fail to appear  on  the

"ee

19

charge and no basis  for believing the offense  will continue.°
Limitations  on  the power  to arrest  for misdemeanors,
common  in many states,  reveal  that modern  society  finds  it
unreasonable  to vest  law enforcement  officers  with  such
unlimited  discretion.  Interestingly,  Texas  law, by statute,
prohibits  a custodial  arrest  for speeding.  Tex.  Trans.  Code
Ann.  § 543.003  (West  1999).  Speeding  carries  a higher fine
under Texas  law than the crime  for which  the petitioner was
arrested,  failure  to wear  a seat  belt.  And while  speeding
potentially  endangers  others,  failure  to wear  a seat  belt
imposes a risk only to those  who  elect  to violate  the law.

itl.  THE  MANNER  OF A SEIZURE  MUST  BE

REASONABLE  IN LIGHT  OF THE  STATE'S
INTERESTS

Probable  cause  to believe  a motorist  has committed  a

crime,  however  minor,  does not  end the inquiry  into the
reasonableness  of the seizure.  Probable  cause  permits  a
seizure,  Whren  v.  United States,  517  U.S.  806, 813  (1996),
but the manner  in which  the seizure  is carried  out  must  be
reasonable  in light of the government's  interest  in the seizure.
Garner,  471  U.S.  at 8.  The  lower  court  decision  would
permit  an  officer  to seize a citizen  in any manner  he saw  fit

° See,  e.g.  Ala.  Code  § 32-1-4  (1989);  Colo.  Rev.  Stat.  § 42-4-1707
(1997);  Haw.  Rev. Stat. Ann.  § 286-10  (Michie  1998);  Ind.  Code  Ann.
§ 9-30-2-5  (Michie  1997);  La.  Rev.  Stat.  Ann.  § 32:391(A)  (West  1989);
Md.  Code  Ann.,  Trans.  § 26-202(a)(2)  (1999);  Mass.  Gen.  Laws  Ann.
ch. 90 § 21  (Law.  Co-op.  1994);  Minn.  Stat.  Ann.  §.169.91  (West Supp.
2000);  Neb.  Rev.  Stat.  § 29-435  (1995);  N.M.  Stat.  Ann.  § 66-8-123
(Supp.  1998); N.D.  Cent.  Code  § 39-07-07  (1987);  Ohio  Rev.  Code  Ann.
§ 2935.26  (West  1997);  Okla.  Stat.  Ann.  tit. 22 § 1115.1(A)  (West Supp.
2000);  Or.  Rev.  Stat.  § 133.310(1)  (1997);  S.C.  Code  Ann.  § 56-25-30
(Law.  Co-op.  Supp.  1991);  S.D.  Codified  Laws  § 32-33-2  (1998);  Tenn.
Code  Ann.  § 40-7-118(b){1)  (1997);  Vt. R. Crim.  P. 3(a), (c) (Supp.
1999);  Va. Code  Ann. § 46.2-936  (Michie  Supp. 2000);  Wes. Stat. Ann.
§§ 345.22 to .23  (West Supp.  1999).

so  long as the officer  had probable  cause to believe  that some
offense  -- however  petty -- had been  committed  and the
arresting  officer  did not actually  kill or maim the suspect.
Atwater  v.  City of Lago  Vista,  195  F.3d 242, 245  (Sth Cir.
1999) (en banc).

A.  The State's  Interest  in Ensuring  the Defendant's
Presence  at Trial  and  Protecting  Society  from  a
Criminal  Suspect  Does  Not Justify  a Custodial
Arrest  for a Fine-Only  Misdemeanor.

that the citizenry  is safe  from her pending trial. See

Once a state has probable  cause to believe  a  citizen
guilty  of a criminal  infraction,  the state's interests  in taking
custody of her consist  of ensuring  her presence  at trial and
ensuring
United States  v.  Salerno,  481  U.S.  739, 748  (1987)
(recognizing  these  interests  to justify pretria!  detention).  It
has been  recognized  that the seriousness of the offense  is a
key indicator  of the likelihood  of the defendant  appearing  to
answer  the charges  against her.  Edward  C. Fisher,  Laws  of
Arrest  189 (1967).  Further,  this Court  in Salerno  recognized
that the seriousness  of the offense  was a valid  indicator  of
whether  the defendant  posed a threat  to society  if released
pending  trial.  Salerno,  481  U.S.  at 749.  If a goal of
punishment  is incapacitation,  society needs protection  from
the violent  once  their dangerous  tendencies  become  evident.

For most  modern  misdemeanors,  pretrial  release  is all

but certain,  making the relevant  inquiry whether  the
misdemeanor  suggests  the defendant  presents  a danger she
will  not  later present  once  booked  and bonded  out.  The
common  law rule prohibiting  misdemeanor  arrest  recognizes
the legitimate  interests  of ensuring  the offender's  presence  at
trial  and protecting  society  from  the suspect.  For ordinary
misdemeanors,  where  the penalty  is less than  severe,  the
suspect  is not  likely to fail to appear  as often  the sanction  for
failure  to appear  exceeds  the sanction  for the underlying

20

21

misdemeanor.  And, unlike the crimes  of extortion  and
conspiracy to commit  murder contemplated  in Salerno,° the
commission  of a non-violent  misdemeanor  does not suggest
that society needs  immediate  protection  from the suspect.

Where,  however,  the state  illustrates  that the

does pose an immediate

pene  annie despite the relative  minor classification  of his
crime,
threat to public safety, the
common  law rule provides  for his immediate  incarceration.
If the constable  reasonably  believed  that the misdemeanant
would  continue  to threaten  harm to society  if not arrested,
then  an  arrest  was  permitted.

A modern  example  of a misdemeanor  that itself suggests

of incarceration  is driving  under the influence

the necessity
where  the driver poses  a risk to society  because  of his
intoxication,  a condition  unchanged by his initial  seizure.
Modern  statutes  universally  recognize  an officer's  right to
arrest a motorist suspected

of being  intoxicated.’

Whren  v.  United States,  517 U.S.  806 (1996)  stands  for

the rather unremarkable  principle that any person  who
violates  the traffic  code may be forced to suffer  the
consequences  established  by the legislature,  a very modest
fine.  Whren  did not overrule  this Court's  holding that the
degree of the intrusion  occasioned  to prosecute  the offense
must be balanced  against  the state's interest  in that degree of
intrusion.

* In Salerno, the defendants were charged with 35 acts of racketeering
activity  including  fraud, extortion,  gambling,  and conspiracy  to commit
murder.  Salerno,  481 U.S. at 743.
” See, e.g.,  Tenn. Code Ann. § 40-7-118(c)(7)  (requiring misdemeanor
arrest "the person  is so intoxicated  that such person could be a  danger to
such person or to others.)

B.  Absent  a Fear  for Public  Safety  or Inability  to

Otherwise  Bring  Offender  to Justice,  the Means
of Seizure  May Not Exceed  the Punishment
for the Offense.
Authorized

As this Court  recognized  in Garner,  at common  law,
was a capital crime.  Garner,  471  U.S. at 13.

burglary
Imploring  deadly  force to stop an unarmed  burglar  therefore
involved  no greater  consequence  than the burglar  would face
if convicted.  Modern  laws provide  much more lenient
penalties  for burglary  and this Court accordingly  held in
Garner  that the consequence  of death  was disproportionate to
the state’s  interest  in ensuring  prosecuting  of a  non-capital
felon  who posed no risk to the community.  /d.

Judge Easterbrook  has recognized  that an arrest for a
misdemeanor  offense  amounts  to more  punishment  than  will
typically  be assigned  by a neutral judicial  officer.  Gramenos
v.  Jewel  Companies,  Inc.,  797  F.2d  432, 441  (7th Cir.  1986).
A warrantless  arrest  for such  an  offense  is a decision  by a
member  of the law enforcement  community  to bring about  a
sanction  exceeding  that  likely imposed  by a neutral  and
detached judicial  officer.  When  the offense  is a fine-only
misdemeanor,  the law enforcement  officer  has visited  a
sanction  upon a citizen  exceeding  even  a judicial  officer's
authority  to punish.

23

CONCLUSION

The decision  of United  States  Court of  Appeals  for

the Fifth Circuit  misconstrued  this Court's  precedent
regarding  limitations  on the manner  of seizure  an officer  may
perform  once  he has probable  cause.  A custodial  arrest  for
the offense  of failure  to wear a seat belt is no more
reasonable  than the slaying  of non-violent  fleeing  felon. The
judgment  of the Court  of Appeals  should  be reversed.

Respectfully  submitted,

XIV

INTEREST  OF  AMICI  '

The  American  Civil  Liberties  Union  (ACLU)  is  a
nationwide,  nonprofit,  nonpartisan  organization  with  nearly
300,000  members  dedicated  to  the  principles  of  liberty  and
equality  embodied  in  the  Constitution.  In  support  of  these
principles,  the  ACLU  has  appeared  before  this  Court  in
numerous  Fourth  Amendment  cases,  both  as  direct  counsel
The  ACLU  of  Texas  is  one  of  its
and  as  amicus  curiae.
statewide  affiliates.
Because  this  case  addresses  an  impor-
tant  Fourth  Amendment  question,  its  proper  resolution  is  a
matter  of substantial  concern  to  the  ACLU  and  its  members.

For  over  35  years  the  Mexican  American  Legal  Defense
and  Educational  Fund  (MALDEF)  has  fought  to  promote
and  protect  the  rights  of  Latinos  in  the  United  States.
MALDEF’s  interest
in  Fourth  Amendment  issues  stems
from  our  Immigrant  Rights  and  Access  to  Justice  program
areas,  which  currently  monitor  police  conduct  for  compli-
ance  with  the  Fourth  Amendment,  and  seek  to  address  issues
profiling  by  law  enforcement  agencies.
of  racial/ethnic
MALDEF  has a substantial  interest  in  the  proper  resolution
of  this  case,
to  ensure  full  protection  of  the  rights  of
members  of the  Latino  community.

The  National  Police  Accountability  Project  of  the  Na-
tional  Lawyers  Guild  is  dedicated  to  protecting  all  persons
from  the  unlawful  or  unconstitutional  use  of  police  power.
The  Project  provides  information  and  resources  necessary
for  police  misconduct  litigation  and  support  for  legislative
efforts  to  strengthen  remedies  for  victims  of police  abuse.

'  Letters  of consent  to  the  filing  of this  brief  have  been  lodged  with  the
Clerk  of  the  Court  pursuant  to  Rule  37.3.
Pursuant  to  Rule  37.6,
counsel  for  amici  states  that  no  counsel  for  a  party  authored  this  brief  in
whole  or  in  part  and  no  person,  other  than  amici,  its  members,  or  its
counsel,  made  a  monetary  contribution  to  the  preparation  or  submission
of this  brief.

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE

Gail  Atwater  was  driving  her  two  children  home  from
soccer  practice  one  afternoon,  at  a  speed  of fifteen  miles  per
hour  through  a  residential  neighborhood  in  Lago  Vista,
Texas.  None  of the  three  was  wearing  a  seat  belt.  Atwater
was  stopped  by  City  of  Lago  Vista  Police  Officer  Bart
Turek,  who  screamed  at  her  that  they  had  met  before  (sever-
al  months  previously  Turek  had  stopped  Atwater,  apparently
suspecting  that  her  son  was  riding  in  the  car  without  a  seat
belt,  which  turned  out  not  to  be  the  case)  and  that  this  time
she  was  going  to  jail.  Atwater  v.  City  of Lago  Vista,  165
F.3d  380,  382  (Sth  Cir.  1999).

Texas  law  provides  that  failure  to  wear  a  seat  belt  is  a
misdemeanor,  subject  to  a  maximum  punishment  of  $50.
Texas  Transp.  Code  Ann.  §545.413(d)  (West  1999).  Texas
law  allowed  Officer  Turek  the  discretion  to  issue  a  citation
for  this  offense,  id.  §543.005,  or  to  make  a  custodial  arrest,
id.  §543.001.  Neither  the  statute  nor  Lago  Vista  police
department  policy  provides  any  guidelines  or  criteria  for  of-
ficers  on  how  to  decide  between  a  citation  and  an  arrest  in
165  F.3d  at  383.
such  circumstances.

Officer  Turek  chose  to  arrest  Atwater  for  the  seat  belt
offense.
After  what  was  described  by  witnesses  as  a  "ti-
rade"  on  his  part,  Turek  took  Atwater  into  custody,  hand-
cuffing  her  behind  her  back,  and  refusing  her  request  that
she  be  allowed  to  take  her  six-year-old  daughter  and  four-
year-old  son  to  a  neighbor's  house  two  houses  away  from
where  they  were  standing,  rather  than  have  them  accompany
Atwater  was  taken  to  the  police
her  to  jail.

/d.  at  382.°

*  When  Turek  demanded  Atwater’s  driver's  license  and  proof  of  in-
surance  card,  Atwater  replied  that  those  documents  were  in  a  purse
Turek  subsequently  added  charges  of
stolen  several  days  previously.
(continued...)

to

station  in  Turek’s  squad  car,  where  she  was  required  to
empty  her  pockets,  remove  her  shoes  and  glasses,  and  have
her  picture  taken.
She  then  spent  about  an  hour  in a jail
cell  before  being  taken  to  a  magistrate  who  released  her
when  she  posted  bond.  Her  children  were  spared  the  trip to
the  station  house  when  a  neighbor  happened  on  the  scene.

Atwater  pleaded  no  contest  to  the  seat  belt  charge.
Because  this  incident  caused  Atwater  and  her  children  ex-
treme  emotional  distress,  requiring  medication  and  coun-
seling,  they  brought  an  action  pursuant  to  42  U.S.C.  §1983,
alleging,  inter  alia,  that  Turek’s  arrest  was  an  unreasonable
seizure  under  the  Fourth  Amendment.
The  VU.  S.  District
Court  for  the  Western  District  of  Texas  granted  the  defen-
dants  summary  judgment,  a  panel  of  the  Fifth  Circuit  re-
versed,  165  F.3d  380,  and  the  Fifth  Circuit  en  banc  reversed
the  panel,  holding  that  the  arrest  was  not  unconstitutional
under  current  law.  195  F.3d  242  (1999).

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

The  Fourth  Amendment  prohibits  custodial  arrest  for
fine-only  misdemeanors,  at  least  in  the  absence  of  exigent
The  Texas  statute  authorizing  custodial  ar-
circumstances.
rest  for  any  violation  of its traffic  code  resembles  the  gener-
al  warrants  that  were  one  of the  principal  motivating  factors

*  (...continued)
driving  without  a  license  and  insurance  card  to  the  original  seat  belt
charge  against  Atwater,  but  these  charges  were  dismissed  on  the  same
195  F.3d  242,  252  (Sth  Cir.  1999)(en  banc)
day  they  were  filed.
Under  Texas  law,  driving  without  proof  of
(Dennis,  J.,  dissenting).
insurance  is  neither  a  crime  nor  an  offense,  see  id.,  and  failure  to  carry
or  exhibit  a  driver's  license  as  a  first  offense  is a  fine-only  misdemean-
or,  see  id.,  so  even  if the  after-the-fact  additional  charges  were  deemed
to  have  been  legitimate,  Ms.  Atwater’s  arrest  was  still,  at  most,  for  a
fine-only  misdemeanor.

behind  the  American  Revolution  and  the  drafting  of  the
Fourth  Amendment.  See  Nelson  B.  Lasson,  THE  HISTORY
AND  DEVELOPMENT  OF  THE  FOURTH  AMENDMENT  TO  THE
UNITED  STATES  CONSTITUTION  at  13-78  (1937).

The  Fourth  Amendment  axiom  that  excessive  police  dis-
cretion  can  lead  to  arbitrary  enforcement  is demonstrated  by
Atwater’s  case;  the  potential  for  racially  discriminatory  en-
forcement,  see  Illinois  v.  Wardlow,  528  U.S.  __,  120  S.Ct.
673,  677,  682  nn.9-10  (2000)(Stevens,  J.,  dissenting),  is
even  more  alarming.

In  Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.  806  (1996),  the
Court  declined  to  question  whether  stops  for  traffic  offenses
are  in  fact  pretexts  to  investigate  suspected  drug  offenders.
If  there  are  no  objective  limitations  on  the  more  intrusive
power  to  arrest,  other  than  probable  cause,  states  may,  as
some  already  have,  authorize  arrest  for  a  vast  array  of  of-
fenses  --  not  only  the  entire  traffic  code,  as  in  Texas,  but
littering,  jaywalking  and  perhaps  even  parking  violations.
Law  enforcement  officers  then  have  license  to  select  anyone
at  all,  wait  for  them  to  commit  any  one  of thousands  of pos-
sible  infractions,  and  then  place  their  target  under  arrest  in
order  to  conduct  a  search  incident  to  arrest  of the  designated
person  and  vehicle.
Arrests  would,  in  truth,  become  inci-
dent  to  the  desired  searches.

What  is a  "reasonable"  seizure  under  the  Fourth  Amend-
ment  must  be  determined  under  the  Court’s  usual  balancing
test,  weighing  the  relative  intrusion  on  individual  interests
against  the  state’s  legitimate  needs.
Tennessee  v.  Garner,
471  U.S.  1  (1985).
The  Fifth  Circuit  is  incorrect  in  as-
serting  that  there  is  no  need  to  apply  the  balancing  test  in
this  case,  mischaracterizing  dicta  in  Whren,  517  U.S.  at  817,
as  assuming  the  result  of a  balance  of interests  not  yet  con-
ducted.
Presuming  that  the  legislature  may  authorize  offi-
cers  to  arrest  for  any  trivial  infraction,  in  addition  to  beg-
ging  the  question  posed  in  this  case,  flies  in  the  face  of the

balance  struck  at  common  law,  which  authorized  arrest  only
for  fairly  serious  "misdemeanors"  involving  a  breach  of the
peace,  see  1  James  F.  Stephen,  A  HISTORY  OF  THE  CRIM-
INAL  LAW  OF  ENGLAND  193  (London,  MacMillian  1883);
Horace  L.  Wilgus,  “Arrest  Without  a  Warrant,"  22  Mich.L.
Current  "misdemeanors"  or
Rev.  541,  572-77  (1923-24).
minor  offenses  which  have  actually  led  to  custodial  arrests,
like  eating  on  the  subway,  failure  to  have  a  bell  or  gong  on
one’s  bicycle,  littering,  and  "walking  as  to  create  a  hazard,"
radically  expand  the  arrest  authority  granted  at  common  law.
It carries  with  it not
only  the  automatic  right  to  search  an  arrestee  and  his  or  her
vehicle  incident  to  the  arrest,  but  also  the  right  to  use  some
degree  of  necessary  force  to  effect  the  arrest,  to  transport
the  person  to  the  police  station,  to  handcuff,  book  and  fin-
gerprint  the  person,  to  detain  the  person  for  a  period  of time
(presumably  up  to  48  hours)  before  presentation  to  a  magis-
trate,  and  to  maintain  a  record  of the  arrest.

Custodial  arrest  is highly  intrusive.

On  the  other  side  of the  balance,  the  state  ordinarily  has
no  real  need  to  take  an  individual  into  custody  for  a  fine-
In  a  case  like  Atwater’s,  there  was  no  breach
only  offense.
of the  peace,  no  reason  to  fear  that  Atwater  would  abscond,
and  no  need  whatever  to  arrest  rather  than  issue  a  summons.
The  legislature's  choice  not  to  impose  any  jail  time  for
a  code  violation  is the  best  indication  of the  state’s  level  of
interest  in  taking  custody  of  individuals  who  violate  such
Welsh  v.  Wisconsin,  466  U.S.  740  (1984).  In
prohibitions.
other  areas  of constitutional  interpretation,  the  Court  has  de-
ferred  to  the  legislature’s  classification  of  offenses  as  fine-
only  or  not,  predicating  the  existence  of rights  like  the  right
to  counsel  or  right  to jury  trial  on  whether  or  not  an  offense
leads  to  incarceration.  See  Argersinger  v.  Hamlin,  407  US.
Pro-
25  (1972);  Baldwin  v.  New  York,  399  U.S.  66  (1970).
hibiting  custodial  arrest  for  fine-only  offenses  in the  absence
of exigent  circumstances  would  provide  a  clear  and  conven-

4

5

ient  bright-line  rule  for  courts  and  police  to  follow,  re-
specting  a  line  created  by  the  legislature  rather  than  sub-
jecting  every  arrest  to  after-the-fact  scrutiny  by the  courts.

Arrest  may  not  be  used  as  a  summary  punishment,  im-

posing  jail  time  that  no  legislature  has  authorized.

ARGUMENT

CUSTODIAL  ARREST  FOR  A  FINE-ONLY
OFFENSE,  IN  THE  ABSENCE  OF  EXIGENT
CIRCUMSTANCES,  IS  AN  UNREASONABLE
SEIZURE  WITHIN  THE  MEANING  OF  THE
FOURTH  AMENDMENT

A.  The  History  And  Core  Principles  Of
The  Fourth  Amendment  Support  Limi-
tations  On  Custodial  Arrest  For  Minor
Offenses

Texas  law  delegates  to  every  peace  officer  the  power  to
arrest,  and  therefore  to  search  incident  to  that  arrest,  see
United  States  v.  Robinson,  414  U.S.  218,  221-22  (1973),
any  person  who  has  committed  any  infraction  of  its  traffic
code,  Tex.  Transp.  Code  Ann.  §543.001  (West  1999),  or
any  one  of a  broad  array  of other  offenses.  See  Tex.  Crim.
P.  Code  Ann.  §§14.01(a)-(b),  14.03  (2)-(4)  (West  1998).
No  arrest  warrant  is required;  no  guidelines  are  provided.

This  extravagant  grant  of  discretionary  authority  is
reminiscent  of  the  general  warrants  the  framers  of  the
Fourth  Amendment  found  so  offensive.
In  fact,  it  was
exactly  such  excessive  delegations  of  the  power  to  search
and  seize  (like  the  statutes  authorizing  writs  of  assistance)
that  the  Fourth  Amendment  was  intended  to  prevent.
See
Jacob  Landynski,  SEARCH  AND  SEIZURE  AND  THE  SUPREME
COURT  30-42  (1966).  It  is by now  familiar  history  that  the
framers’  dismay  at  statutes  granting  such  general  preroga-

6

tives  was  one  of  the  principal  motivating  factors,  both  for
the  Revolution  and  the  creation  of  the  Fourth  Amendment
itself.  See  Lasson,  supra,  at  13-78  (events  during  the  thirty
years  preceding  the  drafting  of  the  Fourth  Amendment  in-
spired  the  framers  to  elevate  the  principle  of  "reasonable-
The  decisions
ness"  to  one  of  constitutional  significance).
of this  Court  consistently  identify  excessive  police  discretion
as  the  central  concern  of the  Fourth  Amendment.  See  Pay-
ton  v.  New  York,  445  U.S.  573,  583-85  (1980).  "The  essen-
tial  purpose  of the  proscriptions  in the  Fourth  Amendment  is
to  impose  a  standard  of  ‘reasonableness’  upon  the  exercise
of discretion  by  government  officials,  in  order  to  safeguard
the  privacy  and  security  of  individuals  against  arbitrary  in-
Delaware  v.  Prouse,  440  U.S.  648,  653-54
vasions.".
See  also  Almeida-Sanchez  v.  United  States,  413
(1979).
U.S.  266,  270  (1973):  United  States  v.  Martinez-Fuerte,  428
U.S.  543,  559  (1976);  Camara  vy.  Municipal  Court,  387  U.S.
527,  532-33  (1967).

Recent  history  underscores  the  need  to  respect  the
Fourth  Amendment’s  traditional  concerns  by  imposing  ap-
In  Whren  vy.
propriate  limitations  on  the  discretion  to  arrest.
United  States,  517  U.S.  806,  the  Court  declined  to  examine
whether  stops  for  traffic  offenses  were  in  fact  pretexts  to
investigate  suspected  drug  offenders,  believing  that  it would
be  inappropriate  as  part  of  Fourth  Amendment  analysis  to
If
explore  the  subjective  motivation  of  individual  officers.
the  Fourth  Amendment  is  also  construed  as  not  imposing
any  objective  limitations  on  the  arrest  power,  other  than
probable  cause,  any  state  or  municipal  legislature  may  au-
thorize  custodial  arrest  for  any  “offense”  at  all,  no  matter
how  trivial,  and  the  potential  for  abuse  of  discretion  and
selective  enforcement  is magnified  exponentially.

This  is  more  than  a  hypothetical  concern.

Infractions
today’s  legislatures  have  already  constituted  as  arrestable
offenses,  and  which  have  actually  led  to  full  custodial  ar-

rests,  include,  in  addition  to  the  Texas  seat  belt  offense,
eating  on  the  subway,  see  Fisher  v.  Washington  Met.  Area
Transit  Auth.,  690  F.2d  1133  (4th  Cir.  1982);  failure  to  have
a  bell  or  gong  on  one’s  bicycle,  see  Thomas  v.  Florida,  614
So.2d  468  (Fla.  1993);  failure  to  obtain  a  license  for  a
telemarketing  business,  see  Ricci  v.
Village  of Arlington
Heights,  116  F.3d  288  (7th  Cir.  1997),  cert.  dism'd  as  im-
providently  granted,  523  U.S.  613  (1998);  littering,  see
United  States  v.  Herring,  35  F.Supp.2d  1253  (D.  Or.  1999);
and  "walking  as  to  create  a  hazard,"  see  Barnett  v.  United
States,  525  A.2d  197,  198  (D.C.Ct.App.  1987).

For  some  overzealous  officers,  the  temptation  to  use
this  blanket  authority  to  trawl  for  drug  offenders  could  be
overwhelming.
Officers  in  permissive  jurisdictions  could
target  anyone  at  all,  watch  their  target  until  she  or  he
committed  some  infraction,  even  if  only  littering  or  jay-
walking,  and  then  claim  the  right  to  perform  a search  inci-
dent  to  a  strategic  arrest  of  the  individual,  and  in  cases  of
traffic  offenses,  search  the  individual’s  vehicle  as  well.
If
the  opportunity  to  declare  virtually  anyone  to  be  under  arrest
is too  readily  available,  the  search  incident  to  arrest  doctrine
is  effectively  turned  on  its  head,  with  arrests  becoming
incident  to  the  real  goal  of suspicionless  searches.

The  facts  of  Thomas  v.  Florida,  614  So.2d  468,  are  in-
structive  and  alarming.  Carl  Thomas  was  riding  his  bicycle
through  a  predominantly  black  neighborhood  in  Orlando
known  for  drug  activity.  A  local  officer,  apparently  having
no  objective  reason  to  stop  Thomas  on  suspicion  of  any
other  criminal  activity,  arrested  him  for  the  offense  of riding
a  bicycle  not  equipped  with  a  bell  or  gong  (under  a  city  or-
dinance  whose  dubious  validity  under  Florida  jaw  had  not
yet  been  litigated,  apparently  because  it  was  so  rarely
enforced).
Conducting  a  search  incident  to  this  ingenious
arrest,  the  officer  found  a  handgun  in  Thomas's  pocket.  /d.
at  469.
Whren  instructs  us  not  to  inquire  into  the  officer's

for  suddenly  deciding  to  enforce  this  vestigial

reason
ordinance.

While  Atwater’s  case  proves  the  conventional  Fourth
Amendment  wisdom  that  excessive  delegations  of arrest  au-
thority  can  lead  to  arbitrary  enforcement,  or  enforcement  out
of  personal  vendetta.’  Thomas’s  case  raises  the  specter  of
the  rampant  discriminatory  enforcement  that  can  take  place
in  the  absence  of  objective  limits  on  legislative  grants  of
arrest  power,  with  its  concomitant  search  power.  Sadly,  the
fear  that  excessive  grants  of discretion  will  lead  to  racially
discriminatory  law  enforcement  is  more  than  hypothetical.
See  Illinois  v.  Wardlow,  120  S.Ct.  at  682  nn.9-10  (Stevens,

For  an  explanation  of  why  equal  protection
tion  Clause.
claims  are  not  an  adequate  substitute  for  control  over  excessive  police

rest.  Now  that  the  Court  has  invalidated  that  effort  to  sever

State  or  local  jurisdictions  will
rather  than  traffic  citations.
be  encouraged  to  emulate  Texas  by  permitting  arrest  for

The  searches  and  arrests  in  such  cases  must  be  deemed
unreasonable  within  the  meaning  of the  Fourth  Amendment,
both  because  of  the  excessive,  unreasonable  delegation  of
discretion,  and  because,  on  balance,  the  state's  interest  in
enforcing  minor  offenses  does  not  reasonably  outweigh  the
individual’s  interest  in  liberty,  privacy,  and  dignity.

When  Gail  Atwater  was  transported  to  the  police  sta-
tion,  she  was  subjected  to  a  full  custodial  arrest.  See  Duna-
way  v.  New  York,  442  U.S.  200,  213-14  (1979).
Because
this  Court  has  always  recognized  that  custodial  arrest  is  the
gravest  intrusion  on  an  individual’s  liberty  and  privacy,  it
has  strictly  observed  the  requirement  that  any  arrest  must  be
The  Court  has  also
based  on  probable  cause.

warrants  as  a  precondition  to  a  valid  arrest,  see  Payton  v.
New  York,  445  U.S.  at  585  ("a  warrantless  arrest  of a  person
is  a  species  of  seizure  required  by  the  Amendment  to  be
reasonable");  Welsh  v.  Wisconsin,  466  U.S.  740  (warrantless

204  (1981)(search  warrant  required  for  arrest  in  another’s
home),  and  sometimes  imposing  limitations  on  the  method
of arrest,  see  Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  1 (limitation  on
use  of deadly  force  in  arrest);  Wilson  v.  Arkansas,  514  U.S.
927  (1995)(knock-and-announce  limitation  on  execution  of
warrants);  Richards  v.
Wisconsin,  520  U.S.  385  (1997)
(same).

As  the  Court  has  explained  on  many  occasions,  whether
Or  not  an  arrest  is  reasonable  must  be  determined  by  “bal-
anc[ing]  the  nature  and  quality  of  the  intrusion  on  the
individual's  Fourth  Amendment  interests  against  the  impor-
tance  of  the  governmental  interests  alleged  to  justify  the
Tennessee  v.  Garner,  471  U.S.  at  8.  See  also
intrusion."
United  States  v.  Place,  462  U.S.  696,  703  (1983);  Winston
v.  Lee,  470  U.S.  753,  760  (1982);  United  States  v.  Hensley,
469  U.S.  221,  228  (1985):  Delaware  v.  Prouse,  440  U.S.  at
654;.  United  States  v.  Martinez-Fuerte,  428  U.S.  at  556-62.

While  the  Court  has  not  yet  addressed  the  question  of
what  limitations  the  Fourth  Amendment  imposes  on  the
power  to  arrest  for  minor  offenses,  a  number  of  Justices
have  expressed  concern  about  the  need  to  limit  the  power  to
See  Gustafson  v.  Florida,
arrest  under  such  circumstances.
414  U.S.  260,  266-67  (1973)(Stewart,  J., concurring)("a  per-
suasive  claim  might  have  been  made  ..
.  that  the  custodial
arrest  of  the  petitioner  for  a  minor  traffic  offense  violated
his  rights  under  the  Fourth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments");
Robbins  v.  California,  453  U.S.  420,  450  n.11  (1981)
(Stevens,  J.,  dissenting);  cf.  Maryland  v.  Macon,  472  U.S.
463,  471  (1985)("[w]e  leave  to  another  day  the  question
whether  the  Fourth  Amendment  prohibits  a  warrantless  ar-
rest  for  the  state  law  misdemeanor  of distribution  of obscene
materials" )(opinion  of the  Court  by O’Connor,  J.);  Welsh  v.
Wisconsin,  466  U.S.  at  749  n.11  (leaving  open  the  question
of  whether  warrantless  arrest  in  the  home  for  a  nonjailable

offense  is  ever  reasonable,  even  if  exigent  circumstances
exist)(opinion  of  the  Court  by  Brennan,  J.).  Three  Terms
ago,  the  Court  granted  certiorari  in  Ricci  v.  Village  of Ar-
lington  Heights,  116  F.3d  288,  to  consider  the  question  of
whether  petitioner's  arrest
for  violation  of  a  fine-only
municipal  ordinance  by  operating  a  telemarketing  business
without  a  license  was  an  unreasonable  seizure,  but  subse-
quently  dismissed  certiorari  as  improvidently  granted,  523
U.S.  613  (1998)(No.  97-501).°

.

The  en  banc  majority  below  took  as  dispositive  of  the
certiorari  question  in  this  case,  and  in  Ricci,  dicta  from  the
Court’s  decision  in  Whren  v.  United  States,  517  U.S.  at  817,
commenting  that  if  an  arrest  is  based  on  probable  cause,
.  the  result  of  that  balancing  is not
"with  rare  exceptions  .
in  doubt."  See  195  F.3d  at  243.  While  it is understandable
that  lower  federal  and  state  courts  have  been  reluctant  to
venture  into  territory  not  previously  charted  by  this  Court,
the  dicta  in  Whren  cannot  be  read  as  obviating  the  need  to
balance  the  state  and  individual  interests  in  this  case.
First,
what  Whren  found  to  be  constitutionally  reasonable  was
only  a  stop  based  on  probable  cause  that  a traffic  offense
was  being  committed,  not  an  arrest.  Whren  was  not  placed
under  arrest  until  the  officers  observed  illegal  drugs  in  plain

°  The  Court  has  left  this  question  open  for  so  long  that  some  observers
have  assumed  that  the  Court’s  inaction  is  an  implicit  rejection  of  the
argument  that  the  Fourth  Amendment  imposes  limitations  on  the  arrest
power  beyond  probable  cause.
See,  e.g.,  Wayne  R.  LaFave,  SEARCH
AND  SEIZURE  §5.1(c)  at  23  (3d  ed.  1996);  Higbee  v.  City  of San  Diego,
911  F.2d  377,  379  n.2  (9th  Cir.  1990).
Even  if they  do  not  simply
assume  the  Fourth  Amendment  to  be  so  limited,  some  state  and  lower
federal  courts  have  announced  that  they  prefer  to  await  the  Supreme
Court’s  lead  rather  than  engaging  in  independent  analysis  of  what
common  law  requirements  the  Fourth  Amendment  incorporates  with  re-
See,  eg.  Fisher,  690  F.3d  at  1139  n.6;  City  of
spect  to  arrest.
Milwaukee  v.  Nelson,  439  N.W.2d  562,  570  (Wis.  1989).

12

13

view  during  the  traffic  stop.  517  U.S.  at  809.  Second,  al-
though  the  Court  remarked  that  the  balancing  analysis  has
been  reserved  for  "extraordinary"  or  "extreme  intrusions,  id.
at  818,  the  Court  went  on  to  list  cases  in which  various  lim-
itations,  usually  gleaned  from  common  law,  were  added  to
what  had  previously  been  assumed  by the  Court  to  be  rea-
sonable  arrests.’  Overreading  the  Whren  dicta  simply  begs
the  question  posed  herein  by  assuming  the  result  of  a  bal-
ancing  not  yet  conducted.

The  offenses  for  which  custodial  arrest  was  permitted  at
common  law  --  typically  serious  offenses  involving  breach
of the  peace,  see  infra,  at  pp.19-20  --  could  indeed  be  pre-
sumed,  on  balance,  to  be  reasonable  occasions  for  custodial
arrests.
The  state's  interest  in  restoring  the  peace  after  a
disruptive  infraction  could  reasonably  be viewed  as  presump-
tively  outweighing  the  individual’s  interest  in  remaining
free.  Whether  the  state  is entitled  to  the  same  presumption
where  an  arrest  is for  failing  to  wear  a  seat  belt  or  operating
a  business  without  a  license  is  a  question  that  has  never
been  considered  by  this  Court.
It  is  because  the  world  of
regulatory  offenses  has  proliferated  in  a manner  the  common
law  could  not  have  imagined  that  it  has  become  necessary
for  the  courts  to  decide  whether  arrests  for  petty  offenses
are  reasonable,  instead  of assuming  that  they  are  simply  be-
cause  a  legislature  or  city  council  has  granted  the  preroga-
tive  to  arrest.
Legislatures  are  not  the  final  arbiter  of what
constitutes  a  reasonable  arrest  practice.
See  Knowles  v.
lowa,  525  U.S.  113.

”  Before  Payton  v.  New  York  was  decided,  for  example,  the  Court  de-
clared  that  it  had  never  “invalidated  an  arrest  based  on  probable  cause
because  the  officers  failed  to  secure  a  warrant,”  see,  e.g.,  Gerstein  v
Pugh,  395  U.S.  103,  113  (1975),  and  that  probable  cause  was  thus  the
only  requirement  for  a  valid  arrest.

C.  The  Intrusiveness  Of  A  Custodial  Arrest
Outweighs  The  State’s  Interests  In  Con-
ducting  Such  Arrests  For  Fine-Only
Offenses

Every  arrest  is a  serious  intrusion  on  individual  liberty,
privacy,  and  dignity.  Incident  to  any  lawful  arrest,  as  de-
scribed  above,  police  may,  with  no  additional  showing,  con-
duct  a  full  search  of the  individual  being  arrested,  including
containers  on  their  person,  United  States  v.  Robinson,  414
U.S.  at  221-22,  even  for  minor  offenses  and  where  there  is
no  evidentiary  purpose,  Gustafson  v.  Florida,  414  U.S.  260
(driving  without  a  license),  and  may  search  the  passenger
compartment,  including  containers,  of  an  arrestee’s  vehicle,
see  New  York  v.  Belton,  453  U.S.  454  (1981).

An  arrestee,  like  Gail  Atwater,  may  be  publicly  humili-
ated  by  being  led  away  by the  police,  in  handcuffs,  in  front
of  neighbors  or  strangers,  and  then  frightened  by  being
transported  to  the  station  house,  where  he  or  she  may  be
booked  and  fingerprinted,  and  then  detained  without  seeing
a  magistrate  for  up  to  48  hours,  and  possibly  even  longer.
See  County  of Riverside  v.  McLaughlin,  500  U.S.  44  (1991).
Petitioner  Atwater  was  held  for  only  one  hour;  others  less
fortunate  could  be  held  for  a  substantially  longer  period  of
time,  particularly  if they  are  unable  to  secure  bail  or  post  a
demanded  bond.*  Like  Atwater,  an  arrestee  may  also  have
dependent  children  who  might  be  forced  to  share  their  par-
ent’s  humiliating  and  traumatic  experience,  or  left  without
adequate  care.”

*  This  practice  would  have  severe  and  potentially  unconstitutional
consequences  for  poor  people.  See  Tate  v.  Short,  401  U.S.  395  (1971).

*  There  is  little  question  in this  case  as  to  whether  probable  cause  for  the
arrest  actually  existed  --  Atwater  admitted  that  she  and  her  children  were
Permitting
not  wearing  seatbelts,  as  the  officer  had  personally  observed.
(continued...)

14

15

In  addition  to  the  serious  intrusion  on  the  arrestee’s
liberty  and  dignity,  an  arrest  can  continue  to  compromise
privacy  interests.  Even  in  cases  resulting  in  acquittal  or
dismissal,  arrest  records  may  be  maintained  and  dissemi-
nated,  further  damaging  the  individual’s  reputation.
See
Paul  v.  Davis,  424  U.S.  693  (1976);  Donald  L.  Doernberg
&  Donald  H.  Zeigler,  "Due  Process,  Versus  Data  Proc-
essing:
An  Analysis  of  Computerized  Criminal  History
Information  Systems,"  55  N.Y.U.L.Rev.  110,  114  (1980).

No  legitimate  interest  of the  state  or  city,  on  the  other
hand,  was  served  by  Atwater’s  arrest  that  would  not  have
been  as  well  served  by  issuance  of a citation.
Atwater  did
not  create  or  threaten  to  create  a  breach  of the  peace.
She
did  not  refuse  to  identify  herself  or  attempt  to  evade  Officer
Turek.
She  did  not  refuse  to  fasten  her  own  and  her  chil-
dren's  seat  belts  when  apprehended  by  Officer  Turek;  the
officer  did  not  request  that  she  do  so  because  he  decided  so
precipitously  to  place  her  under  arrest.
The  state’s  interest
in  assuring  a  defendant's  presence  for  further  proceedings
can  be  satisfied  in  fine-only  cases  by issuing  a  summons  for
a  code  violation."
The  less  serious  the  offense,  the  less
likely  a  defendant  is  to  abscond.
See  Welsh,  466  U.S.  at
759  (White,  J.,  dissenting).
In  light  of  Atwater’s  unim-
peachable  roots  in  her  community  and  the  nature  of her  of-
fense,  it can  scarcely  be argued  that  the  state  had  an  interest
in  preventing  her  from  absconding  in  order  to  avoid  a  $50

custodial  arrest  to  be  authorized  for  a  vast  array  of  minor  infractions,
however,  would  also  allow  arrests  and  two-day  detentions  even  in  cases
where  the  existence  of probable  cause  is highly  questionable.

fine.  There  was  simply  no  legitimate  need  for  the  arrest.

Respondents  cannot  argue  that  the  purpose  of this  arrest
was  to  attempt  to  deter  Atwater  from  committing  future  vio-

cisions  about  what  is  an  appropriate  punishment  must  be
made  by a  representative,  politically  accountable  body.  C7.

tive  prerogative  to  decide  punishment  levels).
the  legislature  decided  not  to  make  any  form  of  incarcera-

sion  below,  remarked  that  Officer  Turek  had  acted  as  "a

195
F.3d  at  250.  He  could  aptly  have  added  "cum  legislature."

An  exigent  circumstances  exception  to  the  rule  that  cus-
todial  arrest  is not  generally  permitted  for  fine-only  offenses
would  be  more  than  sufficient  to  deal  with  those  cases
where  a  state  does  have  a  legitimate  reason  to  arrest  an  indi-
vidual  for  such  an  offense,  perhaps  because  the  individual  is
threatening  a  breach  of  the  peace,  or  refuses  to  identify
herself.''
See  Thomas  R.  Folk,  "The  Case  for  Constitu-
tional  Constraints  Upon  the  Power  to  Make  Full  Custody
Arrests,"  48  U.Cin.L.Rev.  321  (1979)(arguing  that  custodial
arrest  for  minor  offenses  should  be  confined  to  limited  situa-
tions  like  these).  What  exigent  circumstances  would  justify  a
custodial  arrest  for  a  fine-only  offense  is not  a  question  that
arises  in  this  case,  however,  because  there  was  no  claim  of
exigency  with  respect  to  petitioner  Atwater’s  arrest  and  no
basis  for  any  such  claim.

D.  Common  Law  Tradition  Supports  Pro-
hibition  Of  Custodial  Arrest  For  Fine-
Only  Offenses

This  Court  has  often  said  that  the  "Fourth  Amendment
is to  be  construed  in the  light  of what  was  deemed  an  unrea-
sonable  search  and  seizure  when  it  was  adopted,  and  in  a
manner  which  will  conserve  public  interests  as  well  as  the
interests  and  rights  of individual  citizens."  Carroll  v.  United

Wyoming  v.  Houghton,
States,  267  U.S.  132,  149  (1925).
526  U.S.  295,  299  (1999).
This  is  not  a  case  where  logic
must  defer  to  history,  see  United  States  v.  Watson,  423  U.S.
411,  429  (1976)(Powell,  J.,  concurring),  for  logic  is  sup-
ported  by history.

At  common  law,  a  summons  procedure  rather  than  cus-
todial  arrest  was  customarily  used  for  minor  offenses  not
constituting  a  breach  of the  peace.  See  Julius  Goebel,  Jr.  &
T.  Raymond  Naughton,  LAW  ENFORCEMENT  IN  COLONIAL
New  York  417  (1944)(Patterson  Smith  Reprint  Series,
1970);  Salken,  supra,  at  258-59  and  authorities  cited  therein.
Warrantless  custodial  arrest'?  was  authorized  for  "“misde-
meanors"  only  if they  constituted  a  breach  of the  peace  and
occurred  in  the  officer’s  presence.
See  Stephen,  supra,  at
193  ("The  common  law  did  not  authorise  the  arrest  of  per-
sons  guilty  or  suspected  of  misdemeanours,  except  in  cases
of  an  actual  breach  of  the  peace  either  by  an  affray  or  by
violence  to  an  individual");  1  Joseph  Chitty,  A PRACTICAL
TREATISE  ON  THE  CRIMINAL  LAW  15  (1816)(Garland  Pub-
lishing,  Inc.  1978)("no  person  can,  in  general,  be  taken  into
custody  without  warrant,  for  a  mere  misdemeanour  unat-
tended  with  violence,  as  perjury  or  libel");  4 William  Black-
stone,  COMMENTARIES  *289;  Matthew  Hale,  PLEAS  OF  THE
CROWN:  A  METHODICAL  SUMMARY  92  (1678)(P.  R.  Glaze-
brook  ed.,  Professional  Books  Ltd.  1972)("in  case  of  any
other  breach  of  the  peace,  the  Constable  may  imprison  the
party  in the  Stocks,  in the  Goal  [sic],  or  in his  House,  till  he
can  bring  him  before  a  Justice  of  the  Peace");  Edward  C.

‘'  In  many  cases  where  an  individual's  conduct  threatens  a  breach  of the
peace  or  defies  an  officer's  authority,  that  individual  might  be  subject  to
arrest  for  a  separate  offense  like  disorderly  conduct,  obstruction  of jus-
tice,  or  refusal  to  identify  oneself,  depending  on  the  legislature's  treat-
ment  of those  offenses.

only  misdemeanor,  or  for  an  offense  not  constituting  a  breach  of  the
peace.  The  en  banc  court  below,  in  fact,  found  that  Atwater  had  proce-

As  one  recent  history  concluded,  the
framers  of  the  Fourth  Amendment  would  have  perceived
peace  officers  as  possessing  little  "significant  ex  officio
discretionary  arrest  or  search  authority."  Thomas  Y.  Davies,
